text: A customer support agent built with the Claude Agent SDK occasionally skips an identity verification step before processing refund requests, leading to unauthorized refunds in ~8% of interactions. The team has already added system-prompt instructions emphasizing verification. What is the most effective way to ensure the verification step always occurs before refund processing?

options.text: Implement a PreToolUse hook that blocks the process_refund tool until get_customer has returned a verified customer ID
Add more emphatic language to the system prompt requiring verification before any financial operation
Create a separate verification subagent that runs independently before the main support agent
Configure tool_choice to force the get_customer tool on every interaction

optionExplanations.A: Correct. A PreToolUse hook is deterministic code that runs before process_refund executes, so it can block the call until it confirms get_customer has returned a verified customer ID, guaranteeing the verification step always happens.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. More emphatic system-prompt language is still probabilistic guidance with a non-zero failure rate, which is unacceptable for gating a financial action like a refund.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A separate verification subagent adds architectural complexity but doesn't guarantee that verification actually runs before the refund tool is called.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Forcing get_customer on every interaction breaks normal conversation flow and still doesn't specifically gate the process_refund tool call.

text: A multi-agent research system has a coordinator delegating to three subagents: web_search, document_analysis, and synthesis. The synthesis subagent produces reports lacking the web_search agent's findings, even though the coordinator receives all subagents' results correctly. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The coordinator is not passing the complete web_search findings into the synthesis subagent's prompt context
The synthesis subagent's system prompt needs more explicit instructions about including all sources
The web_search subagent returns results in a format incompatible with the synthesis subagent
The synthesis subagent needs the web_search tool to verify findings independently

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Subagents run in isolated contexts and don't automatically inherit other subagents' outputs; if the coordinator fails to explicitly include the web_search findings in the synthesis subagent's prompt, that data is simply unavailable to it.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Better instructions can't surface data the synthesis subagent never received in its context in the first place.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A format incompatibility is unlikely since the coordinator itself parsed the web_search results correctly.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Giving the synthesis subagent its own web_search tool violates least privilege and causes redundant work instead of fixing the context-passing gap.

text: An architect is designing an agentic loop with the Claude API that must handle tool calls, detect completion, and manage errors. Which combination of stop_reason values correctly identifies when to continue the loop versus exit?

options.text: Continue on 'tool_use' or 'end_turn'; exit on 'max_tokens' or 'refusal'
Continue on 'tool_use' or 'max_tokens'; exit on 'end_turn' or 'stop_sequence'
Continue on 'tool_use'; exit only on 'end_turn'
Continue on 'tool_use'; exit on 'end_turn', 'max_tokens', 'refusal', or 'stop_sequence'

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Treating 'end_turn' as a continue condition is wrong since 'end_turn' signals the response is complete, and it omits 'stop_sequence' from the exit set.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. 'max_tokens' indicates truncation, not a request to run a tool, so continuing the loop on it is wrong; it also misplaces 'end_turn' and 'stop_sequence'.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Exiting only on 'end_turn' ignores other terminal conditions like 'max_tokens', 'refusal', and 'stop_sequence' that also require the loop to stop and be handled.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. The loop should continue only on 'tool_use' and exit on every other terminal stop_reason: 'end_turn' (done), 'max_tokens' (truncated), 'refusal' (declined), and 'stop_sequence' (hit a custom stop).

text: A code-review system generates code and then validates it. When the same Claude session both generates code and reviews it, the reviews are less critical than when a separate session does the review. What architectural change most effectively addresses this bias?

options.text: Add stronger instructions in the review prompt to be critical and thorough
Use a separate Claude instance without the generation context to perform the review
Implement a multi-pass review within the same session with explicit self-critique prompts
Increase the temperature parameter during the review phase to encourage diverse perspectives

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Stronger review instructions don't remove the underlying bias caused by the reviewer sharing context with the generator.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. An independent Claude instance with a clean context, free of the generation reasoning, produces a more objective review since it can't rationalize work it didn't produce.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Multi-pass self-critique within the same session still carries the contaminating generation context that causes the bias.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Temperature affects randomness, not objectivity, and it isn't even an available knob on current Claude models.

text: A coordinator manages three research subagents investigating 'AI applications in creative industries.' All three covered visual arts extensively but missed music, creative writing, and film entirely. Each subagent executed its assigned task correctly and thoroughly. What is the root cause of this gap?

options.text: The subagents need larger context windows to cover more topics
The subagents should have web search tools to discover additional creative domains
The synthesis step should have identified and requested additional research on missing topics
The coordinator's task decomposition was too narrow, limiting scope to visual arts subtopics

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Larger context windows don't expand the scope each subagent was assigned to research.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Web search tools don't fix a scoping problem; the subagents already executed their assigned tasks correctly.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A synthesis-step fix only patches the symptom after the underlying research has already been too narrowly scoped.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. The coordinator's task decomposition limited all three subagents to visual-arts subtopics instead of distributing coverage across the full creative-industries space, so the gap originated upstream at decomposition.

text: An architect needs two analysis paths that explore different hypotheses from the same shared baseline data. The paths must be independent but start from identical context. Which capability best supports this?

options.text: Create two separate sessions and manually copy the baseline data into each
Use session forking to create independent branches from the shared baseline
Use agent teams with a shared task list to coordinate the investigation
Run both analyses sequentially in the same session with context-separation prompts

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Manually copying baseline data into two separate sessions is error-prone and risks drift between the two paths.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. Session forking creates independent branches that start from identical accumulated context and then diverge, matching the requirement of a shared baseline with independent exploration.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Agent teams are designed for collaborative, communicating work, not for isolated, divergent exploration from a shared starting point.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Running both analyses sequentially in the same session loses parallelism and risks cross-contamination between the two analyses.

text: A support agent must handle multi-concern messages (e.g., a billing dispute AND a feature question in one message). Currently it addresses only the first concern. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Instruct the agent to process both concerns in sequence within a single response
Route the message to two different specialized agents simultaneously
Decompose the multi-concern request into distinct items and investigate each in parallel
Ask the customer to submit separate tickets for each concern

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Sequential handling within a single response is still prone to the same attention dilution that causes later concerns to be dropped.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Blind dual-routing skips the decomposition step needed to first identify all the concerns present in the message.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. Explicitly decomposing the multi-concern message into distinct items and investigating each (in parallel) ensures full coverage and avoids attention dilution.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Pushing the work back to the customer to split tickets is a poor experience and doesn't solve the underlying technical gap.

text: Using Claude Agent SDK hooks, a developer wants to enforce that refunds over $500 require manager approval. Which hook configuration correctly implements this?

options.text: A PostToolUse hook on process_refund that reverses transactions over $500
A SessionStart hook that sets a global $500 limit for all financial operations
A system-prompt instruction specifying the $500 threshold with examples
A PreToolUse hook on process_refund that denies the call when the amount exceeds $500

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. A PostToolUse hook only fires after the refund has already executed, so it can't prevent the unauthorized transaction.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. A SessionStart hook runs once at session start and can't evaluate the amount argument of each individual refund call.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A system-prompt instruction is probabilistic guidance and is unsuitable for enforcing a hard financial control.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. A PreToolUse hook on process_refund intercepts the call before it runs, inspects the amount, and denies it when it exceeds $500, stopping the refund before any money moves.

text: A team resumes a Claude Code session after making significant code changes outside the session. The agent gives recommendations based on the previous, now-stale code state. What is the most reliable approach for accurate responses?

options.text: Use --resume with the session ID and immediately ask Claude to re-read all changed files
Fork the previous session and provide a diff of all changes since the last interaction
Use --continue to load the most recent session with updated file context
Start a fresh session with a structured summary of prior findings and the current code state

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Resuming the prior session reloads a reasoning chain anchored to the old, now-stale code state even if files are re-read afterward.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Forking the previous session with a diff still carries the stale baseline context forward into the new branch.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Continuing the most recent session still inherits prior reasoning based on outdated file contents.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. Starting a fresh session with a concise summary of prior findings plus the current code state gives Claude a clean, accurate basis instead of one anchored to stale tool results.

text: A support agent handles tickets ranging from simple password resets (70% of volume) to complex multi-system outage investigations (5%). Should it use adaptive decomposition or a fixed sequential pipeline?

options.text: Fixed sequential pipeline — apply the same thorough process to every ticket for consistency
A single adaptive agent with a mandatory initial classification step before any processing
Two separate agents: one fixed pipeline for simple tickets, one adaptive for complex tickets
Adaptive decomposition — generate subtasks dynamically based on what's discovered at each step

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Applying the same thorough process to every ticket wastes effort on the 70% of tickets that are simple password resets.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. While closer to adaptive, a mandatory initial classification gate is unnecessarily rigid compared to letting depth scale dynamically without a hard gate.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Splitting into two separate agents adds operational overhead and requires an extra routing decision that isn't needed.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. Adaptive decomposition lets simple tickets resolve in one or two steps while complex ones dynamically generate deeper investigation, matching effort to the task.

text: An MCP server exposes 18 tools to a support agent. The agent frequently confuses get_order_status with get_order_details and check_shipping with track_package. What is the most effective solution?

options.text: Add retry logic that detects incorrect tool selection and re-routes to the correct tool
Use tool_choice to force specific tools based on keywords in the customer message
Consolidate overlapping tools and expand descriptions with clear differentiators
Provide few-shot examples of correct tool selection for each disambiguation case

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Retry logic that detects and re-routes incorrect tool selection treats the symptom rather than the root cause of confusing tool descriptions.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Forcing tool_choice based on keyword matching bypasses Claude's reasoning entirely and is brittle to phrasing variations.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. Consolidating overlapping tools and writing descriptions with clear differentiators fixes the root cause, since Claude selects tools primarily from their descriptions.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Few-shot examples for each disambiguation case can help but don't fix the structural problem of having 18 tools with thin, overlapping descriptions.

text: An MCP tool for customer returns accepts a JSON object with nested fields (order_id, return_reason, item_conditions, refund_method). Agents frequently malform the nested item_conditions array. Which tool-definition improvement most reduces input errors?

options.text: Add validation inside the tool that rejects malformed inputs with helpful error messages
Include input examples in the tool definition showing correctly structured nested objects
Simplify the tool to accept only flat string parameters instead of nested objects
Split the tool into separate tools for each parameter to eliminate nesting

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. In-tool validation only catches malformed input after the error has already been made, rather than preventing it.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. Concrete input examples in the tool definition show the exact correctly-structured nested shape for item_conditions, which is more effective than just describing it.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Flattening the tool to only string parameters sacrifices the nested data model the tool is meant to capture.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Splitting into separate tools per parameter causes tool proliferation, which itself degrades tool selection.

text: A document-extraction pipeline must always call extract_metadata first, but subsequent steps should let Claude choose tools based on content. Which tool_choice sequence is correct?

options.text: Use {"type":"tool","name":"extract_metadata"} on the first request, then switch to "auto"
Use "any" on the first request to guarantee a tool call, then "auto"
Use "auto" throughout and instruct the model to always start with extract_metadata
Use "none" on the first request to let Claude plan, then "auto"

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Forcing the specific tool ({type:'tool', name:'extract_metadata'}) on the first request guarantees that exact tool runs first, and switching to 'auto' afterward lets Claude reason freely about subsequent tool calls.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. 'any' guarantees that some tool is called first, but not specifically extract_metadata.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. 'auto' plus a prompt instruction to start with extract_metadata is only probabilistic and doesn't guarantee the required ordering.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. 'none' forbids any tool call on the first turn, which is the opposite of requiring extract_metadata to run first.

text: A multi-agent system's web_search subagent times out against an external API. The coordinator receives a generic 'Operation failed' and terminates the whole workflow. How should error handling be improved?

options.text: The coordinator should catch all errors and silently proceed without the web results
The subagent should automatically retry three times before reporting failure
The subagent should return structured error context: failure type, attempted query, partial results, and alternative approaches
The system should fall back to cached results from previous queries

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Silently proceeding without the web results hides a real failure from the coordinator instead of surfacing it for an informed decision.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Automatic retries only help with transient errors and still give the coordinator no context to make a recovery decision.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. Structured error context (failure type, attempted query, partial results, alternative approaches) lets the coordinator decide to retry, delegate elsewhere, or proceed with partial results instead of aborting on an opaque failure.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Falling back to cached results risks serving stale or irrelevant data without signaling that a fallback occurred.

text: A team wants to share MCP server configurations across all developers while letting individuals also use personal experimental MCP servers. Where should each configuration go?

options.text: Team config in CLAUDE.md; personal config in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
Team config in .mcp.json at the project root; personal config in user-level settings
Team config in .claude/settings.json; personal config in environment variables
Both in .mcp.json with conditional loading based on the developer's username

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. CLAUDE.md is meant for instructions and memory, not for defining MCP server configurations.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. Project-scoped MCP servers belong in a version-controlled .mcp.json at the repo root so the whole team gets them, while personal/experimental servers belong in the developer's user-level config, which isn't committed.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Storing team config in .claude/settings.json and personal config in environment variables is a non-standard split for MCP server definitions.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Putting both configs in .mcp.json with username-conditional loading is brittle and not a standard mechanism.

text: A monorepo needs different coding conventions for React component files, API route files, and DB migration files, each scattered across many directories. What is the most maintainable way to scope conventions by file type?

options.text: Path-scoped rule files that match each file type by glob pattern, loaded only when editing matching files
Separate CLAUDE.md files in every directory that contains these file types
All conventions in the root CLAUDE.md under clear section headers for each file type
Custom slash commands for switching between convention modes

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Glob-scoped rule files load the right conventions only when a matching file is being edited, regardless of directory, which fits file types scattered across a monorepo while keeping irrelevant context out.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Separate CLAUDE.md files in every directory require duplication of conventions and still miss files scattered outside those directories.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A monolithic root CLAUDE.md loads every convention regardless of which file type is actually being edited.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Slash commands require the developer to manually switch convention modes rather than applying scoping automatically.

text: A team wants a reusable Claude Code skill for database migrations that runs in an isolated context (so verbose migration logs don't pollute the main conversation) and has access only to the Bash and Read tools. Which SKILL.md frontmatter achieves this?

options.text: An isolated/forked execution context, allowed-tools limited to [Bash, Read], and model-invocation disabled
An isolated/forked execution context with allowed-tools limited to [Bash, Read]
isolation: true, tools: [Bash, Read], disable-model-invocation: true
An isolated context with allowed-tools [Bash, Read, Write]

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Disabling model invocation would prevent the model from triggering the skill at all, which isn't part of the stated requirement.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. Running the skill in an isolated/forked execution context keeps its verbose migration logs out of the main conversation, and restricting allowed-tools to exactly [Bash, Read] enforces the requested tool scope.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. The field names used (isolation: true, tools, disable-model-invocation) aren't the actual SKILL.md frontmatter keys.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Including Write in allowed-tools grants access beyond what was requested (only Bash and Read).

text: An invoice-extraction system uses a JSON schema. Some invoices lack certain fields (e.g., purchase-order numbers). How should the schema handle potentially missing fields to prevent Claude from fabricating values?

options.text: Make all fields required and instruct Claude to use 'N/A' for missing values
Use enum fields with an 'unknown' option for fields that might be missing
Make potentially-absent fields nullable so Claude can return null instead of guessing
Add post-processing validation to detect and remove likely-fabricated values

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Forcing all fields to be required and using 'N/A' still pressures the model to emit a value and pollutes the field's type with non-data strings.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Enum fields are meant for fixed value sets, not for representing the presence or absence of a field.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. Making potentially-absent fields nullable gives the model a legitimate way to indicate the field is genuinely not present in the source document, returning null instead of guessing a plausible value.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Post-processing validation tries to detect fabricated values after they've already been generated rather than preventing fabrication in the first place.

text: A team uses the Message Batches API for overnight compliance reports (non-blocking) and wants to also use it for pre-merge code-review checks in CI to save cost. What is the correct recommendation?

options.text: Use batch processing for the overnight reports only; use real-time API calls for pre-merge checks
Use batch processing for both — the 50% savings justify the CI delay
Use real-time API for both — batch processing only helps above 100K requests
Use batch processing for pre-merge checks only since they benefit from structured output

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Batch processing's lack of a latency SLA (up to ~24 hours) is fine for overnight reports but unacceptable for a blocking pre-merge CI check, so reports should batch while pre-merge checks use real-time calls.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Batching pre-merge checks would stall the developer workflow with unpredictable delays just to save cost.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. There is no real volume floor like 100K requests required before batch processing becomes worthwhile.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. This reverses the appropriate use cases — batching suits the non-blocking overnight reports, not the latency-sensitive pre-merge checks.

text: A code-review tool has high false positives — flagging correct error handling as 'missing error handling' and intentional type assertions as 'unsafe casts.' Adding 'be more conservative' didn't help. What most effectively reduces false positives?

options.text: Add a confidence threshold that suppresses findings below 90% confidence
Increase the context window to give Claude more surrounding code
Provide 2–4 few-shot examples of the specific false-positive patterns and the correct non-flagging behavior
Use tool_choice to force a specific static-analysis tool instead of Claude's judgment

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Self-reported confidence thresholds are poorly calibrated and don't reliably distinguish true from false positives.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. More surrounding context doesn't fix the underlying reasoning error causing correct patterns to be mis-flagged.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. Providing 2-4 few-shot examples of the specific false-positive patterns alongside the correct non-flagging behavior teaches the model to generalize and stop over-reporting, more effectively than vague 'be conservative' instructions.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Forcing a separate static-analysis tool via tool_choice removes Claude's own review judgment entirely rather than improving it.

text: A CI/CD pipeline uses Claude Code for automated code review but hangs during execution because Claude is waiting for interactive input. What is the correct fix?

options.text: Set a CLAUDE_CI=true environment variable to enable headless mode
Add the -p (--print) flag to run Claude Code non-interactively
Redirect stdin from /dev/null to prevent interactive prompts
Use a --daemon flag to run Claude Code as a background process

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. CLAUDE_CI=true is not the documented environment variable for enabling Claude Code's non-interactive mode.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. The -p / --print flag runs Claude Code in non-interactive mode, processing the prompt, printing the result, and exiting without waiting for input, which is exactly what CI needs.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Redirecting stdin from /dev/null is a fragile workaround that doesn't actually change Claude Code's interactive behavior.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. There is no documented --daemon flag for running Claude Code as a background process in this context.

text: During a long debugging session in Claude Code, the agent starts giving vague, generic responses like 'this follows typical patterns' instead of referencing the specific classes and methods it examined earlier. What is happening and what should you do?

options.text: Context degradation from an extended session — use /compact and persist key findings in scratchpad files
The model's temperature is rising over time — restart with a lower temperature
The model is rate-limited — wait a few minutes before continuing
The agent needs more explicit instructions — add detailed debugging steps to the system prompt

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Vague, 'typical-patterns' answers replacing specific references are a classic sign of context degradation in a long session; running /compact and persisting key findings to scratchpad files preserves them across context boundaries.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Temperature doesn't drift over the course of a session and isn't even an available knob on current Claude models.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Rate limiting affects request throughput, not the specificity or quality of the agent's answers.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Adding more instructions to the system prompt adds further load to an already-overloaded context rather than relieving it.

text: A support agent resolves 55% of tickets but frequently escalates simple password resets while handling complex billing disputes itself. How should you improve escalation accuracy?

options.text: Add a confidence-score threshold where the agent self-reports certainty below 70% and escalates
Implement an ML classifier outside Claude to make escalation decisions
Add explicit escalation criteria with few-shot examples of when to escalate vs. resolve autonomously
Restrict the agent to only handle password resets and escalate everything else

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Self-reported confidence scores are poorly calibrated and don't reliably predict when escalation is actually warranted.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Building a separate ML classifier is heavy engineering for what is fundamentally a prompt-level calibration problem.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. Explicit, written escalation criteria plus few-shot examples showing the correct decision for each scenario type give the model concrete decision boundaries, fixing the miscalibration between simple and complex tickets.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Restricting the agent to only password resets throws away most of its value instead of fixing the escalation logic.

text: A data-extraction system shows 97% aggregate accuracy, but one vendor's invoices have a 23% error rate on 'tax_amount'. The current evaluation only checks aggregate accuracy across all vendors. What is the most effective improvement?

options.text: Lower the overall accuracy threshold from 97% to 90% to account for the problematic vendor
Add more training examples from the problematic vendor to the system prompt
Create a separate extraction pipeline for the problematic vendor
Use stratified sampling that measures accuracy by document type AND by field

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Lowering the overall accuracy threshold just hides the problem rather than surfacing or fixing it.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Adding training examples to the system prompt may help generation accuracy but doesn't fix the measurement gap that hid the issue in the first place.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Creating a separate pipeline for the problematic vendor is premature before you fully understand the distribution of issues across vendors and fields.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. Stratified sampling that measures accuracy by document type and by field surfaces concentrated failures, like one vendor's 23% tax_amount error rate, that aggregate accuracy hides.

text: After Claude responds with a tool_use block, how must you send the tool result back?

options.text: A user message with a tool_result content block (containing the tool_use_id and content), placed before any text blocks
A separate 'tool' role message with the tool name, result, and a reference to the call ID
An assistant message with a tool_result block appended after the tool_use block
A user message with the tool result as a plain text string referencing the tool name

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Tool results must be sent as a user message containing a tool_result content block keyed by tool_use_id, and that block must appear before any text blocks in the content array or the API returns a 400.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. There is no 'tool' role in the API; only user and assistant roles exist.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Tool results don't go in an assistant message; they must be sent in a user message.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. A plain text string lacks the tool_use_id correlation that the API requires to match the result to its originating tool call.

text: A document pipeline needs Claude to output JSON conforming to a specific schema. Prompt instructions alone occasionally yield malformed JSON with missing fields. What most reliably guarantees schema-compliant output?

options.text: Add JSON formatting examples in the system prompt with explicit field requirements
Use tool_use with a JSON schema and tool_choice forcing the extraction tool
Post-process Claude's output with a parser that fills defaults for missing fields
Use output_config.format with type json_schema (strict)

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Prompt-based JSON formatting examples are still probabilistic and can occasionally yield malformed JSON with missing fields.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. A forced tool_use call with a JSON schema also yields schema-valid output, but at the tool-call level rather than at the response level, so it's not the strongest guarantee here.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Post-processing to fill defaults fixes problems after they occur rather than preventing malformed output in the first place.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. Structured outputs via output_config.format with type json_schema (strict) constrain generation to the schema at the response level, the strongest guarantee of schema compliance.

text: A monorepo has frontend (React), backend (Express), and shared library packages, each needing context-specific instructions. What is the optimal CLAUDE.md configuration?

options.text: A single root CLAUDE.md with all instructions organized by package in sections
Root CLAUDE.md with universal standards plus subdirectory CLAUDE.md files for package-specific conventions
Only .claude/rules/ files with glob patterns targeting each package's file types
A user-level CLAUDE.md containing all project-specific instructions

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. A single root CLAUDE.md organized by package section still loads everything regardless of which package's context is currently relevant.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. The CLAUDE.md hierarchy is designed for this: a root file holds universal standards while per-package subdirectory CLAUDE.md files add package-specific conventions only when working in those directories.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Glob-based rule files work well for file-type conventions but don't capture broader package-level context the way subdirectory CLAUDE.md files do.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. A user-level CLAUDE.md isn't shared with the team, so project-specific instructions wouldn't be available to everyone.

text: A synthesis subagent occasionally needs fact-checking. 85% of fact-checks are simple lookups it could do itself; 15% need complex verification via the coordinator. What tool-distribution approach is recommended?

options.text: Remove all fact-checking from the synthesis agent and route everything through the coordinator
Give the synthesis agent full access to all research tools to avoid bottlenecks
Give the synthesis agent a scoped verify_fact tool for simple lookups; route complex checks through the coordinator
Create a dedicated fact-checking subagent that handles all verification regardless of complexity

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Routing all fact-checks, including the 85% of simple lookups, through the coordinator creates an unnecessary bottleneck.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Giving the synthesis agent full access to all research tools violates least privilege beyond what its role requires.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. A scoped verify_fact tool lets the synthesis agent handle the common 85% of simple lookups locally while still escalating the 15% of complex verification cases to the coordinator, balancing least privilege with practicality.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. A dedicated fact-checking subagent for all verification adds coordination overhead disproportionate to handling mostly trivial checks.

text: A coordinator wants to spawn multiple research subagents simultaneously to investigate different aspects of a question. How should it trigger parallel subagent execution?

options.text: Make sequential Task tool calls, one per subagent, with a 'parallel: true' flag
Configure the system prompt with a parallelism parameter specifying concurrent agent count
Use the batch processing API to submit all subagent requests simultaneously
Emit multiple Task tool calls in a single coordinator response

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. There is no 'parallel: true' flag for Task tool calls; sequential calls would also not achieve simultaneous execution.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. There is no system-prompt parallelism parameter for specifying a concurrent agent count.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. The Batches API is designed for independent, latency-tolerant requests, not for coordinating live, communicating subagents.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. Claude supports parallel tool calling: emitting multiple Task tool_use blocks within a single coordinator response allows the harness to execute those subagents concurrently.

text: A codebase-migration project requires investigating the legacy system, designing the new architecture, implementing changes, and writing tests. The phases are highly interdependent and the workers need to discuss findings. Subagents or agent teams?

options.text: Agent teams — teammates share findings, claim tasks from a shared list, and communicate directly
Subagents — each handles one phase and reports results back to the coordinator
A single agent session with extended context to maintain coherence across all phases
Prompt chaining with separate sessions per phase, passing summaries between them

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Agent teams support a collaborative model where teammates share findings, claim tasks from a shared list, and communicate directly, which fits highly interdependent phases that need back-and-forth discussion.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Subagents follow a report-back-only pattern to the coordinator, which doesn't support the direct discussion needed for interdependent phases.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A single long session risks context degradation as it accumulates work across all four interdependent phases.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Prompt chaining across separate sessions loses cross-phase context and real-time coordination between workers.

text: When tuning the effort parameter across a multi-agent system, which combination is most appropriate?

options.text: max for coordinator reasoning, high for primary analysis agents, low for simple data-fetching subagents
high for all agents to ensure consistent quality
low for the coordinator to minimize cost, max for analysis subagents
medium for all agents with manual temperature adjustments per task type

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Matching effort to cognitive load — max for coordinator reasoning, high for primary analysis, low for simple data-fetching — allocates compute efficiently across roles with different demands.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Setting high effort uniformly for all agents wastes tokens and cost on trivial, mechanical subagent tasks.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Minimizing the coordinator's effort starves it of the reasoning capacity it needs for planning and delegation.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Uniform medium effort with manual temperature adjustments ignores per-role tuning, and temperature isn't an available knob on current Claude models.

text: A financial-extraction pipeline uses a validation-retry loop. After three retries, 'total_amount' still fails because the extracted line items (which are correct) don't sum to the stated total. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: The retry loop needs more iterations to converge
The schema must be simplified to reduce extraction complexity
The retries are ineffective because the discrepancy is in the source document, not the extraction
Claude needs a higher effort level to do accurate arithmetic during extraction

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. More retry iterations won't converge on a correct total when the underlying line items, which are already correct, simply don't sum to the stated total in the source document.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Simplifying the schema doesn't address a discrepancy that originates in the source data, not in the extraction process.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. Since the line items are extracted correctly but don't sum to the stated total, the inconsistency exists in the source document itself, so retries on the extraction step can't fix it.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. A higher effort level for arithmetic doesn't help when the problem isn't extraction accuracy but a genuine inconsistency in the source document.

text: A research agent loses source attribution during summarization: the final report has statistics with no source, and conflicting data points are silently resolved by picking one value. What design change addresses both issues?

options.text: Add post-processing that uses web search to re-attribute claims
Require subagents to return structured data with claim-to-source mappings, and annotate conflicts with attribution rather than silently picking one value
Increase the context window to prevent information loss during summarization
Store all raw sources in a database and link report claims to source IDs

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Re-attributing claims after the fact via web search is unreliable and doesn't fix the structural loss of provenance during summarization.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. Requiring subagents to return structured claim-to-source mappings preserves provenance through the pipeline, and annotating conflicts (showing both values with their sources) avoids silently resolving them by picking one value.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A bigger context window doesn't address the structural reason attribution is lost during the summarization step.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Storing raw sources in a database with linked IDs adds infrastructure but still doesn't fix how the summary itself handles attribution and conflicting data.

text: A developer's response includes both a text block and a tool_use block. When sending the tool result, they place a text block before the tool_result block in the user message and get a 400 error. What is wrong?

options.text: Text blocks are not allowed in messages that contain tool_result blocks
The tool_result must be sent in a separate message from any text content
tool_result blocks must come first in the user message's content array, before any text blocks
The original tool_use block must be included in the tool_result message

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Text blocks are allowed in messages containing tool_result blocks; the issue is ordering, not exclusion.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. tool_result doesn't need to be in a separate message from text content; they can coexist in the same message.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. The API requires tool_result blocks to come first in the user message's content array, before any text blocks, so placing text ahead of the tool_result triggers a 400 error.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. The original tool_use block belongs in the prior assistant message and should not be echoed into the tool_result message.

text: A Claude Code skill for database migrations should accept a migration name as a parameter and use it in the skill's instructions. Which feature enables this?

options.text: A required-params field in the SKILL.md frontmatter
A custom input_schema JSON definition in the frontmatter
Environment variables set before invocation via a shell frontmatter field
The $ARGUMENTS substitution variable in the skill body, with argument-hint in frontmatter to prompt for it

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Skills don't use a required-params frontmatter field for parameter passing.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Skills don't define a custom input_schema JSON definition in their frontmatter.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A shell frontmatter field is meant for setup actions, not for passing parameters like a migration name into the skill body.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. Skill bodies support $ARGUMENTS substitution (and positional $1, $2…) for the migration name, and an argument-hint field in frontmatter prompts the user for the value when it isn't supplied.

text: An MCP tool returns a transient timeout on its first call, but the agent treats all errors identically and abandons the operation. How should the tool's error response be improved for smarter recovery?

options.text: Return structured error metadata including errorCategory, an isRetryable boolean, and a description distinguishing transient from permanent failures
Return a generic is_error: true with a human-readable message
Implement automatic retry inside the tool, hiding transient errors from the agent
Log all errors externally and return success with empty results

optionExplanations.A: Correct. Structured error metadata, including errorCategory, an isRetryable boolean, and a description distinguishing transient from permanent failures, lets the agent decide intelligently whether to retry or escalate.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. A generic is_error: true with just a message gives the agent no signal about whether the failure is worth retrying.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Hiding retries inside the tool removes the agent's ability to reason about failures and can mask systemic problems from view.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Returning fake success with empty results is an anti-pattern that corrupts downstream reasoning by hiding the fact that the call actually failed.

text: A security-audit skill produces verbose logging that fills the main conversation context and crowds out earlier context. Which skill configuration prevents this?

options.text: Set max_tokens in the skill frontmatter to limit output length
Add logging: false to suppress verbose tool output
Run the skill in a forked/isolated sub-agent context so only its final result returns to the main conversation
Use the Explore subagent instead of a custom skill

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. max_tokens limits output length but isn't the mechanism for isolating verbose intermediate output from the main conversation.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. A logging: false flag isn't the right frontmatter mechanism for isolating a skill's output from the main conversation.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. Running the skill in a forked/isolated sub-agent context keeps its verbose logging out of the main conversation, returning only the summarized final result.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Switching to the Explore subagent replaces the custom skill entirely rather than configuring the skill itself to isolate its output.

text: An architect needs to see which Claude Code memory files are currently loaded in a session, to debug why some instructions seem ignored. Which command shows this?

options.text: /status
/config
/memory — shows which memory (CLAUDE.md) files are loaded in the current session
/debug

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. /status reports session/connection information, not which memory files are currently loaded.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. /config is for adjusting configuration settings, not for inspecting loaded memory files.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. /memory shows which CLAUDE.md memory files are loaded at the various levels (user, project, subdirectory) in the current session, which is exactly how you debug instructions that aren't taking effect.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. There is no /debug command serving this purpose in Claude Code.

text: A long customer-support conversation is nearing the context-window limit. You must keep operating without losing critical transaction details (order numbers, refund amounts, commitments) from earlier. What is recommended?

options.text: Increase max_tokens to expand the context window
Extract transactional facts into a persistent 'case facts' block and use compaction to summarize older conversation
Start a new conversation and ask the customer to repeat the issue
Enable streaming to reduce the conversation's memory footprint

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. max_tokens controls the length of generated output, not the size of the context window available for the conversation.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. Extracting transactional facts (order numbers, refund amounts, commitments) into a persistent 'case facts' block, combined with compaction to summarize older conversation, preserves critical details while freeing up context space.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Starting a new conversation and asking the customer to repeat the issue is a poor customer experience and discards information unnecessarily.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Streaming affects how the response is delivered, not how much context the conversation consumes.

text: A team wants to use the Explore subagent in Claude Code to understand a large codebase before making changes. What are its key characteristics?

options.text: It uses a lightweight model (Haiku) with read-only access, isolates verbose output, and returns summaries
It uses a high-capability model with full write access for comprehensive exploration
It runs in the same context as the main agent but with enhanced search
It spawns multiple parallel sub-agents to explore different parts simultaneously

optionExplanations.A: Correct. The Explore subagent uses a lightweight model (Haiku) with read-only access, isolates its verbose searching output from the main conversation, and returns concise summaries, preserving the main context.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. The Explore subagent doesn't use a high-capability model with full write access; it's intentionally lightweight and read-only.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. The Explore subagent runs in its own isolated context rather than sharing the main agent's context.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. The Explore subagent is a single focused discovery agent, not a parallel fan-out of multiple sub-agents.

text: A coordinator must pass findings from a web_search subagent to a synthesis subagent. The web_search agent returns a large amount of raw data (full page contents, URLs, timestamps, metadata). What is the best practice for structuring this transfer?

options.text: Use a structured format that separates content from metadata, including source URLs and page references
Pass the complete raw data to preserve fidelity
Have the coordinator summarize findings into a brief paragraph before passing them on
Store raw data in a shared database and pass only record IDs

optionExplanations.A: Correct. A structured format that separates content from metadata, including source URLs and page references, preserves provenance while keeping the payload lean and consumable by the synthesis subagent.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Passing the complete raw data (full page contents, URLs, timestamps, metadata) bloats the synthesis subagent's context unnecessarily.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Having the coordinator pre-summarize into a brief paragraph risks losing detail that the synthesis step actually needs.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Storing raw data in a shared database and passing only record IDs adds infrastructure that the synthesis agent may not be able to query.

text: Using extended/adaptive thinking together with tool use, a developer strips all thinking blocks from the conversation before sending the next request, and the API errors. What rule was violated?

options.text: Thinking blocks must always be preserved in the full conversation history forever
Thinking blocks can only be removed after the entire conversation ends
Thinking is incompatible with tool use and must be disabled during tool calls
Thinking blocks must be returned alongside their corresponding tool results; they can be dropped only after those tool results have been processed

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Thinking blocks don't need to be preserved forever; they can be dropped on later turns once their corresponding tool results have been processed.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Thinking blocks can be removed before the conversation ends, just not before their corresponding tool results have been processed.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Thinking is fully compatible with tool use; it's not disabled during tool calls.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. Thinking blocks tied to a turn's tool_use must be echoed back alongside that turn's tool results on the same model; stripping them before the tool results have been processed breaks the turn and causes the API to error.

text: An agent harness is running a conversation that exceeds ~150,000 input tokens and wants to automatically summarize older context while keeping recent turns. Which API feature handles this?

options.text: A token-limit parameter that auto-truncates messages at 150K
Automatic prompt caching that compresses repeated content
A client-side sliding window that drops the oldest messages
Server-side compaction (beta) configured with a trigger threshold

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. There is no auto-truncate token-limit parameter that summarizes older context at 150K tokens.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Automatic prompt caching reduces cost on repeated content prefixes but doesn't shrink or summarize the context itself.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A client-side sliding window is a manual approach that bluntly drops the oldest messages without intelligent summarization.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. Server-side compaction (a beta feature) automatically summarizes earlier context when input tokens cross a configurable threshold, emitting a compaction block that subsequent requests build on.

text: An MCP server needs API tokens for authentication. The tokens must stay out of version control but be available to all team members. How should this be configured in .mcp.json?

options.text: Hardcode the tokens directly in .mcp.json
Use environment-variable expansion like ${API_TOKEN} in .mcp.json, with each developer setting the variable locally
Store tokens in a separate gitignored .mcp-secrets.json file
Put tokens in user-level config and cross-reference them from .mcp.json

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Hardcoding tokens directly in .mcp.json leaks secrets into version control.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. .mcp.json supports ${VARIABLE} environment-variable expansion, so the committed config references a variable while the actual secret lives in each developer's local environment, keeping it version-controllable yet secret-free.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A separate gitignored secrets file is a non-standard mechanism for this configuration.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Cross-referencing tokens between user-level config and .mcp.json isn't a supported mechanism.

text: A review system for a 14-file pull request shows inconsistent depth across files, missed bugs in complex files, and contradictory feedback between files. What architecture best addresses this?

options.text: One file per subagent, reviewed in parallel with no cross-file coordination
Increase the context window to include all 14 files at once
Per-file local analysis passes, followed by a separate cross-file integration pass
A single long review session with instructions to keep depth consistent

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Reviewing each file in parallel with no cross-file coordination misses interactions between files, like incompatible API changes.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Increasing the context window to include all 14 files doesn't solve the attention dilution causing inconsistent depth and missed bugs.
optionExplanations.C: Correct. Per-file local analysis passes fix attention dilution by giving each file focused, consistent depth, while a separate cross-file integration pass catches interactions like incompatible API changes between files.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. A single long review session with consistency instructions reproduces the original problem of inconsistent depth across files.

text: A code-review system prompt must cover security vulnerabilities, code style, and documentation completeness, but natural-language paragraphs lead to inconsistent rule application. Which structuring technique most improves consistency?

options.text: Use XML tags to structure each concern area with explicit criteria
Number each rule and add a 1–10 priority ranking
Write all rules as a single bulleted paragraph for easy scanning
Create separate system prompts per concern and run three independent reviews

optionExplanations.A: Correct. XML-tagged sections (e.g. <security_rules>, <style_rules>, <documentation_rules>) give Claude clear structural boundaries between concern areas, reducing cross-contamination and improving consistent rule application.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. Numbering rules with a priority ranking adds ordering information but doesn't structurally separate the different concern areas.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. A single bulleted paragraph is still unstructured and doesn't separate the security, style, and documentation concerns from each other.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Running three separate independent reviews triples cost and doesn't improve consistency within any single review.

text: A support agent's customer asks to speak with a human manager after one failed troubleshooting attempt. The agent has more troubleshooting steps it could try. What should it do?

options.text: Attempt the remaining troubleshooting steps since it might resolve the issue
Honor the customer's explicit request for a human immediately
Ask whether they'd like to try one more step before escalating
Escalate, but first run diagnostics to give the human context

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Attempting more troubleshooting steps ignores the customer's explicit request to speak with a human.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. An explicit request for a human is a hard escalation trigger and should be honored immediately, since overriding it to keep troubleshooting damages customer trust.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Asking the customer to try one more step delays the escalation they explicitly requested.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. Running diagnostics before escalating also introduces delay instead of honoring the request immediately, even though attaching context to the handoff afterward is good practice.

text: Using the Claude Agent SDK (Python), a developer wants to load project-level configuration — skills, CLAUDE.md memory, and custom commands. How should they configure the query() call?

options.text: Pass config_path pointing to the project's .claude directory
Use the --settings CLI flag to point at the config files
Manually read and inject CLAUDE.md into the system prompt
Set setting_sources to include 'project' in ClaudeAgentOptions

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. config_path is not the SDK mechanism for loading project-level skills, memory, and commands.
optionExplanations.B: Incorrect. --settings is a CLI flag, not an option available within the Python SDK's query() configuration.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Manually reading and injecting CLAUDE.md into the system prompt only handles memory and misses skills and custom commands entirely.
optionExplanations.D: Correct. Setting setting_sources to include 'project' in ClaudeAgentOptions opts the query() call into loading project-level skills, CLAUDE.md memory, and custom commands.

text: For large batch reports, Claude sometimes produces very detailed thinking traces that increase time and cost even on straightforward extraction. What configuration helps optimize this?

options.text: Disable extended thinking entirely for all batch processing
Set effort to 'low' for batch extraction so Claude reduces thinking depth on simple items
Reduce the context window to force shorter thinking traces
Set max_tokens low to truncate lengthy thinking traces

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Disabling extended thinking entirely removes its benefit on any items in the batch that genuinely need deeper reasoning.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. The effort parameter is a behavioral dial across thinking, tool calls, and verbosity; setting it to 'low' makes Claude far more economical on straightforward extraction items while still allowing deeper thinking when genuinely needed.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. Reducing the context window doesn't specifically target or reduce the length of thinking traces.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. A low max_tokens truncates the final output rather than reducing the depth of the thinking process itself.

text: An agentic system using prompt caching sees cache hits drop to 0% whenever the tool definitions change, even though the rest of the prompt is identical. What explains this?

options.text: Tool definitions aren't part of the cache, so something else must be the cause
Tool definitions sit at the top of the cache hierarchy; changing them invalidates the tools, system, and messages layers
Caching requires a minimum of 4096 tokens of tool definitions
The 5-minute cache TTL expired between calls

optionExplanations.A: Incorrect. Tool definitions are in fact part of the prompt cache, so they are exactly the cause here, not a separate issue.
optionExplanations.B: Correct. Prompt caching is a prefix match with render order tools → system → messages; since tools sit at the top of that hierarchy, changing tool definitions invalidates the tools, system, and messages layers, dropping cache hits to zero.
optionExplanations.C: Incorrect. There is no minimum 4096-token rule specific to tool definitions for caching to apply.
optionExplanations.D: Incorrect. TTL expiry wouldn't correlate specifically with tool-definition changes; the cache invalidation here is caused by the prefix-match hierarchy, not the cache simply expiring.

text: A legal-contract extractor uses a JSON schema with required fields effective_date, termination_clause, governing_law. Some contracts are drafts with no termination clause, and Claude fabricates one. What schema change prevents this?

options.text: Add a draft_status boolean field to indicate incomplete contracts
Make termination_clause nullable so Claude can return null instead of fabricating
Remove termination_clause from required and make it optional
Add post-processing validation to detect likely-fabricated clauses

optionExplanations.A: A draft_status flag would let Claude signal an incomplete contract, but it does nothing to stop the model from still fabricating a termination_clause value for that same draft, since the required field still demands content.
optionExplanations.B: Making termination_clause nullable gives the model a sanctioned, schema-legal way to represent absence as null instead of inventing a clause that isn't present in the draft contract, which is exactly why it is the standard 'don't guess' pattern.
optionExplanations.C: Simply making the field optional may stop fabrication but can also cause silent omission of the field with no signal at all about whether it was deliberately absent or just missed, which is weaker than an explicit null.
optionExplanations.D: Post-processing validation to detect likely-fabricated clauses only reacts after the fabrication has already happened, rather than preventing the model from inventing the clause in the first place.

text: A CLI tool runs Claude Code in a Docker container for CI code reviews and needs consistent results regardless of any local configuration. Which flag combination is most appropriate?

options.text: -p with --output-format json
-p with --bare and --output-format json
-p with --bare plus an explicit ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to skip local config discovery
-p with --system-prompt to override loaded CLAUDE.md files

optionExplanations.A: Print mode with JSON output makes results easy to parse programmatically, but on its own it does not prevent the CLI from auto-discovering local hooks, skills, MCP servers, or CLAUDE.md files that could change behavior between machines.
optionExplanations.B: Adding --bare on top of JSON output stops auto-discovery of local project configuration, but without explicitly supplying the API key there is still ambiguity from keychain or OAuth-based credential discovery that could vary across CI environments.
optionExplanations.C: Combining -p (non-interactive mode) with --bare (skip auto-discovery of hooks/skills/MCP/CLAUDE.md) and an explicit ANTHROPIC_API_KEY removes both configuration-based and credential-based sources of variance, which is exactly what determinism in a Docker CI environment requires.
optionExplanations.D: Overriding only the system prompt addresses one possible source of variation but leaves all other local configuration sources (hooks, skills, MCP servers, credentials) active and able to affect results.

text: A coordinator routes a message mentioning both billing AND shipping to the billing agent only. What design change most effectively handles both concerns?

options.text: Train the coordinator with few-shot examples of multi-concern routing
Decompose the request into separate items and route each to the appropriate specialist
Keyword detection to identify all topics before routing
A generalist agent that handles all request types without routing

optionExplanations.A: Few-shot examples of multi-concern routing can improve the coordinator's behavior in similar cases, but it is still a probabilistic pattern-matching fix that doesn't guarantee complete coverage for every multi-concern message.
optionExplanations.B: Decomposing the request into separate concern-items and routing each one to its appropriate specialist guarantees that both billing and shipping concerns are handled, while still preserving the benefits of specialized agents for each domain.
optionExplanations.C: Keyword detection to identify all topics before routing is brittle, since it depends on exact wording and can miss nuanced or implicitly stated concerns that don't contain the expected keywords.
optionExplanations.D: A single generalist agent that handles all request types without routing would address both concerns but sacrifices the specialization benefits that dedicated billing and shipping agents provide.

text: Claude's code-review output varies in format (sometimes JSON, sometimes markdown, sometimes prose). You need consistent structured output every time. Which approach gives the strongest format guarantee (among these options)?

options.text: Add explicit format instructions and examples to the system prompt
Use few-shot examples of the exact desired JSON
Define a tool with a JSON schema for the output and force it with tool_choice
Post-process Claude's output to convert any format to the target

optionExplanations.A: Explicit format instructions and examples in the system prompt can steer Claude toward more consistent output, but as a prompt-level technique it remains probabilistic and doesn't guarantee the format every time.
optionExplanations.B: Few-shot examples of the exact desired JSON help the model imitate the target structure, but like other prompting techniques, it improves consistency without architecturally enforcing it.
optionExplanations.C: Defining a tool with a JSON schema for the output and forcing it via tool_choice constrains Claude's response to conform to that schema, which eliminates the format variance seen with JSON/markdown/prose mixing, making it the strongest guarantee among the listed options.
optionExplanations.D: Post-processing Claude's output to convert any format to the target only fixes the inconsistency after generation rather than preventing the format variance from occurring in the first place.

text: A long-running agent makes repeated API calls: the system prompt and first few turns are stable, but recent messages change frequently. Where should explicit cache breakpoints go for optimal savings?

options.text: On the last stable content block before the frequently-changing messages, using up to 4 breakpoints on stable blocks
On every message to cache as much as possible
Only on the system prompt, since tool definitions shouldn't be cached
On the most recent messages to cache the newest content

optionExplanations.A: Placing up to 4 explicit breakpoints on the last stable content blocks before the frequently-changing messages maximizes the cacheable prefix, since content after the final breakpoint changes per request anyway and wouldn't benefit from caching.
optionExplanations.B: Setting a breakpoint on every message wastes cache writes on volatile, frequently-changing content that will rarely if ever be reused, reducing the efficiency of the caching strategy.
optionExplanations.C: Restricting breakpoints to only the system prompt and excluding tool definitions misses additional caching opportunities, since tool definitions are also part of the stable prefix that can benefit from caching.
optionExplanations.D: Placing breakpoints on the most recent messages caches content that changes every turn, so there is essentially nothing stable to reuse and savings are lost.

text: A document classifier uses an enum: invoice, contract, memo, report. 15% of documents fit none, and Claude forces them into the closest match. How should the schema be redesigned?

options.text: Add more enum values to cover all possible types
Remove the enum and use a free-text category string
Add an 'other' enum value paired with a detail string describing the actual category
Add a confidence score that flags uncertain classifications

optionExplanations.A: Adding more enum values to try to cover every possible document type is impractical since you cannot enumerate every type that might appear, leaving the same forcing problem for whatever falls outside the expanded list.
optionExplanations.B: Removing the enum entirely and using a free-text category string would accept any document type, but it loses the structure and validation benefits that the enum provided for the known categories.
optionExplanations.C: Adding an 'other' enum value paired with a detail string lets Claude legitimately classify the 15% of documents that don't fit the known categories while preserving enum structure for known types and capturing what those unknown documents actually are for later analysis.
optionExplanations.D: A confidence score that flags uncertain classifications doesn't stop the underlying problem of forced misclassification, especially when the model is confidently wrong because no legitimate alternative category exists.

text: A support agent finds two customer records matching the provided name (one NY, one Chicago); the customer gave no other identifier. What is the correct behavior?

options.text: Select the most recently active account as the likely match
Process the request against both accounts and let the customer clarify later
Request additional identifying information to disambiguate
Use IP geolocation to pick the account

optionExplanations.A: Selecting the most recently active account as the likely match is an unreliable heuristic that risks acting on the wrong customer's account when there is no real evidence pointing to either record.
optionExplanations.B: Processing the request against both accounts exposes or affects an account that may not belong to the actual customer, creating privacy and financial risk for the unrelated party.
optionExplanations.C: Requesting additional identifying information to disambiguate is the correct approach because acting on an ambiguous identity carries financial and privacy consequences, so the agent should resolve the ambiguity rather than guess.
optionExplanations.D: Using IP geolocation to pick an account is an unreliable signal that doesn't reliably establish which record actually belongs to the customer, risking incorrect account selection.

text: A developer wants file modifications under /production to require explicit approval, while other directories auto-approve. Which hook configuration achieves this?

options.text: A permission hook that denies all write operations system-wide
A PostToolUse hook that reverts changes to /production after they're made
A PreToolUse hook on Write/Edit that matches /production paths and returns an 'ask' (confirm) decision
A CLAUDE.md instruction to always ask before modifying production files

optionExplanations.A: A permission hook that denies all write operations system-wide is too broad, since it blocks legitimate auto-approved edits to directories outside /production as well.
optionExplanations.B: A PostToolUse hook that reverts changes to /production after they're made is dangerous, since the change has already been applied (and possibly had side effects) before being undone.
optionExplanations.C: A PreToolUse hook on Write/Edit that matches /production paths and returns an 'ask' (confirm) decision precisely gates only the edits under /production while letting changes elsewhere pass through automatically, exactly meeting the requirement.
optionExplanations.D: A CLAUDE.md instruction to always ask before modifying production files is a probabilistic, prompt-level control, which is not reliable enough for a safety-relevant gate around production files.

text: An architect must have Claude answer questions about a 500-page regulatory document that exceeds practical attention even within the context window. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Split the document into chunks and process each with separate API calls
Summarize the document first, then answer from the summary
Use embeddings to find relevant chunks and include only those
Place the full document at the top of the prompt and the questions at the end (long-context layout)

optionExplanations.A: Splitting the document into chunks processed by separate API calls loses cross-section context, which matters for a regulatory document where clauses may reference or depend on one another.
optionExplanations.B: Summarizing the document first and answering from the summary loses the fine-grained detail that questions about a 500-page regulatory document are likely to require.
optionExplanations.C: Using embeddings to retrieve only relevant chunks adds unnecessary complexity and retrieval risk when the full document actually fits within Claude's large context window.
optionExplanations.D: Placing the full document at the top of the prompt and the questions at the end follows Claude's recommended long-context layout (data first, queries last), which improves answer quality since the document fits within the context window despite exceeding practical attention span.

text: A shared project must enforce mocking conventions for test files spread across dozens of __tests__/ directories, without dozens of duplicated CLAUDE.md files. What is the most efficient solution?

options.text: Put all testing conventions in the root CLAUDE.md so they're always available
Create a rule file scoped by glob patterns like **/__tests__/** and **/*.test.* in its frontmatter
Create a test-conventions skill that developers invoke before writing tests
Use a pre-commit hook to validate test files against conventions

optionExplanations.A: Putting all testing conventions in the root CLAUDE.md causes them to load even when editing non-test application code, which is unnecessary context overhead for files that aren't tests.
optionExplanations.B: A rule file scoped by glob patterns like **/__tests__/** and **/*.test.* loads the mocking conventions automatically only when matching test files are being edited, across every __tests__/ directory in the project, without any duplication.
optionExplanations.C: A test-conventions skill requires developers to manually invoke it before writing tests, so conventions aren't guaranteed to be applied unless someone remembers to call it.
optionExplanations.D: A pre-commit hook validates test files against conventions only after they've already been written, reacting to violations rather than guiding the writing of correct tests from the start.

text: A customer-lookup MCP tool returns order history. Which response format best serves the downstream agent?

options.text: A formatted markdown table with all order details for readability
Raw database JSON with internal IDs, audit timestamps, and system metadata
A natural-language summary paragraph of the order history
Only high-signal fields with semantic, stable identifiers — status, amounts, dates, customer-facing order IDs

optionExplanations.A: A formatted markdown table with all order details optimizes for human readability rather than for what a downstream agent needs to reason over programmatically.
optionExplanations.B: Raw database JSON with internal IDs, audit timestamps, and system metadata wastes context on internal fields that aren't relevant to the agent's task and adds noise.
optionExplanations.C: A natural-language summary paragraph discards the structured data that the agent could otherwise use programmatically to take action on the order history.
optionExplanations.D: Returning only high-signal fields with semantic, stable identifiers like status, amounts, dates, and customer-facing order IDs gives the agent exactly what it needs to reason and act, without irrelevant internal noise.

text: An architect must understand a large unfamiliar codebase's architecture before changing it, while preserving main-conversation context. Which workflow is best?

options.text: Use the Explore subagent for verbose discovery, then plan mode for architecture decisions, then direct execution
Use Grep extensively, then manually piece together the architecture
Read all key files, then ask Claude to summarize
Use Bash to run analysis tools and pipe output into the conversation

optionExplanations.A: Using the Explore subagent for verbose discovery isolates the noisy exploration work and returns concise summaries, then plan mode lets the architect review the approach before direct execution, keeping the main conversation context clean throughout.
optionExplanations.B: Using Grep extensively and manually piecing together the architecture is inefficient, requiring significant manual effort to assemble a coherent picture of the codebase.
optionExplanations.C: Reading all key files directly and asking Claude to summarize floods the main context with raw file content instead of isolating that discovery work elsewhere.
optionExplanations.D: Using Bash to run analysis tools and piping output into the conversation also floods the main context with verbose output rather than keeping discovery contained.

text: With server-side compaction, an architect wants to inject fresh context (e.g., updated tool results) after compaction occurs but before the conversation continues. Which configuration enables this?

options.text: A pause-after-compaction option that returns a 'compaction' stop_reason, letting the harness inject context before continuing
Set 'instructions' in the compaction config to embed the extra context in the summary
A PreCompact hook that injects context before compaction occurs
Append context as a system message after receiving the compaction block

optionExplanations.A: A pause-after-compaction option that returns a dedicated 'compaction' stop_reason hands control back to the harness after compaction completes, letting it inject fresh context (like updated tool results) before the conversation continues.
optionExplanations.B: Setting 'instructions' in the compaction config shapes what goes into the summary itself, but it doesn't provide a mechanism for injecting arbitrary fresh context after compaction has already occurred.
optionExplanations.C: A PreCompact hook injects context before compaction occurs, which risks that injected content being summarized away during the compaction process itself, rather than being preserved fresh afterward.
optionExplanations.D: Appending context as a system message after receiving the compaction block isn't the documented mechanism for this; the configuration is the pause-after-compaction stop_reason rather than a manual append step.
