text: Claude flags the same issue repeatedly across CI re-runs of the same PR, generating duplicate review comments. What is recommended to prevent this?

options.text: Use a unique session ID per PR and append all previous reviews to context
Include prior review findings in the context for subsequent runs to avoid duplicate flagging
Cache review results and skip files that haven't changed
Deduplicate comments in post-processing before posting

optionExplanations.A: Using a unique session ID per PR and appending all previous reviews to context bloats the context with full review history without specifically targeting the duplicate-flagging problem.
optionExplanations.B: Including prior review findings in the context for subsequent runs lets Claude focus on new or unresolved issues instead of re-reporting already-known findings, directly addressing the duplication.
optionExplanations.C: Caching review results and skipping unchanged files would miss recurring issues that still exist in code that hasn't changed since the last run.
optionExplanations.D: Deduplicating comments in post-processing treats the symptom by removing duplicates after they're generated, rather than preventing Claude from re-flagging the same issue in the first place.

text: Claude must always follow a fixed sequence (analyze → identify issues → rank by severity → recommend) but occasionally skips the ranking step. What most reliably enforces the full sequence?

options.text: Add explicit numbered steps with 'you MUST complete all 4'
Use XML tags for each step with detailed instructions
Prompt chaining — a separate API call per step, each feeding the next
Add a self-check: 'verify all 4 steps are completed before responding'

optionExplanations.A: Adding explicit numbered steps with 'you MUST complete all 4' improves compliance but remains a probabilistic instruction within a single response, so the ranking step can still be skipped occasionally.
optionExplanations.B: Using XML tags for each step with detailed instructions similarly improves structure and compliance but is still probabilistic within one response and doesn't guarantee the step can't be skipped.
optionExplanations.C: Prompt chaining makes each step its own separate API call, so a step like ranking by severity must complete and produce output before the next call begins, and intermediate results can be inspected, which most reliably enforces the full sequence.
optionExplanations.D: Adding a self-check instruction to verify all 4 steps are completed before responding improves compliance but is still a probabilistic check within a single response rather than a structural guarantee.

text: An agent has get_customer and lookup_order, each with a one-line description, and frequently calls get_customer when it should call lookup_order. What is the root cause?

options.text: The tools overlap and should be merged into one
The agent needs more training data on tool selection
The tool descriptions are too minimal — they need detail on inputs, outputs, edge cases, and when to use each
tool_choice should force the correct tool based on conversation state

optionExplanations.A: The tools serve genuinely different purposes (customer lookup vs. order lookup), so merging them into one tool would be wrong rather than addressing the actual problem with descriptions.
optionExplanations.B: You don't 'train' the underlying API model on tool selection; tool choice is driven by the descriptions provided at call time, not by additional training data.
optionExplanations.C: The tool descriptions are too minimal — one-line descriptions can't disambiguate similar tools, so adding detail on inputs, outputs, edge cases, and when to use each is the root-cause fix, since tool descriptions are the primary signal Claude uses to choose tools.
optionExplanations.D: Forcing the correct tool via tool_choice based on conversation state overrides Claude's own reasoning rather than informing it, and doesn't fix the underlying ambiguity in how the tools are described.

text: A schema extracts experiments from research papers; papers have anywhere from 2 to 15 experiments with varying measurement types. How should the schema handle this variability?

options.text: A fixed array of 15 experiment slots, most defaulting to empty
A flexible array whose items follow a reusable experiment sub-schema with clear required and optional fields
A single concatenated text field for all experiments
Separate schemas per experiment count

optionExplanations.A: A fixed array of 15 experiment slots wastes space and confuses the model when most papers have far fewer experiments, since most slots would default to empty.
optionExplanations.B: A flexible array whose items follow a reusable experiment sub-schema with clear required and optional fields handles the 2-to-15 range while preserving structure, with required fields guaranteeing quality and optional fields absorbing variation in measurement types.
optionExplanations.C: A single concatenated text field for all experiments loses all the structure that makes the extracted data usable for analysis.
optionExplanations.D: Separate schemas per experiment count would be unmaintainable, requiring a distinct schema definition for every possible count from 2 to 15.

text: With extended thinking enabled, tool_choice 'any' returns an error, but the same setup works with tool_choice 'auto'. What restriction causes this?

options.text: Extended thinking doubles token usage and exceeds the window with 'any'
Extended thinking requires specific tools in allowed_tools before 'any' can be used
'any' is deprecated and should be replaced with 'auto' everywhere
With extended thinking, only 'auto' and 'none' tool_choice are supported; 'any' and forced tool selection error

optionExplanations.A: Extended thinking doubling token usage and exceeding the context window is unrelated to why 'any' specifically errors while 'auto' works; the issue is about which tool_choice values are permitted, not token budget.
optionExplanations.B: The restriction isn't about needing specific tools listed in allowed_tools before 'any' can be used; the issue is that 'any' itself is restricted when extended thinking is enabled, regardless of which tools are listed.
optionExplanations.C: 'any' is not deprecated and is not meant to be universally replaced by 'auto'; it simply isn't supported in combination with extended thinking.
optionExplanations.D: With extended thinking enabled, only 'auto' and 'none' tool_choice values are supported, since forcing 'any' or a specific tool would conflict with letting the model reason about whether and which tool to use, causing the error.

text: A team keeps the system prompt and tool definitions stable but alternates tool_choice between 'auto' and a forced tool across consecutive calls, and sees low cache hit rates. Why?

options.text: tool_choice changes invalidate the messages-level cache, lowering overall hit rates
The 5-minute TTL expires between consecutive calls
The system prompt is below the minimum cacheable length
Forced tool selection disables prompt caching entirely

optionExplanations.A: Changing tool_choice between calls invalidates the messages-level layer of the cache hierarchy, while the tools and system prompt layers can still be cached, which explains why alternating tool_choice lowers but doesn't zero out the overall hit rate.
optionExplanations.B: The 5-minute TTL expiring between consecutive calls wouldn't correlate specifically with the pattern of alternating tool_choice values, so it doesn't explain this particular drop in hit rate.
optionExplanations.C: The system prompt being below the minimum cacheable length isn't indicated here, and isn't the mechanism that ties cache invalidation to changes in tool_choice.
optionExplanations.D: Forced tool selection doesn't disable prompt caching entirely; caching still works with forced tool choice, it's the alternation between values that invalidates the messages layer.

text: A handoff to a human currently passes only the customer's name and last message. What structured handoff information should be included?

options.text: Customer details, root-cause analysis, steps already attempted, and recommended next actions
The complete conversation transcript so the human can read everything
A confidence score explaining why the agent couldn't resolve it
A reference to the customer's previous support tickets

optionExplanations.A: A structured handoff including customer details, root-cause analysis, steps already attempted, and recommended next actions lets the human continue immediately without having to re-diagnose the issue from scratch.
optionExplanations.B: The complete conversation transcript gives the human everything but is too much to parse efficiently compared to a distilled, structured summary.
optionExplanations.C: A confidence score explaining why the agent couldn't resolve it isn't actionable on its own; it doesn't tell the human what's been tried or what to do next.
optionExplanations.D: A reference to the customer's previous support tickets is useful background information, but it isn't a summary of the current interaction the human needs to act on immediately.

text: A model with context awareness receives a system warning about high token usage. What does this indicate and what behavior should you expect?

options.text: The API will automatically truncate the next response
The model automatically switches to a lower-effort mode
An error will occur on the next request
The model should proactively manage context — compacting, summarizing, or being more concise — since it knows the remaining budget

optionExplanations.A: A system warning about high token usage isn't an automatic truncation mechanism for the next response; the API doesn't unilaterally cut off output because of the warning.
optionExplanations.B: The model doesn't automatically switch to a lower-effort mode in response to the warning; effort/behavior changes are not an automatic side effect of context awareness.
optionExplanations.C: The warning precedes a potential error rather than being one itself; it doesn't mean an error will definitely occur on the next request.
optionExplanations.D: Context awareness lets the model see its remaining token budget and respond behaviorally, such as compacting, summarizing, or being more concise, since it knows how much budget remains and can proactively manage it.

text: After several Claude Code sessions, auto-accumulated project memories start affecting behavior. Where are these automatically-accumulated memories stored?

options.text: Appended to the root CLAUDE.md after the original content
In a per-project memory directory (e.g. ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md), with the first portion auto-loaded each session
In a .claude/memory/ directory in the project, shared with the team
In session history, persisted between restarts but not in any file

optionExplanations.A: Auto-accumulated memories are not appended to the root CLAUDE.md; that file holds project instructions, not the personal auto-memory store.
optionExplanations.B: These memories are stored in a per-project memory directory at the user level (e.g. ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md), with the first portion automatically loaded into each session, making them personal rather than shared with the team.
optionExplanations.C: Auto-accumulated memories are not stored in a shared .claude/memory/ directory within the project; that would make personal accumulated context visible to the whole team, which isn't how this feature works.
optionExplanations.D: The memories are persisted in an actual file rather than only in transient session history, so they survive beyond just being held in memory between restarts.

text: Using the API-level MCP connector, a team wants to restrict the agent to only 'search' and 'retrieve' tools from a server, blocking all others. How?

options.text: List only the allowed tools in the mcp_servers configuration
Add filtering logic in the MCP server to only expose search and retrieve
Use tool_choice to only allow search and retrieve
Use an mcp_toolset entry with an allowlist pattern matching 'search' and 'retrieve'

optionExplanations.A: Simply listing servers in the mcp_servers configuration doesn't provide per-tool filtering; it configures which servers are connected, not which specific tools from a server are exposed.
optionExplanations.B: Adding filtering logic in the MCP server itself to only expose search and retrieve may be impossible to do for a third-party server you don't control, making it an impractical solution at the client level.
optionExplanations.C: tool_choice controls which tool Claude is forced or allowed to call in a given turn, not which tools are made available to the model from the server at all, so it doesn't restrict the exposed tool set.
optionExplanations.D: Using an mcp_toolset entry with an allowlist pattern matching 'search' and 'retrieve' is the connector-level mechanism that filters which server tools are exposed to the model, blocking all others as required.

text: A team wants their agent to think deeply on complex coding problems but skip extended thinking on simple file reads. On Claude 4.x, how should they configure this?

options.text: Set manual budget_tokens per request type based on estimated complexity
Set thinking enabled with a very large budget and let Claude self-limit
Implement client-side complexity detection that toggles thinking per request
Use adaptive thinking with an appropriate effort level — Claude decides when and how much to think

optionExplanations.A: Setting manual budget_tokens per request type based on estimated complexity is the deprecated, fixed-budget approach being replaced by adaptive thinking on Claude 4.x.
optionExplanations.B: Setting thinking enabled with a very large budget and letting Claude self-limit still relies on the older budget_tokens mechanism rather than the recommended adaptive effort-level approach.
optionExplanations.C: Implementing client-side complexity detection that toggles thinking per request adds brittle, custom complexity that duplicates what adaptive thinking is designed to handle automatically.
optionExplanations.D: Using adaptive thinking with an appropriate effort level lets Claude itself decide when and how much to think, thinking deeply on complex coding problems while skipping it on simple file reads, which is the recommended Claude 4.x approach replacing fixed budget_tokens.

text: The Edit tool fails because the search text matches multiple locations in a file. What is the recommended recovery using built-in tools?

options.text: Read the full file, modify the content, then Write the complete file back
Retry Edit with a more specific, uniquely-identifying pattern
Use Bash sed to replace only the first occurrence
Split the file into sections and edit each separately

optionExplanations.A: Reading the full file, modifying the content, then writing the complete file back is the documented fallback for non-unique Edit matches: load the file, make the change at the right spot, and write it back.
optionExplanations.B: Retrying Edit with a more specific, uniquely-identifying pattern often works too, but it isn't the stated documented fallback for this situation, even though it's a reasonable alternative.
optionExplanations.C: Using Bash sed to replace only the first occurrence brings shell-escaping risks and isn't the recommended built-in-tools recovery path.
optionExplanations.D: Splitting the file into sections and editing each separately is needlessly complex compared to the straightforward Read-then-Write fallback.

text: An order agent must always verify inventory before confirming an order but occasionally skips it when the customer seems confident. Prompt emphasis reduced but didn't eliminate the issue. Most reliable fix?

options.text: A PreToolUse hook that blocks confirm_order until check_inventory returned available in the session
Add all-caps warnings to the system prompt about the verification requirement
A dedicated verification subagent that runs before the order agent
Log orders that skip inventory checks and review them manually

optionExplanations.A: A PreToolUse hook that blocks confirm_order until check_inventory returned available in the session gives deterministic enforcement, ensuring the order can't be confirmed without verification, which is the most reliable fix after prompting alone failed.
optionExplanations.B: Adding all-caps warnings to the system prompt about the verification requirement is the same probabilistic prompting approach that already failed to eliminate the issue, just with more emphasis.
optionExplanations.C: A dedicated verification subagent that runs before the order agent doesn't guarantee ordering or enforcement on its own without a deterministic gate tying confirmation to verification.
optionExplanations.D: Logging orders that skip inventory checks and reviewing them manually only catches problems after they've already occurred, rather than preventing the skip in the first place.

text: A long agentic task burns excessive tokens because Claude produces detailed thinking and verbose tool plans even for simple operations. What reduces token use while keeping quality on complex work?

options.text: Switch everything from Opus to Haiku to cut per-token cost
Disable extended thinking entirely
Use effort='medium' as a baseline and raise to 'high' only for complex operations
Set a strict max_tokens of 1000 to force concision

optionExplanations.A: Switching everything from Opus to Haiku to cut per-token cost can hurt quality on the complex work that still needs to be done well, even though it reduces cost.
optionExplanations.B: Disabling extended thinking entirely would reduce tokens but hurts complex reasoning that genuinely benefits from thinking.
optionExplanations.C: Using effort='medium' as a baseline and raising to 'high' only for complex operations tunes thinking, tool-call, and verbosity behavior across the board, trimming waste on simple operations while preserving quality where 'high' effort is still needed.
optionExplanations.D: Setting a strict max_tokens of 1000 truncates output rather than actually reducing verbosity, risking cut-off responses instead of more concise ones.

text: An agent must: (1) verify identity, (2) look up the ticket, (3) check account status, then (4) process resolution. Steps 1–3 can run in any order but all must complete before step 4. Which orchestration pattern fits best?

options.text: Sequential pipeline executing 1, 2, 3 in fixed order, then 4
Prompt chaining with separate API calls and programmatic transitions
Run all four steps in parallel and reconcile
Model-driven ordering of 1–3, with a programmatic gate before step 4

optionExplanations.A: A sequential pipeline executing steps 1, 2, 3 in fixed order then step 4 over-constrains the process, since steps 1 through 3 can actually run in any order and don't need to be rigidly sequenced.
optionExplanations.B: Prompt chaining with separate API calls and programmatic transitions adds overhead and removes the adaptive ordering benefit, since steps 1-3 don't require a fixed sequence.
optionExplanations.C: Running all four steps in parallel and reconciling risks firing step 4 (process resolution) before its prerequisites (steps 1-3) have actually completed.
optionExplanations.D: Model-driven ordering of steps 1-3 lets Claude reason about the most efficient order for the unordered steps, while a programmatic gate before step 4 enforces the hard prerequisite that all three must complete first.

text: After compaction, specific numerical values from early in the conversation (balances, dates, transaction IDs) are lost in the summary. What design pattern prevents this?

options.text: Raise the compaction trigger threshold to delay summarization
Extract critical transactional facts into a persistent structured block that survives compaction
Use custom compaction instructions requesting preservation of numbers
Disable compaction and rely on larger context windows

optionExplanations.A: Raising the compaction trigger threshold to delay summarization only postpones when the loss occurs rather than preventing the eventual loss of specific values once compaction does happen.
optionExplanations.B: Extracting critical transactional facts into a persistent structured block that survives compaction reliably preserves exact values like balances, dates, and transaction IDs, since summaries inevitably blur such specifics.
optionExplanations.C: Using custom compaction instructions requesting preservation of numbers helps but is less reliable than maintaining a separate persistent facts block, since it still depends on the summarization process correctly following the instructions.
optionExplanations.D: Disabling compaction and relying on larger context windows just caps how long the conversation can run rather than solving the underlying problem of preserving specific facts.

text: A developer's personal experimental MCP servers from a previous project aren't available after cloning a repo (team servers work). Where should personal MCP servers be configured?

options.text: In the project's .mcp.json under a 'personal' section
In CLAUDE.md under a tools section
In a .mcp-personal.json alongside .mcp.json
In user-level config (e.g. ~/.claude.json), which isn't version-controlled

optionExplanations.A: Putting personal servers in a 'personal' section of the project's .mcp.json would push that configuration to everyone who clones the repo, since .mcp.json is the shared, version-controlled file.
optionExplanations.B: CLAUDE.md is meant for instructions to Claude, not for MCP server configuration, so it isn't the right place for personal server definitions.
optionExplanations.C: A separate .mcp-personal.json file alongside .mcp.json is non-standard and isn't the documented location for personal server configuration.
optionExplanations.D: User-level config such as ~/.claude.json is where personal MCP servers belong, since it isn't version-controlled and stays local to the developer's machine, unlike the project's shared .mcp.json used for team servers.

text: A Claude 4.x coding agent over-engineers — unnecessary helpers, unused parameters, abstractions for one-off operations. What is the most targeted fix?

options.text: Add explicit guidance in CLAUDE.md about minimal implementations and avoiding unnecessary abstractions
Switch to a less capable model that generates simpler code
Reduce effort to 'low' to prevent elaborate responses
Use plan mode for every change before implementation

optionExplanations.A: Adding explicit guidance in CLAUDE.md about minimal implementations and avoiding unnecessary abstractions directly counteracts the over-engineering tendency that recent Claude models can exhibit, making it the most targeted fix.
optionExplanations.B: Switching to a less capable model that generates simpler code trades away overall capability just to suppress over-engineering, which is a blunter trade-off than targeted guidance.
optionExplanations.C: Reducing effort to 'low' to prevent elaborate responses can hurt genuinely complex tasks that still require more thorough reasoning, making it too broad a fix.
optionExplanations.D: Using plan mode for every change before implementation adds overhead to all work, including simple changes that don't have an over-engineering problem.

text: A synthesis subagent occasionally returns lengthy reasoning chains alongside its findings, consuming excessive coordinator context. What architectural change best addresses this?

options.text: Set a max_tokens limit on subagent responses
Add instructions telling the subagent to be concise
Use the Explore subagent for all research tasks
Have subagents return structured data separated from reasoning, with reasoning excluded from the coordinator's context

optionExplanations.A: Setting a max_tokens limit on subagent responses risks truncating real findings along with the unwanted reasoning chains, since it cuts off output indiscriminately.
optionExplanations.B: Adding instructions telling the subagent to be concise is an unreliable, probabilistic fix that doesn't guarantee the reasoning chains will actually be excluded.
optionExplanations.C: Using the Explore subagent for all research tasks would replace specialized subagents and lose the value they provide for synthesis-specific work, rather than addressing the verbosity issue directly.
optionExplanations.D: Having subagents return structured data separated from reasoning, with reasoning excluded from the coordinator's context, lets the coordinator consume only what it actually needs, directly addressing the excessive context consumption.

text: An agentic loop hits stop_reason: 'max_tokens' after a complex analysis request. What does this mean and what should you do?

options.text: Claude finished — treat it the same as 'end_turn'
The context window is full — start a new conversation
Claude wants a tool but was cut off — resume the tool call
The output was truncated at the max_tokens limit — increase max_tokens or handle the partial response

optionExplanations.A: stop_reason 'max_tokens' should not be treated the same as 'end_turn', since 'end_turn' indicates a complete response while 'max_tokens' indicates the output was cut off incomplete.
optionExplanations.B: 'max_tokens' reflects an output cap being hit, not the input context window being full, so it doesn't mean you need to start an entirely new conversation.
optionExplanations.C: 'max_tokens' is distinct from a 'tool_use' stop_reason; it isn't specifically about Claude wanting to make a tool call that got cut off, but about the overall output limit being reached.
optionExplanations.D: 'max_tokens' means the output was truncated at the configured limit and is incomplete, so you should increase max_tokens (or stream for large outputs) or otherwise handle the partial response.

text: After adding an 'other' enum value to fix forced misclassification, Claude now overuses 'other' (25% of entities). What additional schema element is needed?

options.text: A detail string paired with 'other' that captures the specific description, enabling later analysis
More detailed descriptions for each existing enum value
A confidence_score field to filter low-confidence 'other' classifications
A second validation API call to reclassify 'other' entries

optionExplanations.A: A detail string paired with 'other' that captures the specific description forces the model to justify the choice, which reduces lazy overuse of 'other' and captures what the real category actually was for later analysis or schema expansion.
optionExplanations.B: More detailed descriptions for each existing enum value can help matching against the known categories but don't address the need to capture what the misclassified-as-'other' entities actually are.
optionExplanations.C: A confidence_score field to filter low-confidence 'other' classifications adds cost without improving the initial classification decision or capturing the actual category information.
optionExplanations.D: A second validation API call to reclassify 'other' entries adds cost without addressing why the model is overusing 'other' in the first place.

text: To find entry points in an unfamiliar codebase using Claude Code's built-in tools, which should be used first?

options.text: Read on common entry files like main.ts, index.ts, app.ts
Bash to run 'find . -name "*.ts" | head -50'
Glob to find files matching entry-point patterns, then Grep for exports/imports
Write an exploration script that maps the structure

optionExplanations.A: Reading specific common entry files like main.ts, index.ts, or app.ts directly is hit-or-miss, since you don't yet know which files actually exist or serve as entry points in this unfamiliar codebase.
optionExplanations.B: Using Bash to run a raw find command is less integrated with Claude Code's built-in search tooling, which is purpose-built for this kind of pattern-based discovery.
optionExplanations.C: Using Glob to find files matching entry-point patterns, then Grep to search their content for exports/imports, lets you navigate efficiently and build understanding incrementally, which is why it should be used first.
optionExplanations.D: Writing a custom exploration script to map the structure creates unnecessary artifacts in the codebase rather than using the lightweight built-in discovery tools.

text: Choosing between Claude's structured outputs (output_config.format) and tool_use with a JSON schema for a pipeline that must always return exactly one extraction result per document — which is more appropriate?

options.text: tool_use with tool_choice 'any' to guarantee a tool call even for unknown document types
Neither — use prompt instructions to request JSON and parse the response
Both concatenated — tool_use for extraction, then output_config for final formatting
output_config.format with a json_schema — response-level structured output regardless of content

optionExplanations.A: Using tool_use with tool_choice 'any' guarantees a tool call even for unknown document types, but this operates at the tool-call level rather than providing the more direct, response-level guarantee that output_config.format offers.
optionExplanations.B: Relying on neither feature and instead using prompt instructions with manual parsing is probabilistic and doesn't guarantee the structured single result per document that's required here.
optionExplanations.C: Concatenating both approaches, using tool_use for extraction and then output_config for final formatting, is needless complexity when output_config.format alone can directly guarantee the structured result.
optionExplanations.D: output_config.format with a json_schema provides response-level structured output regardless of content, which is the most direct, guaranteed way to ensure exactly one structured extraction result per document.

text: Aggregate accuracy across document types is 96%, but contracts are 99% and invoices only 78%. The evaluation reports only aggregate metrics. What change is needed?

options.text: Increase total sample size for statistical significance
Add regression tests comparing each extraction to a golden dataset
Implement stratified sampling measuring accuracy by document type AND by field
Focus evaluation resources on invoices since they're lowest

optionExplanations.A: Increasing total sample size improves statistical significance of the existing aggregate metric but doesn't reveal the breakdown between document types that's actually needed to find the problem.
optionExplanations.B: Adding regression tests comparing each extraction to a golden dataset checks fixed cases but doesn't reveal the systematic, type-level patterns behind why invoices specifically underperform.
optionExplanations.C: Implementing stratified sampling that measures accuracy by document type and by field exposes exactly where the 78% accuracy concentrates, likely in specific invoice fields, so fixes can be targeted appropriately.
optionExplanations.D: Focusing evaluation resources only on invoices since they're lowest may miss issues elsewhere and doesn't establish the systematic, stratified visibility needed across all document types and fields.

text: A team needs Python-file conventions to apply whenever editing any .py file, regardless of directory, replacing a monolithic root CLAUDE.md. Most targeted approach?

options.text: A subdirectory CLAUDE.md in each directory that has Python files
A Python section in the root CLAUDE.md with headers
A skill in .claude/skills/ for Python development
A rule file scoped with paths like ['**/*.py'] in its frontmatter

optionExplanations.A: Creating a subdirectory CLAUDE.md in each directory that has Python files requires duplicating the same conventions across many directories, which is exactly the redundancy being avoided.
optionExplanations.B: Adding a Python section in the root CLAUDE.md with headers still loads for all file types, not just when editing Python files, defeating the goal of targeted, conditional loading.
optionExplanations.C: Creating a skill in .claude/skills/ for Python development requires developers to manually invoke it, so it won't automatically apply whenever a .py file is being edited.
optionExplanations.D: A rule file scoped with paths like ['**/*.py'] in its frontmatter loads Python conventions only when a Python file is being edited, anywhere in the repo, without duplicating content or polluting non-Python contexts.

text: An MCP tool returns customer data plus internal DB IDs, audit timestamps, and query metrics; the agent sometimes leaks internal IDs into customer-facing replies. What should change?

options.text: Post-process responses to strip internal IDs before sending to customers
Redesign the tool to return only high-signal information with semantic, stable identifiers relevant to the task
Instruct Claude in the system prompt to never reference internal IDs
Create two tools: one returning internal data for logging, one returning customer-facing data

optionExplanations.A: Post-processing responses to strip internal IDs before sending to customers treats the symptom after the fact rather than fixing why the irrelevant internal data was returned in the first place.
optionExplanations.B: Redesigning the tool to return only high-signal information with semantic, stable identifiers relevant to the task removes the irrelevant internal data the model might surface, fixing the root cause of the leakage.
optionExplanations.C: Instructing Claude in the system prompt to never reference internal IDs is a probabilistic rule that may not reliably prevent leakage in every response.
optionExplanations.D: Creating two separate tools, one for internal logging data and one for customer-facing data, over-complicates what is fundamentally a payload-shaping problem that a single well-designed tool response can solve.

text: Which command generates an initial CLAUDE.md capturing build commands, testing framework, code style, and directory structure for a new project?

options.text: /config init
/setup
/memory create
/init — analyzes the project and generates the initial CLAUDE.md

optionExplanations.A: /config init is not the command that analyzes a project and generates a starter CLAUDE.md; it isn't the documented initializer.
optionExplanations.B: /setup is not a real Claude Code command for generating the initial CLAUDE.md from project analysis.
optionExplanations.C: /memory create is not the command that inspects the codebase to build the initial CLAUDE.md; memory commands relate to the separate auto-memory feature, not project initialization.
optionExplanations.D: /init inspects the repository and produces a starter CLAUDE.md capturing build commands, testing framework, code style, and directory structure, making it the correct initializer command.

text: After server-side compaction with custom instructions, critical technical decisions are summarized too tersely and lose their rationale. What configuration change addresses this?

options.text: Increase the trigger threshold to delay compaction
Provide custom compaction instructions that explicitly require preserving technical decisions, rationale, and architectural choices
Disable compaction and manually remove non-essential messages
Use pause-after-compaction to review and supplement each summary

optionExplanations.A: Increasing the trigger threshold to delay compaction postpones when summarization happens but doesn't improve the quality or completeness of the summary once compaction does occur.
optionExplanations.B: Providing custom compaction instructions that explicitly require preserving technical decisions, rationale, and architectural choices directly directs the summarizer to retain exactly the content that was being lost too tersely.
optionExplanations.C: Disabling compaction and manually removing non-essential messages gives up the benefit of automatic context management entirely, rather than improving how compaction itself summarizes.
optionExplanations.D: Using pause-after-compaction to review and supplement each summary patches the problem afterward rather than fixing the underlying quality of the summary the compaction process produces.

text: A support issue could stem from three different root causes; the agent investigates one at a time and often spends all context on a wrong first hypothesis. What orchestration improvement helps?

options.text: Spawn parallel subagents to investigate each hypothesis simultaneously; the coordinator evaluates all results
Create a fixed diagnostic decision tree to follow
Instruct the agent to check the most common cause first
Increase the context window to allow sequential investigation of all three

optionExplanations.A: Spawning parallel subagents to investigate each of the three hypotheses simultaneously avoids exhausting context on a single wrong path, and lets the coordinator evaluate and compare all results to determine the actual root cause.
optionExplanations.B: Creating a fixed diagnostic decision tree to follow is too rigid, since it forces a predetermined order regardless of which hypothesis is actually most likely for a given case.
optionExplanations.C: Instructing the agent to check the most common cause first is only a statistical tweak rather than a structural fix, and the agent can still spend all its context on a wrong hypothesis if the common cause turns out not to apply.
optionExplanations.D: Increasing the context window to allow sequential investigation of all three addresses the symptom of running out of context rather than fixing the inefficiency of investigating one hypothesis at a time.

text: Where is a custom Claude Code subagent (e.g. for SQL tasks, limited to Read/Bash/Grep with a plan permission mode) defined?

options.text: In CLAUDE.md under a ## Subagents section
In a .claude/agents/ definition file with frontmatter for tools, disallowedTools, and permissionMode
Both B and C are valid
Only via a JSON config passed with a --agents CLI flag

optionExplanations.A: CLAUDE.md is used for instructions to Claude, not for defining subagents, so a ## Subagents section there isn't how custom subagents are actually configured.
optionExplanations.B: Custom subagents are defined as files under .claude/agents/ with frontmatter specifying allowed and disallowed tools (like Read/Bash/Grep), permission mode (like plan), and model, which is the documented mechanism for the SQL-task subagent described.
optionExplanations.C: The 'Both B and C' option is self-referential and incoherent since C refers back to itself, making it not a valid or meaningful answer.
optionExplanations.D: Subagents are not defined only via a JSON config passed with a --agents CLI flag; the standard, documented mechanism is the .claude/agents/ definition file.

text: Combining prompt caching with compaction: after compaction creates a summary block, subsequent requests show no cache hits even though the system prompt is unchanged. Why?

options.text: Compaction is incompatible with prompt caching
The compaction block itself needs a cache_control breakpoint to establish a new cacheable base for subsequent requests
The compaction block is below the minimum cacheable threshold
The cache TTL expired during compaction processing

optionExplanations.A: Compaction is not incompatible with prompt caching; they can work together, it's just that the cached prefix needs to be re-established after compaction changes the content.
optionExplanations.B: The compaction block itself needs a cache_control breakpoint placed on it to establish a new cacheable base for subsequent requests, since the content before the summary block is dropped and the old prefix no longer matches.
optionExplanations.C: The compaction block being below the minimum cacheable threshold doesn't explain a hit-rate drop specifically tied to compaction occurring, since size alone isn't indicated as the issue.
optionExplanations.D: Cache TTL expiring during compaction processing doesn't specifically explain why cache hits drop right after compaction when the system prompt itself is unchanged; the real issue is the changed prefix needing a new breakpoint.

text: A team wants the Claude Agent SDK to use a private AWS Bedrock deployment instead of the default API. How should they configure the SDK?

options.text: Set CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1 and configure AWS credentials
Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to a Bedrock access key and let the SDK auto-detect
Pass the Bedrock endpoint URL to query()
Use a custom HTTP client that proxies to Bedrock

optionExplanations.A: Setting CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1 and configuring standard AWS credentials is how the SDK selects the Bedrock provider via environment variables, which is the documented mechanism (with similar flags existing for Vertex and Foundry).
optionExplanations.B: Setting ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to a Bedrock access key and expecting auto-detection doesn't work, since an API key isn't a valid Bedrock credential and the SDK doesn't auto-detect Bedrock this way.
optionExplanations.C: There is no endpoint URL parameter on query() for configuring Bedrock; provider selection happens through environment variables, not a per-call endpoint argument.
optionExplanations.D: Using a custom HTTP client that proxies to Bedrock bypasses the SDK's built-in provider support rather than using the supported configuration mechanism.

text: An agent must reliably produce a report with exactly: executive summary, methodology, findings (array), recommendations. What is the most robust approach on Claude 4.x?

options.text: Detailed system-prompt instructions describing the structure
Few-shot examples of correctly structured reports
A tool with the report structure as its JSON schema, forced via tool_choice
output_config with a json_schema and strict mode (or a strict tool)

optionExplanations.A: Detailed system-prompt instructions describing the structure are probabilistic and don't provide a hard guarantee that the report will always contain exactly the required sections.
optionExplanations.B: Few-shot examples of correctly structured reports also remain probabilistic, improving but not guaranteeing strict adherence to the exact required structure.
optionExplanations.C: A forced tool with the report structure as its JSON schema, forced via tool_choice, is a strong guarantee but operates at the tool-call level rather than constraining the response itself.
optionExplanations.D: output_config with a json_schema and strict mode (or a strict tool) constrains generation to the schema at the grammar level, which is the strongest guarantee for reliably producing the exact report structure on Claude 4.x.

text: A team wants Claude Code to always read a file before modifying it, but only when the file already exists. Most appropriate solution?

options.text: Use plan mode for all file operations
A PreToolUse hook on Write/Edit that blocks edits without a prior Read of an existing file
Add 'always read files before editing them' to the project CLAUDE.md
Set --permission-mode to require manual approval for every file change

optionExplanations.A: Using plan mode for all file operations adds friction to every operation, which is disproportionate for a convention that isn't a safety-critical, irreversible control.
optionExplanations.B: A PreToolUse hook that blocks edits without a prior Read is a valid mechanism but over-engineers what is a useful convention rather than a safety-critical control with irreversible consequences.
optionExplanations.C: Adding 'always read files before editing them' to the project CLAUDE.md is the proportionate fix, since read-before-edit is a convention reliably followed through instructions rather than a control requiring deterministic enforcement, unlike safety-critical operations such as financial controls.
optionExplanations.D: Setting --permission-mode to require manual approval for every file change adds friction to all file operations, not just the specific read-before-edit convention being addressed.

text: Processing 5,000 documents via the Message Batches API where each needs extraction then a follow-up validation call referencing the extraction. Can this two-step workflow be done in a single batch request per document?

options.text: Yes — use multi-turn messages within each batch request to chain extraction and validation
Yes — include both tool definitions and Claude calls them sequentially
No — Batches doesn't support multi-turn tool calling; run extraction as one batch, then validation as a second batch
No — each batch request is capped at 1,000 tokens

optionExplanations.A: Multi-turn messages within a single batch request to chain extraction and validation aren't supported, since Batches assumes multi-turn interactive support that isn't actually there.
optionExplanations.B: Including both tool definitions and expecting Claude to call them sequentially within one batch request also assumes multi-turn tool-calling support that the Batches API doesn't provide.
optionExplanations.C: Batches doesn't support multi-turn tool calling since each batch request is a single independent completion, so the dependent two-step workflow needs two separate batches: extraction first, then validation referencing those results.
optionExplanations.D: A 1,000-token cap per batch request is fabricated and not an actual limitation of the Message Batches API.

text: A customer is increasingly frustrated; the agent can resolve the technical issue. The system currently escalates on negative sentiment. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Escalate to a human immediately based on the negative sentiment
Acknowledge the frustration while offering resolution within the agent's capability
Ignore the sentiment, resolve the issue, and confirm resolution
Ask if they'd like a manager before attempting resolution

optionExplanations.A: Escalating to a human immediately based on negative sentiment alone is unnecessary when the agent is actually capable of resolving the technical issue itself.
optionExplanations.B: Acknowledging the frustration while offering resolution within the agent's capability is correct, since escalation should be triggered by explicit requests, policy gaps, or inability to progress, not sentiment alone, and validating the emotion while resolving the issue best serves the customer.
optionExplanations.C: Ignoring the sentiment entirely and just resolving the issue without any acknowledgment harms the customer experience by failing to validate their frustration.
optionExplanations.D: Asking if they'd like a manager before attempting resolution unnecessarily delays a resolution that is actually within the agent's reach.

text: With server-side compaction, billing calculations come out lower than actual usage. Where is the complete token usage tracked?

options.text: In a usage.iterations array that separately tracks compaction-iteration and message-iteration tokens
In a separate /v1/usage endpoint that aggregates all consumption
In the top-level usage.input_tokens / output_tokens fields
In an X-Token-Usage response header

optionExplanations.A: A usage.iterations array that separately tracks compaction-iteration and message-iteration tokens contains the complete picture of token usage, including compaction iterations, which must be summed to get the full count.
optionExplanations.B: There is no separate /v1/usage endpoint that aggregates all consumption; this isn't how complete token usage is exposed with server-side compaction.
optionExplanations.C: The top-level usage.input_tokens / output_tokens fields cover only the final attempt and under-count total usage by omitting the tokens consumed during compaction iterations.
optionExplanations.D: There is no X-Token-Usage response header that exposes complete usage; this isn't the documented mechanism for tracking tokens across compaction iterations.

text: A coordinator spawns three subagents in parallel; two succeed and the third times out while partially completing. The coordinator currently discards the failed subagent's entire output. What is the improved error handling?

options.text: Proceed with only the two successful results and note the gap
Retry the failed subagent with the same parameters until success or a limit
Accept the partial results plus structured error context, and evaluate whether to re-delegate the remaining scope
Terminate the entire workflow since incomplete results may be inaccurate

optionExplanations.A: Proceeding with only the two successful results discards the partially completed work from the timed-out subagent, losing usable progress that could have informed re-delegation.
optionExplanations.B: Retrying the same subagent with identical parameters can burn resources indefinitely on a persistent failure rather than addressing what actually went wrong.
optionExplanations.C: Keeping the partial results alongside structured error context lets the coordinator see exactly what's missing and decide whether to re-delegate just that remaining scope, wasting none of the completed work.
optionExplanations.D: Terminating the entire workflow over one subagent's partial failure is the anti-pattern of failing everything because of a single error, even though two-thirds of the work succeeded.

text: A skill is invoked as /migrate users_table. Inside the skill body, which substitution variables hold that argument?

options.text: $PARAMS['migration_name'] based on argument-hint
${input}, with manual parsing required
$ARGUMENTS holds 'users_table', and $1 holds it as the first positional argument
process.argv[2] passed through to the shell

optionExplanations.A: There is no $PARAMS substitution mechanism tied to argument-hint for skills — this references a variable that doesn't exist.
optionExplanations.B: There is no ${input} substitution requiring manual parsing in skill bodies; this is not a real mechanism for skills.
optionExplanations.C: Skill/command bodies expose $ARGUMENTS, which holds the full argument string ('users_table'), and positional variables like $1, which holds the first positional argument — both correctly capture the invocation argument.
optionExplanations.D: process.argv[2] is a Node.js construct, not a substitution variable available inside a skill body; skills don't pass arguments through the shell this way.

text: Implementing prompt chaining for a 4-step pipeline (extract → normalize → analyze → report) where you want to inspect intermediate outputs. What is the key advantage over a single-prompt approach?

options.text: Each step can use a different model optimized for that task
It enables parallel execution of all four steps
It reduces total token consumption vs. one long prompt
It provides deterministic step ordering with inspectable intermediate outputs at each stage

optionExplanations.A: Using a different model per step is possible with prompt chaining, but it's a secondary, optional benefit rather than the defining advantage of the pattern.
optionExplanations.B: The four steps (extract, normalize, analyze, report) are dependent on each other's outputs, so they cannot run in parallel — chaining does not enable this.
optionExplanations.C: Chaining typically increases total token consumption versus a single prompt, because context tends to be repeated across steps, so this is not an advantage.
optionExplanations.D: The defining benefit of prompt chaining is guaranteed sequential ordering combined with the ability to inspect and validate each step's intermediate output before moving to the next stage.

text: An agent skips customer verification before refunds in 5% of cases. Adding 'THIS IS CRITICAL — NEVER skip verification' reduced it to 2%. What does this pattern indicate about the design?

options.text: Prompt-based enforcement has a non-zero failure rate for financial-consequence compliance, and programmatic enforcement (hooks) should replace it
The prompt needs even stronger language to reach 0%
A 2% failure rate is acceptable for most production systems
The tool execution order should be hardcoded rather than left to the model

optionExplanations.A: Emphatic prompt language reduced but did not eliminate the failure rate, proving prompt-based control cannot guarantee compliance for financially consequential operations — this calls for deterministic enforcement via hooks instead.
optionExplanations.B: Stronger prompt language can push the failure rate down further (as seen going from 5% to 2%), but it approaches zero without ever reliably reaching it, since prompts remain probabilistic.
optionExplanations.C: A 2% failure rate is not acceptable given the financial consequences of skipped verification before refunds; tolerating it ignores real risk.
optionExplanations.D: Hardcoding tool execution order removes flexibility the model may need and doesn't specifically gate the verification step that's failing — it's the wrong lever for this problem.

text: An extraction system handles both structured (amounts, dates) and free-text fields. What validation approach is most effective?

options.text: Schema validation alone, since tool_use with JSON schemas eliminates all extraction errors
Use strict mode to handle both structural and semantic validation
Skip schema validation and rely entirely on post-processing
Schema validation for structural errors; separate programmatic checks for semantic validity (e.g., values summing, dates in range)

optionExplanations.A: Schema validation with tool_use and JSON schemas guarantees structure (types, required fields) but does not eliminate semantic errors like incorrect sums or implausible dates, so it does not eliminate all extraction errors.
optionExplanations.B: Strict mode enforces structural correctness but does not perform semantic validation such as checking that amounts sum correctly or dates fall in a plausible range.
optionExplanations.C: Skipping schema validation discards a real, free benefit (structural guarantees) and forces post-processing to catch problems schema validation would have caught for free.
optionExplanations.D: Schema validation correctly handles structural errors (types, required fields), while separate programmatic checks (e.g., verifying amounts sum correctly or dates are in range) are needed for semantic validity — combining both addresses what each handles well.

text: To trace how a specific function is used across files (through wrappers and re-exports), which built-in tool sequence is most effective?

options.text: Grep to find the definition → Read to understand the signature → Grep again to find imports/usages across the codebase
Glob all TypeScript files → Read each to scan for the name
Bash 'grep -r functionName .' across the codebase
Read the entry point → manually trace each import chain

optionExplanations.A: Using Grep to find the definition, Read to understand its signature, then Grep again to trace imports and usages builds understanding incrementally and targets only relevant files, making it the most effective sequence for tracing usage through wrappers and re-exports.
optionExplanations.B: Globbing all TypeScript files and reading each one to scan for the name is wasteful, since it requires opening many irrelevant files instead of targeting the ones that matter.
optionExplanations.C: A raw recursive Bash grep across the codebase works but is less integrated with the agent's reasoning process compared to using the dedicated Grep and Read tools in sequence.
optionExplanations.D: Manually tracing each import chain from the entry point risks missing indirect references such as wrappers and re-exports, which is exactly the scenario described.

text: An MCP server has 12 tools; 6 are management analytics irrelevant during customer interactions, and the agent sometimes calls them in customer sessions. Best solution?

options.text: Add system-prompt instructions about which tools to use
Rename analytics tools with an 'mgmt_' prefix
Use tool_choice to restrict available tools during customer sessions
Create separate MCP configurations — one scoped for support agents, one for analytics agents

optionExplanations.A: System-prompt instructions about which tools to use rely on probabilistic compliance, so the agent could still call analytics tools during customer sessions despite the guidance.
optionExplanations.B: Renaming analytics tools with an 'mgmt_' prefix is just a naming convention and still relies on the model choosing correctly rather than structurally preventing access.
optionExplanations.C: tool_choice forces the use of a single specific tool rather than restricting the agent to a role-appropriate subset of multiple tools, so it doesn't fit this scenario of excluding 6 of 12 tools.
optionExplanations.D: Creating separate MCP configurations scoped to each role means support agents structurally never see the analytics tools at all, enforcing the boundary rather than relying on the model to comply with instructions.

text: A team needs a /deploy command available to all developers via version control and a /personal-test command only for one developer. Where should each go?

options.text: Both in .claude/commands/ — personal ones marked with a 'personal: true' frontmatter field
/deploy in .claude/commands/ (project-scoped); /personal-test in ~/.claude/commands/ (user-scoped)
/deploy in .claude/skills/; /personal-test in .claude/commands/
Both in ~/.claude/commands/ — project commands deployed via a setup script

optionExplanations.A: There is no 'personal: true' frontmatter flag in .claude/commands/ that changes a command's sharing scope — this mechanism doesn't exist.
optionExplanations.B: Project-scoped commands placed in .claude/commands/ are committed to version control and shared with all developers, while user-scoped commands in ~/.claude/commands/ are personal and uncommitted — exactly matching /deploy needing to be shared and /personal-test needing to stay individual.
optionExplanations.C: Skills (.claude/skills/) are a different construct from commands, so putting /deploy there doesn't achieve version-controlled command sharing as intended.
optionExplanations.D: Placing both commands at the user level means /deploy is never committed to version control and isn't automatically shared with the team, defeating the requirement.

text: A financial-compliance system must ensure identity verification, KYC check, and risk assessment all complete before any transaction. Prompt instructions gave a 3% failure rate. Which hook strategy provides comprehensive enforcement?

options.text: PostToolUse hooks on the three prerequisites that set flags, plus a PreToolUse hook on process_transaction checking all flags
Three separate PreToolUse hooks, each independently blocking process_transaction
A single PreToolUse hook on process_transaction that verifies all three prerequisites completed in the session
A SessionStart hook making the compliance workflow mandatory for the session

optionExplanations.A: Setting flags via PostToolUse hooks on the three prerequisites plus a PreToolUse check adds needless complexity compared to a single consolidated check.
optionExplanations.B: Three separate PreToolUse hooks, each independently blocking process_transaction, splits the logic unnecessarily and adds complexity without being more comprehensive than a single combined check.
optionExplanations.C: A single PreToolUse hook on process_transaction that verifies all three prerequisites (identity verification, KYC check, risk assessment) completed in the session is the cleanest, most maintainable deterministic gate, replacing the unreliable 3% failure rate of prompt instructions.
optionExplanations.D: A SessionStart hook can make the workflow mandatory in principle but can't evaluate per-call prerequisites or enforce that all three specific checks actually completed before each transaction attempt.

text: A retry-with-error-feedback extractor fixes formatting errors well, but has no effect on a recurring issue where Claude pulls the methodology from the introduction instead of the methods section. Why aren't retries effective here?

options.text: Retries are capped at 3 by default, which isn't enough
Retries with error feedback fix structural/format errors, but this is a model-interpretation issue requiring better prompting (e.g., explicit section markers)
The retry approach works but needs higher effort to read more carefully
The error feedback lacks detail about the methodology's location

optionExplanations.A: The retry cap isn't the underlying issue — more retries wouldn't fix a problem rooted in misinterpreting which section contains the methodology.
optionExplanations.B: Retries with error feedback are effective at fixing structural or formatting errors, but pulling methodology from the wrong section is a model-interpretation issue that requires better prompting, such as explicit section markers, not more retries.
optionExplanations.C: Higher effort doesn't address the core problem, which is that the model is misinterpreting which section to read from, not simply reading carelessly.
optionExplanations.D: More detail in the error feedback would only marginally help, since the real fix is clarifying the prompt about section boundaries, not describing the error more thoroughly.

text: An agent handling English and Spanish has a cache breakpoint on the system prompt. Switching languages drops cache hits significantly. Most likely cause?

options.text: Prompt caching doesn't support multilingual content
Spanish tokens are larger and exceed the cache size limit
The system prompt changes between languages, invalidating the system-level cache and everything below it
The 5-minute TTL expires between language switches

optionExplanations.A: Prompt caching is language-agnostic and works regardless of the language used — it doesn't have a limitation specific to multilingual content.
optionExplanations.B: Cache invalidation isn't caused by token size exceeding some limit; the cache is invalidated due to a prefix change, not a size constraint tied to Spanish tokens.
optionExplanations.C: Since the system prompt contains language-specific instructions, switching languages changes the system-layer prefix, which invalidates the system-level cache and everything below it in the cache hierarchy — directly explaining the drop in cache hits.
optionExplanations.D: A 5-minute TTL expiring wouldn't correlate specifically with language switches; the drop happens because the prompt content itself changes, not because of elapsed time.

text: A tool must always output exactly {"status": "success"|"error", "data": object, "metadata": object} with no exceptions. On Claude 4.x, which gives the absolute strongest guarantee?

options.text: output_config.format with json_schema and strict mode and the specified schema
A tool definition with the schema, forced via tool_choice
A system prompt with an explicit JSON template and instructions
Few-shot examples of correct JSON responses

optionExplanations.A: output_config.format with json_schema and strict mode constrains generation to the schema at the response level, providing the strongest possible guarantee that the output always matches the required structure.
optionExplanations.B: Forcing a tool definition via tool_choice also yields schema-valid output, but only at the tool-call level, which is a slightly different and less direct mechanism than constraining the response format itself.
optionExplanations.C: A system prompt with a JSON template and instructions is probabilistic — the model can still deviate from the requested format despite the instructions.
optionExplanations.D: Few-shot examples of correct JSON responses are also probabilistic, demonstrating the desired format without strictly enforcing it.

text: An agentic system makes 100 API calls/minute with stable tool definitions and system prompt, using a single top-level automatic cache_control. To maximize cache hits, what improvement should they make?

options.text: Increase the cache TTL to 1 hour for maximum duration
Use explicit caching with up to 4 breakpoints placed on the last stable content blocks
Remove all caching to reduce per-request overhead
Add cache_control to every content block for maximum granularity

optionExplanations.A: Increasing the cache TTL to 1 hour costs more to write the cache and isn't necessary for a high-frequency workload like 100 calls per minute, where the default short TTL is already sufficient to maintain hits.
optionExplanations.B: Using explicit caching with up to 4 breakpoints placed on the last stable content blocks (tool definitions, system prompt, early stable turns) maximizes the cached prefix, improving on a single automatic breakpoint that may not be optimally placed.
optionExplanations.C: Removing all caching eliminates the cost savings caching provides and would increase costs, not improve cache hit efficiency.
optionExplanations.D: Adding cache_control to every content block isn't how breakpoints work — only up to 4 breakpoints are supported, so this granularity isn't achievable or meaningful.

text: A team wants to test a new prompt strategy without modifying the production CLAUDE.md — temporary instructions that apply only during testing. Best approach?

options.text: Create a temporary branch and modify the project CLAUDE.md
Add a .claude/rules/testing.md with a feature flag
Modify ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, since user-level overrides project-level
Use --append-system-prompt with the testing instructions when invoking Claude Code

optionExplanations.A: Creating a temporary branch to modify the project CLAUDE.md is heavyweight overhead for a transient testing change and still risks the modification leaking into production if not carefully managed.
optionExplanations.B: Adding a .claude/rules/testing.md file still means editing committed configuration, which doesn't meet the requirement of not touching the production CLAUDE.md or other config files.
optionExplanations.C: Modifying ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md affects all projects for that user, not just the one being tested, and it doesn't simply 'override' project-level instructions as implied — it's not a precise or appropriately scoped solution.
optionExplanations.D: --append-system-prompt adds instructions only for that specific invocation without touching any configuration files, making it the ideal way to apply temporary test-only instructions.

text: A team wants a custom 'reviewer' subagent to use Sonnet (while the main agent uses Opus and Explore uses Haiku). How do they specify the reviewer's model?

options.text: In the reviewer's SKILL.md frontmatter via a 'model' field
Via environment variables specifying the model per subagent
In the reviewer's agent-definition frontmatter via a 'model' field
Models can't be configured per subagent — all agents use the same model

optionExplanations.A: SKILL.md frontmatter is for skills, a different construct from subagents, and doesn't control which model a subagent uses.
optionExplanations.B: Environment variables are not the mechanism used to specify a model per subagent.
optionExplanations.C: Custom subagent definitions support a 'model' field directly in their agent-definition frontmatter, allowing the reviewer subagent to be set to Sonnet independently of the main agent's Opus or Explore's Haiku.
optionExplanations.D: Models can in fact be configured per subagent via the frontmatter 'model' field — this option incorrectly claims no such configuration exists, as demonstrated by Explore using Haiku.

text: A document-search MCP tool takes a query plus optional filters (date range, type, author). Claude often omits the optional filters even when the user clearly mentions them. What helps?

options.text: Make all filter parameters required
Add input examples showing queries that correctly use the optional filters alongside the query
Add a system-prompt note listing all filter parameters
Split into separate tools for simple vs. filtered search

optionExplanations.A: Making all filter parameters required forces them to be populated even on simple queries where the user didn't mention any filters, which is not appropriate.
optionExplanations.B: Adding input examples that show queries correctly using the optional filters alongside the main query teaches Claude when and how to populate them, directly addressing the omission problem.
optionExplanations.C: A system-prompt note listing the filter parameters only describes them without demonstrating concrete usage, so it's less effective at teaching when to apply them.
optionExplanations.D: Splitting into separate tools for simple versus filtered search causes tool proliferation, which can degrade tool selection rather than solve the omission issue.

text: A complex refactor spans deep analysis, new-architecture design, and implementation — each phase may approach context limits. What multi-context-window approach is recommended?

options.text: Use the first window for framework and analysis, then start new windows for iteration, carrying forward only essential findings
Complete all phases in one window, using /compact between phases
Three entirely independent sessions with no shared context
Fork the session for each phase from a shared base

optionExplanations.A: Using the first window to establish the framework and analysis, then starting new windows for each subsequent phase while carrying forward only the essential findings, manages context limits while preserving coherence across the deep analysis, design, and implementation phases.
optionExplanations.B: Completing all phases in one window using /compact between phases risks degradation as the single window accumulates content across multiple complex phases.
optionExplanations.C: Three entirely independent sessions with no shared context would lose the cross-phase continuity needed, since later phases depend on findings from earlier ones.
optionExplanations.D: Forking the session for each phase from a shared base shares an initial context but doesn't structure the recommended approach of carrying forward only the essential findings into fresh windows.

text: A developer configured MCP servers with ${GITHUB_TOKEN} expansion in .mcp.json. On a teammate's machine the server fails to connect, though the .mcp.json is identical. Most likely cause?

options.text: A different MCP protocol version on the new machine
.mcp.json must be regenerated per machine
GITHUB_TOKEN isn't set in the teammate's local environment
Env-var expansion only works in user-level config, not .mcp.json

optionExplanations.A: A protocol version mismatch is unlikely to be the cause here, since the .mcp.json configuration is confirmed identical between machines.
optionExplanations.B: .mcp.json doesn't need to be regenerated per machine — the file itself is shared and identical, so this isn't the issue.
optionExplanations.C: ${GITHUB_TOKEN} resolves to a local environment variable; if the teammate hasn't set GITHUB_TOKEN in their own environment, the expansion fails and the server connection breaks, even though the .mcp.json file is identical.
optionExplanations.D: .mcp.json does support environment-variable expansion; this isn't a limitation restricted to user-level config only.

text: A team writes tests first, then has Claude implement code to pass them, iterating by sharing test failures. Which workflow pattern is this?

options.text: The test-driven iteration pattern — write tests first, iterate by sharing failures
Plan mode followed by direct execution
The interview pattern — Claude asks questions before implementing
Prompt chaining with separate testing and implementation steps

optionExplanations.A: Writing tests first and then having Claude implement code to pass them, iterating by sharing test failures, is the test-driven iteration pattern — it uses the tests as concrete, unambiguous specifications to drive implementation.
optionExplanations.B: Plan mode followed by direct execution is a different interaction mode, not one built around writing tests first and iterating on failures.
optionExplanations.C: The interview pattern has Claude ask clarifying questions before implementing, which is a distinct workflow from writing tests first and iterating against them.
optionExplanations.D: Prompt chaining with separate testing and implementation steps is a prompt-engineering technique, not the development workflow described in the scenario of writing tests first and iterating on failures.

text: In a research task, the coordinator's context becomes ~80% verbose tool results (full page contents, reasoning traces, metadata) by the time it synthesizes. Best design change?

options.text: Increase the coordinator's context window to fit the verbose results
Reduce the number of subagents to limit total result volume
Have the coordinator summarize each result before storing it
Trim verbose tool outputs to the relevant fields before they accumulate in the coordinator's context

optionExplanations.A: Increasing the coordinator's context window to fit the verbose results doesn't address the underlying waste of carrying full page contents, reasoning traces, and metadata that aren't actually needed for synthesis.
optionExplanations.B: Reducing the number of subagents would limit total result volume but also reduces the coverage and parallelism the research task needs.
optionExplanations.C: Having the coordinator summarize each result before storing it adds extra processing steps and risks losing important detail compared to simply trimming to relevant fields.
optionExplanations.D: Trimming verbose tool outputs to only the relevant fields before they accumulate in the coordinator's context preserves the needed findings while freeing up context for synthesis, directly addressing the 80% verbose-content problem.

text: Claude Code loads the root CLAUDE.md but ignores packages/api/CLAUDE.md when editing files there. Most likely explanation?

options.text: Only one CLAUDE.md loads per session; root takes precedence
macOS filesystem casing causes a path mismatch
Subdirectory files must be explicitly imported via @import in the root
The packages/api/CLAUDE.md may have a syntax error or be excluded by a claudeMdExcludes-style setting

optionExplanations.A: The CLAUDE.md hierarchy supports loading multiple files (root plus subdirectory files), so the claim that only one loads per session with root taking precedence is incorrect.
optionExplanations.B: A macOS filesystem casing mismatch is an unlikely explanation compared to a more direct configuration issue like exclusion or a syntax error.
optionExplanations.C: Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files load automatically without needing an explicit @import in the root file, so this isn't required for packages/api/CLAUDE.md to be picked up.
optionExplanations.D: Since subdirectory CLAUDE.md files normally load automatically, the most likely explanation for one being ignored is that it's excluded by a configuration setting (e.g., a claudeMdExcludes-style setting common in monorepos) or contains a syntax/parse error.

text: Claude generates a response, a separate step validates it against business rules, and on failure the error is fed back for correction — repeating until it passes or hits a max attempt count. What is this pattern and how is it implemented?

options.text: The validation-retry loop — append the specific validation error to the prompt context on each retry
The retry pattern — simply retry the same prompt on failure
The feedback pattern — a second Claude instance critiques the first's output
The convergence pattern — raise temperature each retry to explore solutions

optionExplanations.A: This is the validation-retry loop: appending the specific validation error to the prompt context on each retry is what makes the correction targeted and effective, since Claude can address the exact problem identified.
optionExplanations.B: Simply retrying the same prompt on failure without incorporating the specific error doesn't let the model learn from what went wrong, unlike the feedback-driven approach described.
optionExplanations.C: A second Claude instance critiquing the first's output describes a different pattern (a critic/feedback pattern), not the single validation step with error feedback described in the scenario.
optionExplanations.D: Raising temperature each retry to explore solutions adds randomness rather than targeted correction, and temperature isn't a knob used this way on current models — it doesn't match the deterministic validation-and-correction loop described.

text: An Agent SDK app must run autonomously and produce structured JSON for downstream consumption. Which configuration is most appropriate?

options.text: Use query() and parse Claude's text response as JSON
Use claude -p with --output-format json and a JSON schema to get guaranteed structured output
Use query() with a custom JSON-output tool in allowed_tools
Pipe Claude's output through jq

optionExplanations.A: Parsing Claude's free-text response as JSON offers no structural guarantee, which is risky for an autonomous app producing output for downstream consumption.
optionExplanations.B: Using claude -p in non-interactive mode with --output-format json and a JSON schema yields guaranteed structured output in a structured_output field, making it the most appropriate configuration for an autonomous app needing reliable downstream JSON.
optionExplanations.C: Using query() with a custom JSON-output tool in allowed_tools requires building and maintaining extra tool infrastructure rather than using the built-in guaranteed structured output mechanism.
optionExplanations.D: Piping Claude's output through jq for post-processing is fragile, since it depends on the raw output already being well-formed and consistent.

text: CI test-generation behaves differently across machines because of different CLAUDE.md files, skills, and MCP servers. What ensures consistent CI execution?

options.text: Run CI on standardized Docker containers with identical configs
Use --bare, which skips auto-discovery of hooks, skills, plugins, MCP, and CLAUDE.md
Set a CLAUDE_CI=true environment variable
Store all developer configurations in version control and load them in CI

optionExplanations.A: Running CI on standardized Docker containers with identical configs adds overhead and may still inherit mounted local configuration, so it doesn't directly eliminate the auto-discovery inconsistency.
optionExplanations.B: --bare skips auto-discovery of hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, and CLAUDE.md files, so only explicitly-passed flags take effect — giving identical, reproducible behavior across machines and making it the recommended mode for scripted or CI use.
optionExplanations.C: Setting a CLAUDE_CI=true environment variable isn't a real toggle that controls auto-discovery of local configuration.
optionExplanations.D: Storing all developer configurations in version control and loading them in CI may not capture every local variation (e.g., machine-specific MCP servers or personal skills), so it doesn't guarantee consistency the way bypassing auto-discovery does.

text: A content system must produce marketing copy in a specific brand voice, always including product name, tagline, a 50–100 word description, and exactly 3 bullets. What combination best achieves consistent, branded output?

options.text: A tool definition with a JSON schema enforcing structure, plus few-shot examples demonstrating the brand voice
A detailed system prompt describing the brand voice with format instructions
Few-shot examples alone showing voice and format
output_config with a strict schema and no additional prompting
