optionExplanations.A: Combining a tool definition with a JSON schema (to enforce structure: product name, tagline, description, exactly 3 bullets) with few-shot examples (to demonstrate the brand voice) addresses both the structural and stylistic requirements together, which are two different problems that each technique alone only partially solves.
optionExplanations.B: A detailed system prompt describing brand voice and format is probabilistic regarding structure — it doesn't guarantee the exact format (e.g., exactly 3 bullets) every time.
optionExplanations.C: Few-shot examples alone can convey brand voice but don't guarantee the structural requirements like word count or bullet count.
optionExplanations.D: A strict schema alone guarantees the structure but says nothing about brand voice, so output could be structurally correct yet off-brand.

text: After running /compact during a debugging session, the agent no longer references specific file paths and function names from earlier. What should you do to preserve investigation findings across compaction?

options.text: Avoid /compact and manually remove irrelevant messages
Re-read all previously investigated files after compaction
Persist key findings (paths, function names, hypotheses) to scratchpad files before compacting
Configure compaction to preserve a higher percentage of the conversation

optionExplanations.A: Avoiding /compact and manually removing irrelevant messages doesn't solve the underlying problem of context continuing to grow during a long debugging session.
optionExplanations.B: Re-reading all previously investigated files after compaction wastes context re-deriving information that could have been preserved more efficiently.
optionExplanations.C: Persisting key findings — paths, function names, hypotheses — to scratchpad files before compacting puts that information outside the context window so it survives compaction and can be re-read on demand, directly solving the loss of references after /compact.
optionExplanations.D: There isn't a real user-facing control to configure compaction to preserve a higher percentage of the conversation; this isn't an actual mechanism.

text: An inquiry mentions both a shipping delay and a product defect; the system routes the whole thing to shipping. What design change properly handles both?

options.text: Train the router with more multi-concern examples
Decompose the inquiry into two tickets — one for shipping, one for product quality
Route to a senior agent who handles all inquiry types
Process the shipping concern first, then follow up on the defect

optionExplanations.A: Training the router with more multi-concern examples may improve accuracy but doesn't guarantee correct handling of every multi-concern inquiry, unlike structurally decomposing it.
optionExplanations.B: Decomposing the inquiry into two separate tickets — one for shipping, one for product quality — ensures each concern is routed to the appropriate specialist and that neither concern is dropped.
optionExplanations.C: Routing to a senior agent who handles all inquiry types loses the benefit of specialized handling for each distinct concern.
optionExplanations.D: Processing the shipping concern first and following up on the defect later risks losing track of or never properly addressing the second concern.

text: A verbose security-audit skill generates over 8,000 tokens of content and the team worries it will survive compaction and permanently consume context. What is the per-skill limit for compaction survival (per the source exam)?

options.text: No limit — all skill content survives compaction indefinitely
About 5,000 tokens per skill, with a total budget around 25,000 across all skills
2,000 tokens per skill with automatic truncation
Skill content is fully dropped during compaction and must be reloaded

optionExplanations.A: Per the source exam, skill content does not survive compaction without limit — there is a per-skill budget, so this option is incorrect.
optionExplanations.B: Per the source exam, skill content survives compaction up to a per-skill budget of about 5,000 tokens, with an overall cap around 25,000 tokens across all skills, meaning an 8,000-token skill exceeds the per-skill limit and should be trimmed or isolated.
optionExplanations.C: The per-skill limit cited by the source exam is around 5,000 tokens, not 2,000, and there's no indication of automatic truncation at 2,000 tokens specifically.
optionExplanations.D: Skill content isn't simply fully dropped during compaction requiring a full reload — it survives up to the stated per-skill and overall budgets.

text: Using the API-level MCP connector, tool calls from the MCP server use different content block types than regular tool_use. What are they?

options.text: mcp_request and mcp_response blocks wrapping standard tool_use
Standard tool_use/tool_result blocks with an 'mcp_server' field
mcp_tool_use and mcp_tool_result content blocks that extend the standard tool-calling format
External tool_use blocks referencing the MCP server by name

optionExplanations.A: There are no mcp_request/mcp_response wrapper blocks around standard tool_use in the MCP connector format — this naming doesn't match the actual block types.
optionExplanations.B: The MCP connector doesn't use standard tool_use/tool_result blocks with an added 'mcp_server' field; it uses distinctly named block types instead.
optionExplanations.C: The MCP connector surfaces mcp_tool_use and mcp_tool_result content blocks, which extend the standard tool-calling format and carry the server name, distinguishing them from regular tool_use/tool_result blocks.
optionExplanations.D: There's no 'external tool_use block referencing the MCP server by name' format — this doesn't match the actual distinctly typed mcp_tool_use/mcp_tool_result blocks.

text: A team migrating from manual budget_tokens wants adaptive reasoning depth on Claude 4.x. What is the recommended configuration?

options.text: Keep using budget_tokens for precise control
Use adaptive thinking together with the effort parameter to control depth dynamically
Set thinking enabled and let the model self-regulate without effort
Disable thinking and use chain-of-thought prompting instead

optionExplanations.A: Continuing to use budget_tokens for precise manual control is the deprecated path being migrated away from, not the recommended configuration for Claude 4.x.
optionExplanations.B: Using adaptive thinking together with the effort parameter lets the model dynamically decide when and how deeply to think, making it the recommended replacement for manual budget_tokens control on Claude 4.x.
optionExplanations.C: Enabling thinking without setting an effort level lacks the directional control needed to guide reasoning depth, missing part of the recommended adaptive configuration.
optionExplanations.D: Disabling thinking and relying on chain-of-thought prompting gives up the dedicated reasoning capability entirely rather than adapting it.

text: An agent's response sometimes contains multiple tool_use blocks. How should the developer handle this?

options.text: Execute only the first tool_use and ignore the rest
Execute all tool_use blocks (possibly in parallel) and return all tool_result blocks in the next user message
Return an error — only one tool_use should appear per response
Queue the calls and execute one per subsequent API call

optionExplanations.A: Executing only the first tool_use block and ignoring the rest drops intended work, since multiple tool_use blocks in a single response are all meant to be executed.
optionExplanations.B: Multiple tool_use blocks in a response are valid and should be executed (possibly in parallel), with all corresponding tool_result blocks returned together in the next user message.
optionExplanations.C: Returning an error is incorrect, since the API allows multiple tool_use blocks per response — it is not a violation requiring an error.
optionExplanations.D: Queuing the calls and executing one per subsequent API call needlessly adds latency compared to executing them together and returning all results at once.

text: Processing 50,000 documents via the Message Batches API, you must correlate each response with its input document. How?

options.text: Use the custom_id field on each batch request to carry a unique document identifier
Process sequentially and rely on response order matching input order
Embed the document ID in the prompt and extract it from the response
Use a built-in request_id that auto-generates tracking IDs

optionExplanations.A: The custom_id field on each batch request is designed exactly for carrying a unique document identifier, and it appears on the corresponding result, making correlation reliable across 50,000 documents.
optionExplanations.B: Processing sequentially and relying on response order matching input order is unreliable, since response order isn't guaranteed by the Message Batches API.
optionExplanations.C: Embedding the document ID in the prompt and re-extracting it from the response is fragile, since it depends on Claude reliably reproducing the ID rather than using a guaranteed correlation mechanism.
optionExplanations.D: There is no built-in auto-generating request_id feature for this kind of tracking — custom_id is the actual mechanism provided.

text: For long documents (200+ pages), what layout does the prompting guidance recommend?

options.text: Query first, then the document, so Claude knows what to look for
Document split into chunks with queries interspersed
Document as a system message, queries as user messages
Longform data at the top, queries/instructions at the end (≈30% quality improvement)

optionExplanations.A: Placing the query first, before the document, is less effective than the recommended layout for long documents.
optionExplanations.B: Splitting the document into chunks with interspersed queries fragments the document, which is not the recommended approach for long-context quality.
optionExplanations.C: Structuring the document as a system message with queries as user messages isn't the recommended layout described in the prompting guidance.
optionExplanations.D: Placing the longform document at the top with queries and instructions at the end is the recommended 'data first, query last' layout, which measurably improves long-context quality by around 30%.

text: Agent A discovers a legacy DB uses a non-standard date format (YYYYDDMM). If the coordinator passes only A's final results to Agent B, B will misread all dates. What metadata practice prevents this?

options.text: Have Agent A reformat all dates so B doesn't need to know about the format
Include metadata about data-format peculiarities, sources, and methodologies alongside A's findings when passing to B
Create a shared config file that all agents reference for formats
Have the coordinator ask Agent A to re-run with explicit format conversion

optionExplanations.A: Having Agent A reformat all dates so B doesn't need to know about the format may miss edge cases and loses provenance information about why the reformatting was necessary.
optionExplanations.B: Including metadata about data-format peculiarities, sources, and methodologies alongside Agent A's findings when passing them to Agent B lets B correctly interpret the non-standard date format and make informed decisions, directly preventing the misreading.
optionExplanations.C: Creating a shared config file that all agents reference adds infrastructure overhead without guaranteeing that Agent B actually consults it before processing the dates.
optionExplanations.D: Having the coordinator ask Agent A to re-run with explicit format conversion wastes computation compared to simply passing along the relevant metadata with the original results.

text: A Claude 4.x agent sometimes spawns subagents for simple tasks it could do directly, raising cost and latency. Recommended mitigation?

options.text: Add CLAUDE.md/system-prompt guidance on when subagent use is warranted vs. handling directly
Disable subagent capabilities entirely
Set a minimum prompt character count to trigger subagents
Lower temperature to make delegation less likely

optionExplanations.A: Adding CLAUDE.md or system-prompt guidance on when subagent use is warranted (e.g., for parallel or independent work) versus handling tasks directly is the targeted fix for over-delegation on simple tasks.
optionExplanations.B: Disabling subagent capabilities entirely removes a valuable capability needed for legitimately parallel or independent work, overcorrecting for the problem.
optionExplanations.C: Setting a minimum prompt character count to trigger subagents is an arbitrary heuristic that doesn't reflect whether a task actually benefits from delegation.
optionExplanations.D: Lowering temperature isn't the lever that controls delegation behavior, and temperature isn't a current configurable knob in this context.

text: A support agent makes many exploratory tool calls to discover available knowledge-base articles. Which MCP feature reduces these calls?

options.text: Pre-fetch all tool results at session start and cache them
Tool definitions whose descriptions list all available articles
MCP resources that expose content catalogs the agent can browse without tool calls
A master query tool returning all articles in one call

optionExplanations.A: Pre-fetching all tool results at session start and caching them is wasteful, since it retrieves content regardless of whether it's ever needed for the conversation.
optionExplanations.B: Tool definitions whose descriptions list all available articles are static and bloat the description, scaling poorly compared to a dedicated discovery mechanism.
optionExplanations.C: MCP resources expose content catalogs that the agent can browse without spending tool calls, directly reducing the exploratory tool calls used to discover available knowledge-base articles.
optionExplanations.D: A master query tool returning all articles in one call dumps excessive data at once, which isn't the efficient discovery mechanism MCP resources provide.

text: Root CLAUDE.md says 'use tabs'; packages/frontend/CLAUDE.md says 'use 2 spaces'. When editing a file in packages/frontend/, which applies?

options.text: Tabs — root always takes precedence
An error occurs due to the conflict
2 spaces — the subdirectory CLAUDE.md is more specific and takes precedence there
Both load and Claude picks randomly

optionExplanations.A: Root CLAUDE.md doesn't always take precedence — in the hierarchy, the more specific subdirectory file wins for files within its scope.
optionExplanations.B: Conflicting instructions between root and subdirectory CLAUDE.md files don't cause an error; the hierarchy resolves them by specificity.
optionExplanations.C: In the CLAUDE.md hierarchy, the more specific instruction — the one closer to the file being edited — takes precedence, so packages/frontend/CLAUDE.md's '2 spaces' rule applies when editing files there.
optionExplanations.D: Both files can load, but resolution isn't random — the more specific subdirectory instruction deterministically wins for files in that subdirectory.

text: When an MCP tool hits a business-rule violation (e.g., refund exceeds policy), how should the error be communicated to the agent?

options.text: Return isError: true with a generic 'Operation failed' message
Throw an exception that terminates the agentic loop
Return isError: false with the policy limit embedded in a success response
Return isError: true with structured metadata (errorCategory='policy_violation', isRetryable: false, customer-friendly description)

optionExplanations.A: Returning isError: true with only a generic 'Operation failed' message offers no guidance to the agent about why it failed or whether it's worth retrying.
optionExplanations.B: Throwing an exception that terminates the agentic loop is too aggressive a response for a business-rule violation that the agent could otherwise communicate to the customer.
optionExplanations.C: Returning isError: false while embedding the policy limit in a success response misrepresents the outcome, since a policy violation is in fact a failure, not a success.
optionExplanations.D: Returning isError: true with structured metadata — including errorCategory='policy_violation', isRetryable: false, and a customer-friendly description — tells the agent the failure category, that it shouldn't retry, and gives it language it can relay to the customer.

text: Which models support 'context awareness' (tracking remaining token budget), per the source exam?

options.text: All Claude models automatically track context-window usage
It's a client-side feature that works with any model
Only Opus 4.6, as it needs the highest-capability model
Sonnet 4.6, Sonnet 4.5, and Haiku 4.5 support budget tracking

optionExplanations.A: Not all Claude models automatically track context-window usage; per the source exam, this capability is attributed to a specific subset of models, not all of them.
optionExplanations.B: Context awareness isn't purely a client-side feature working uniformly with any model; per the source exam, it's tied to specific model versions.
optionExplanations.C: This capability isn't restricted to only Opus 4.6 needing the highest-capability model; per the source exam, multiple specific models support it.
optionExplanations.D: Per the source exam, Sonnet 4.6, Sonnet 4.5, and Haiku 4.5 are the models that support 'context awareness' (tracking remaining token budget).

text: Subagents perform better when the coordinator gives goals and quality criteria rather than step-by-step procedures. Why?

options.text: Goals and criteria let the subagent reason about the best approach, leveraging its own capabilities rather than following rigid steps
Step-by-step procedures exceed the subagent's context window
Subagents can only execute goals, not procedures
Procedures add execution latency

optionExplanations.A: Goals and quality criteria let the subagent apply its own reasoning to find the best approach for the specific situation, which usually outperforms following rigid, prescribed step-by-step procedures.
optionExplanations.B: Step-by-step procedures don't inherently exceed a subagent's context window — that isn't the reason goals work better than procedures.
optionExplanations.C: Subagents can in fact follow procedures; the point is that they perform better with goals and criteria, not that procedures are impossible for them to execute.
optionExplanations.D: Procedures don't inherently add execution latency — the advantage of goals and criteria is about reasoning quality and adaptability, not speed.

text: A skill needs filesystem access but must run only when explicitly invoked by the user (never auto-triggered by the model). Which frontmatter controls this?

options.text: user-invocable: true combined with disable-model-invocation: true
auto-invoke: false
trigger: manual
interactive-only: true

optionExplanations.A: Combining user-invocable: true with disable-model-invocation: true allows the user to manually trigger the skill via slash command while preventing the model from auto-triggering it, exactly matching the requirement that it run only when explicitly invoked.
optionExplanations.B: auto-invoke: false is not an actual frontmatter field used for this purpose; the real mechanism uses the disable-model-invocation key.
optionExplanations.C: trigger: manual is not a real frontmatter field for skills — it doesn't match the documented invocation-control keys.
optionExplanations.D: interactive-only: true is not a real frontmatter field for controlling skill invocation in this way.

text: An agent consistently picks the wrong tool between get_product_info and search_products (both one-line descriptions). What is the primary mechanism Claude uses for tool selection?

options.text: It analyzes each tool's input schema for parameter compatibility
Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism — Claude uses them to understand each tool's purpose and when to use it
It matches tool names to keywords in the user's message
It uses training-data statistics to predict the more common tool

optionExplanations.A: Input schema analysis is a secondary mechanism compared to tool descriptions, which are the primary signal Claude uses for tool selection.
optionExplanations.B: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism — Claude uses them to understand each tool's purpose and when to use it, so minimal one-line descriptions for get_product_info and search_products leave Claude unable to disambiguate them.
optionExplanations.C: Matching tool names to keywords in the user's message oversimplifies how tool selection actually works; descriptions, not name-keyword matching, are the main driver.
optionExplanations.D: Tool selection isn't based on training-data statistics predicting the more common tool; that isn't how API tool-calling selection works.

text: With server-side compaction over multiple compactions, top-level usage fields read lower than actual. Where is the complete billing usage?

options.text: In an X-Usage-Total response header
In a billing dashboard that auto-sums everything
In the per-iteration usage array, which tracks each compaction and message iteration separately
In a usage.compaction_tokens field

optionExplanations.A: There is no X-Usage-Total response header that aggregates billing usage across compactions; this isn't how the information is exposed.
optionExplanations.B: A billing dashboard that auto-sums everything isn't the per-request mechanism for tracking usage across compactions within an API response.
optionExplanations.C: The complete usage is found by summing across the per-iteration usage array, which separately tracks each compaction and message iteration — the top-level usage fields omit compaction and read lower than the actual total.
optionExplanations.D: There is no dedicated usage.compaction_tokens field; the complete picture comes from the per-iteration usage array instead.

text: Per the agentic prompting guidance, what is the benefit of having Claude produce an init.sh that sets up the project environment?

options.text: It provides graceful starts for future context windows, reliably re-establishing the working environment
Shell scripts are more reliable than Claude's direct file operations
It bypasses Claude's permission system for faster setup
init.sh is a reserved filename Claude Code treats specially

optionExplanations.A: An init.sh script provides graceful starts for future context windows, reliably re-establishing the working environment without needing to rediscover setup steps each time.
optionExplanations.B: The benefit isn't that shell scripts are inherently more reliable than Claude's direct file operations — it's about giving future sessions a repeatable way to restore the environment.
optionExplanations.C: init.sh doesn't bypass Claude Code's permission system; its value is in providing a consistent setup mechanism, not in circumventing permissions.
optionExplanations.D: init.sh is not a reserved filename that Claude Code treats specially — its benefit comes from being a script future sessions can run to set up the environment, not from special file-name handling.

text: The Edit tool fails because the search string appears in three places and you want to modify only the second. Adding context still doesn't uniquely identify it. Correct approach?

options.text: Use Edit with an 'occurrence: 2' parameter
Include more surrounding context to uniquely identify the second occurrence
Use Bash sed to target the line number
Read the file, then Write the complete content with the modification applied at the right occurrence

optionExplanations.A: There is no 'occurrence' parameter on the Edit tool that lets you target a specific match number — this option references a feature that doesn't exist.
optionExplanations.B: Including more surrounding context is the first thing to try when a search string isn't unique, but the scenario states this still didn't uniquely identify the second occurrence, so it isn't sufficient here.
optionExplanations.C: Using Bash sed to target the line number introduces shell-escaping risk and bypasses the safer, documented tooling approach.
optionExplanations.D: When unique matching isn't feasible even with added context, the documented fallback is Read + Write: read the file, apply the modification at the correct occurrence, and write the complete content back.

text: An agent matched a customer to two similar-named accounts, picked the more recently active one, and gave order status for the wrong account. What should it have done?

options.text: Checked both accounts and presented both options to the customer
Escalated to a human because the ambiguity was too complex
Used IP geolocation to pick the more likely account
Asked the customer for an additional identifier (email, account number) to disambiguate

optionExplanations.A: Checking both accounts and presenting both options to the customer would leak information about another account's existence and details, which is inappropriate.
optionExplanations.B: Escalating to a human is premature when simply asking for an additional identifier could resolve the ambiguity directly.
optionExplanations.C: Using IP geolocation to pick the more likely account is unreliable for a decision with real consequences like giving out the wrong order status.
optionExplanations.D: The agent should have asked the customer for an additional identifier, such as an email or account number, to disambiguate between the two similar-named accounts rather than guessing based on recent activity.

text: Should an agentic system use a pre-configured decision tree (if user mentions 'order', call lookup_order) or let Claude reason about tool selection from context?

options.text: Pre-configured decision trees provide more reliable selection
Model-driven tool selection based on context, not pre-configured decision trees or fixed sequences
A hybrid where trees handle common cases and Claude handles edge cases
A routing layer that classifies request type before passing to Claude

optionExplanations.A: Pre-configured decision trees constrain reasoning and don't handle the nuance and variety of real requests as well as model-driven selection does.
optionExplanations.B: Model-driven tool selection based on context, rather than pre-configured decision trees or fixed sequences, lets Claude reason about which tool fits given good tool descriptions, handling nuance that rigid rules miss.
optionExplanations.C: A hybrid where trees handle common cases and Claude handles edge cases only partially avoids constraining reasoning, since the tree portion still limits flexibility for the cases it covers.
optionExplanations.D: A routing layer that classifies request type before passing to Claude constrains reasoning by pre-deciding category, similar to a decision tree, rather than letting Claude reason directly from context.

text: A system handles time-sensitive user requests and large-scale data analysis. To optimize cost without hurting user-facing latency, which combination of API features is best?

options.text: Batch processing for everything with a priority queue for urgent requests
Streaming for all requests to reduce perceived latency
Real-time API with prompt caching for user-facing requests; Message Batches API for non-urgent analysis
effort='low' for all requests to minimize cost

optionExplanations.A: Batch processing for everything with a priority queue for urgent requests still delays urgent, time-sensitive requests within batch-style processing rather than serving them in true real time.
optionExplanations.B: Streaming for all requests improves perceived latency but doesn't reduce cost, which is part of the stated optimization goal.
optionExplanations.C: Using the real-time API with prompt caching (about 90% savings on cached prefixes, low latency) for user-facing requests, and the Message Batches API (about 50% savings, up to 24h turnaround) for non-urgent analysis, optimizes cost without hurting user-facing latency by matching each API's strengths to the right workload.
optionExplanations.D: Using effort='low' for all requests minimizes cost but can hurt the quality of the large-scale data analysis, which likely benefits from higher effort.

text: An agent-teams setup has 8 teammates each assigned 10+ tasks and suffers frequent file conflicts and coordination overhead. What best practice applies?

options.text: Reduce to ~3–5 teammates with ~5–6 tasks each, and assign tasks to avoid same-file edits across teammates
Increase to 12 teammates to lower per-teammate workload
Add a dedicated conflict-resolution teammate
Switch from agent teams to subagents to remove coordination

optionExplanations.A: Reducing to about 3 to 5 teammates with about 5 to 6 tasks each, and assigning tasks to avoid same-file edits across teammates, is the best practice that directly reduces file conflicts and coordination overhead from the original 8-teammate, 10+-task setup.
optionExplanations.B: Increasing to 12 teammates would worsen coordination overhead and file conflicts rather than reduce them.
optionExplanations.C: Adding a dedicated conflict-resolution teammate treats the symptom of conflicts rather than the root cause, which is too many teammates with overlapping file edits.
optionExplanations.D: Switching from agent teams to subagents removes the collaboration benefits needed when work is genuinely interdependent, rather than fixing the team-sizing and task-partitioning issue.

text: When should the 'interview pattern' be used, per the guidance?

options.text: When you want Claude to generate documentation for the whole codebase
When Claude needs to interview end users for requirements
When multiple developers collaborate in one session
When the task is ambiguous and having Claude ask clarifying questions before implementing leads to better outcomes

optionExplanations.A: Generating documentation for the whole codebase isn't the scenario the interview pattern addresses — it's not about ambiguous task requirements needing clarification.
optionExplanations.B: Claude interviewing end users for requirements describes a different scenario from the pattern, which is about Claude asking the developer clarifying questions before implementing.
optionExplanations.C: Multiple developers collaborating in one session describes a different collaboration scenario, not the use case for the interview pattern.
optionExplanations.D: The interview pattern should be used when the task is ambiguous, since having Claude ask clarifying questions before implementing leads to better outcomes by ensuring the right thing gets built.

text: A survey-processing pipeline uses strict-mode structured output with required fields. Claude refuses to produce output for a survey containing only profanity with no constructive feedback. What is happening?

options.text: Strict mode doesn't support free-text fields
The schema requires fields the input can't populate, causing a validation failure
The profanity triggers a content filter separate from schema validation
On refusal or max_tokens, strict-mode output isn't guaranteed — the schema is only guaranteed on a successful end_turn completion

optionExplanations.A: Strict mode does support free-text fields, so this isn't a limitation of the mode itself — the explanation explicitly rules this out as the cause.
optionExplanations.B: The issue isn't that the schema requires fields the survey can't populate; the profanity-only input doesn't make required fields unfillable, so this isn't the real cause.
optionExplanations.C: There is no separate content filter independent of schema validation causing the refusal here; the explanation attributes the behavior to the stop reason, not a hidden filter.
optionExplanations.D: Structured-output schema guarantees only hold when generation completes normally via end_turn. Here Claude refuses to produce output, so the schema conformance guarantee doesn't apply, and the application must handle the refusal stop reason rather than assume valid structured output.

text: A team enforces compliance with hooks but also wants Claude to explain to the customer why an operation was blocked. Which hook type lets you inject explanatory feedback when blocking?

options.text: A PreToolUse hook with exit code 2 and JSON output containing the block reason, which Claude uses to explain to the customer
A PreToolUse hook with exit code 0 and a description in stdout
A PostToolUse hook that adds the block reason to a modified tool result
A notification hook that sends the reason directly to the customer

optionExplanations.A: A PreToolUse hook that blocks with exit code 2 and returns structured JSON containing the block reason gives Claude the information it needs to turn into a customer-friendly explanation, satisfying both the compliance block and the desire to explain it.
optionExplanations.B: Exit code 0 signals success and does not block the tool call at all, so this approach fails to enforce compliance even though it puts a description in stdout.
optionExplanations.C: A PostToolUse hook fires only after the tool has already executed, so it cannot prevent the operation — it's too late to block anything.
optionExplanations.D: A notification hook is designed to send alerts, not to feed reasoning back into the conversation so Claude can use it to explain the block to the customer.

text: Configuring the API-level MCP connector, where does the authorization token for the MCP server go?

options.text: In a separate auth header alongside the Anthropic API key
In a .env file the MCP server reads independently
In the system prompt as a tool-accessible credential
In the mcp_servers configuration entry: {type: 'url', url, name, authorization_token}

optionExplanations.A: The MCP authorization token is not combined into a separate header alongside the Anthropic API key; it lives in the mcp_servers configuration entry instead.
optionExplanations.B: The connector does not require the MCP server to read credentials from its own .env file; the token is supplied directly in the connector configuration.
optionExplanations.C: Putting the credential in the system prompt as a tool-accessible value is a security anti-pattern and is not how the API-level MCP connector handles authorization.
optionExplanations.D: The authorization token is configured directly in the mcp_servers entry, e.g. {type: 'url', url, name, authorization_token}, and is passed to the MCP server when the connection is established.

text: An architect has 22 tools with strict mode enabled and hits a limit. What is the constraint (per the source exam's stated limits)?

options.text: Strict mode supports at most 10 tools per request
There's no limit as long as total schema size is under 100KB
Strict mode caps at ~20 strict tools, so the schema must be simplified
22 tools work, but compilation will exceed the API timeout

optionExplanations.A: There is no 10-tool cap for strict mode; the actual documented limit in the source exam is around 20 strict tools, not 10.
optionExplanations.B: There is in fact a limit on the number of strict tools (and on schema complexity such as optional parameters and union types), so it is incorrect to say there's no limit as long as schema size is under 100KB.
optionExplanations.C: Per the source exam, strict structured outputs cap at roughly 20 strict tools along with limits on optional parameters and union types, so having 22 tools exceeds this cap and the schema/tool set must be simplified.
optionExplanations.D: The constraint is a documented schema-complexity/tool-count cap, not an API request timeout during compilation, so this mischaracterizes the limitation.

text: A knowledge agent compiles from multiple sources; two reports give different market sizes for the same market. How should the synthesis agent handle the conflict?

options.text: Average the conflicting values
Use the most recent source's value
Flag the conflict and escalate to a human reviewer
Annotate the conflict with full attribution — present both values with their provenance rather than picking one

optionExplanations.A: Averaging the two conflicting market-size values would manufacture a fabricated number that doesn't correspond to either source's actual finding.
optionExplanations.B: Assuming the most recent source is correct treats recency as a proxy for accuracy, which is unsafe since a newer report isn't necessarily more reliable.
optionExplanations.C: Escalating to a human reviewer is unnecessary overhead when the conflict can simply be presented transparently to the consumer of the synthesis.
optionExplanations.D: The synthesis agent should annotate the conflict with full attribution, presenting both market-size values along with their sources, dates, and methodology so the reader can judge which to trust rather than having the agent silently resolve it.

text: A developer wants a skill to always inject the latest contents of a config file into its context at invocation time. Which SKILL.md feature supports this?

options.text: Backtick-wrapped !command syntax that runs a command and injects its output (e.g. !`cat config.json`)
@import syntax for referencing external files
A 'files' frontmatter field listing files to include
${FILE_PATH} variable substitution

optionExplanations.A: Backtick-wrapped !command syntax (e.g., !`cat config.json`) runs a shell command and injects its current output into the skill's context at invocation time, which is exactly the dynamic, always-fresh injection mechanism needed here.
optionExplanations.B: @import syntax is used for statically referencing external files (e.g., in CLAUDE.md), not for dynamically running a command to fetch live file contents at invocation.
optionExplanations.C: A 'files' frontmatter field would just list static files to include, not dynamically inject the latest contents via a command at invocation time.
optionExplanations.D: ${FILE_PATH} variable substitution is a placeholder mechanism, not the dynamic command-execution injection mechanism described in the scenario.

text: An insurance-claims agent processes multi-coverage claims (auto + medical) in a single pass and sometimes misses the secondary coverage. What scheduling approach helps ensure full coverage?

options.text: Decompose the multi-coverage claim into separate items and investigate each coverage type independently before combining
A fixed checklist of all coverage types to iterate through
Increase the context window to prevent information loss
Route multi-coverage claims to a senior agent

optionExplanations.A: Decomposing the multi-coverage claim into separate items and investigating each coverage type independently before combining results prevents one coverage type from overshadowing the other, addressing the attention dilution that caused secondary coverage to be missed.
optionExplanations.B: A fixed checklist of coverage types is a brittle approach that doesn't adapt well and doesn't directly solve the underlying attention-dilution problem within a single pass.
optionExplanations.C: Increasing the context window doesn't address the root cause, which is attention being spread thin across multiple coverage types in one pass, not a lack of context space.
optionExplanations.D: Routing to a senior agent defers the problem to another party rather than fixing the underlying scheduling/decomposition issue in the agent's own processing.

text: Which MCP tool-naming convention follows best practice?

options.text: tool1, tool2, tool3 with descriptions explaining each
Internal function names like handlePRList, sendSlackMsg
Service-based namespacing like github_list_prs, slack_send_message, jira_create_ticket
Single words like list, send, create

optionExplanations.A: Generic numbered names like tool1, tool2, tool3 carry no inherent meaning about what the tool does, even with descriptions attached, making them poor naming practice.
optionExplanations.B: Names that mirror internal function implementations (handlePRList, sendSlackMsg) leak internal implementation details rather than clearly communicating the tool's purpose to the model.
optionExplanations.C: Service-based namespacing such as github_list_prs, slack_send_message, and jira_create_ticket follows the service_action_target convention, making each tool's purpose unambiguous and avoiding collisions across services.
optionExplanations.D: Single-word names like list, send, or create are too generic and would collide across multiple services that all need similarly named actions.

text: A compliance system runs monthly on the 15th, processing 10,000 documents; the reports are consumed the next business day. Real-time API costs $2,000 per run. What feature reduces cost?

options.text: Prompt caching to reduce repeated document-processing cost
effort='low' to reduce per-document cost at the expense of quality
Message Batches API for ~50% savings, since the reports don't need real-time results
A smaller model (Haiku) for lower per-token cost

optionExplanations.A: Prompt caching helps with repeated prompt prefixes across calls, but it doesn't address the per-document processing volume cost that's driving the $2,000 run.
optionExplanations.B: Setting effort='low' could reduce per-document cost, but doing so risks degrading the quality of the monthly compliance reports, an undesirable tradeoff.
optionExplanations.C: Since the reports are only consumed the next business day, the workload tolerates latency, making the Message Batches API ideal: it offers roughly 50% savings (about $2,000 down to about $1,000) well within its 24-hour processing window.
optionExplanations.D: Switching to a smaller model like Haiku could lower per-token cost but may reduce the accuracy that a compliance use case requires, making it a riskier tradeoff than using batching.

text: Adding several product images alongside thinking mode makes the context window fill faster. What explains the interaction between images, thinking, and context?

options.text: Images are incompatible with extended thinking and must be processed separately
All tokens, including thinking tokens, count toward the context window, so images plus thinking traces consume context faster than either alone
Each image consumes fixed tokens regardless of thinking mode
Images automatically disable thinking mode

optionExplanations.A: Images are not incompatible with extended thinking — they can be used together, so this option misstates the relationship.
optionExplanations.B: All tokens, including thinking tokens generated during extended thinking, count toward the context window, so adding token-heavy images alongside thinking traces consumes context faster than either alone — this is the correct explanation of the interaction.
optionExplanations.C: While each image does consume a relatively fixed number of tokens, this option misses the key point that thinking tokens add on top of that, compounding the context consumption.
optionExplanations.D: Images do not automatically disable thinking mode; the two features can be used simultaneously.

text: A DB-migration workflow has 4 steps; step 1 (analyze current schema) is verbose and reads many files. Which workflow best preserves context for steps 2–4?

options.text: Execute all four steps in one conversation
Use the Explore subagent for step 1, then handle steps 2–4 in the main conversation using its summary
Four separate conversations, one per step
Use plan mode for all four steps before executing

optionExplanations.A: Executing all four steps in one conversation lets the verbose schema-analysis output from step 1 flood the context, leaving less room for the remaining migration steps.
optionExplanations.B: Using the Explore subagent for the verbose step 1 schema analysis isolates that noisy work and returns just a summary, preserving the main conversation's context for generating, dry-running, and executing the migration in steps 2 through 4.
optionExplanations.C: Four separate conversations, one per step, would lose continuity between steps since each step depends on context from the prior one.
optionExplanations.D: Using plan mode for all four steps doesn't address the specific problem of step 1's verbose file-reading consuming context that's needed for the later steps.

text: A PostToolUse hook strips PII from MCP tool results, yet Claude still occasionally references PII. Most likely issue?

options.text: PostToolUse hooks can only inspect, not modify, tool results
Claude is retrieving PII from its training data
The PII appeared in earlier conversation messages or prior tool results that weren't transformed
A race condition lets some results pass unmodified

optionExplanations.A: PostToolUse hooks can in fact modify tool results, not just inspect them, so this isn't the cause of PII leaking through.
optionExplanations.B: Claude referencing PII isn't because it's retrieving specific customer PII from its training data — the PII originated from the conversation's own tool calls.
optionExplanations.C: The PostToolUse hook only strips PII from the current tool result; any PII that appeared in earlier conversation messages or prior tool results that weren't passed through the transformation remains in context and can still be referenced by Claude.
optionExplanations.D: A race condition letting some results pass unmodified is a less likely explanation than the more straightforward issue of historical, untransformed context still containing PII.

text: A 14-file PR needs thorough per-file analysis AND detection of cross-file issues (e.g., incompatible API changes between a service and its consumer). Analyzing all files at once gave inconsistent depth. What multi-pass strategy works?

options.text: Randomly sample 5 files for deep analysis, summary-level for the rest
Group files by directory and analyze each directory independently
Single pass with instructions to keep depth consistent
First pass: per-file local analysis; second pass: cross-file integration using the first-pass summaries

optionExplanations.A: Randomly sampling only 5 files for deep analysis would miss issues in the files left at summary level, failing to provide thorough per-file analysis across all 14 files.
optionExplanations.B: Grouping files by directory and analyzing each group independently risks missing cross-file issues that span different directories, such as an API change between a service and a consumer in another directory.
optionExplanations.C: A single pass with instructions to keep depth consistent is essentially the same approach that already produced inconsistent depth, so it doesn't solve the underlying attention-dilution problem.
optionExplanations.D: Using a first pass for per-file local analysis followed by a second pass dedicated to cross-file integration (using the first pass's summaries) gives each file focused depth while still catching inter-file issues like incompatible API changes — directly addressing both requirements.

text: To include a team's coding standards from a separate document inside CLAUDE.md without duplicating content, which syntax is used?

options.text: #include './coding-standards.md' (C-preprocessor style)
An 'includes' field in the CLAUDE.md frontmatter
@import syntax to reference external files, keeping CLAUDE.md modular
Symbolic links to the external document

optionExplanations.A: C-preprocessor style #include syntax is not supported by CLAUDE.md for referencing external files.
optionExplanations.B: There is no 'includes' field in CLAUDE.md frontmatter for pulling in external documents.
optionExplanations.C: CLAUDE.md supports @import syntax to reference external files like a coding-standards document, allowing the content to be pulled in without duplication and keeping CLAUDE.md modular.
optionExplanations.D: Symbolic links are a filesystem-level trick that doesn't integrate with how CLAUDE.md actually loads and references content.

text: Which models support the 1M-token context window (per the source exam)?

options.text: All Claude 4 models
Claude Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, and Mythos Preview
Only Opus 4.6; others are limited to 200K
Sonnet 4.5 and Opus 4.5 support 1M, while 4.6 models support 2M

optionExplanations.A: Not all Claude 4 models support the 1M-token context window; older models such as Sonnet 4.5 are limited to 200K per the source exam.
optionExplanations.B: Per the source exam, the 1M-token context window is attributed specifically to Claude Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, and Mythos Preview.
optionExplanations.C: The 1M context window is not Opus-only; Sonnet 4.6 and Mythos Preview are also listed as supporting it per the source exam.
optionExplanations.D: The claim that Sonnet 4.5 and Opus 4.5 support 1M while 4.6 models support 2M is incorrect; the source exam attributes 200K to the older models and 1M to the 4.6/Mythos Preview models, not 2M.

text: An agent treats 'tool error' and 'tool ran fine but found nothing' identically, saying 'I couldn't find that.' What response distinction should the tool implement?

options.text: Use HTTP status codes (404 vs 500) in the response
Distinguish access failures (isError: true, isRetryable: true for timeouts) from valid empty results (isError: false with an empty result set)
Use the same format but add a log_level field
Always return isError: true with a severity field

optionExplanations.A: Using HTTP status codes like 404 vs 500 doesn't fit the MCP tool-result model and doesn't provide the agent-level distinction it needs between retryable failures and valid empty results.
optionExplanations.B: The tool should distinguish genuine access failures (isError: true, with isRetryable: true for things like timeouts) from valid empty results (isError: false with an empty result set), so the agent can retry when appropriate and accurately report 'no data' when the tool succeeded but simply found nothing.
optionExplanations.C: Keeping the same format and adding a log_level field doesn't give the agent a structural signal to distinguish failure from a legitimate empty result.
optionExplanations.D: Always returning isError: true regardless of outcome would make every empty-but-successful result look like a failure, which is the opposite of the needed distinction.

text: A team runs compaction then immediately needs current market data, having configured pause-after-compaction. What stop_reason do they get, and what should they do before continuing?

options.text: stop_reason 'end_turn' — continue normally
stop_reason 'tool_use' — compaction triggers a tool call
stop_reason 'compaction' — inject the updated market data, then continue the next request
stop_reason 'max_tokens' — the summary consumed the budget

optionExplanations.A: An 'end_turn' stop_reason would imply normal completion with no special handling needed, but pause-after-compaction is designed to interrupt the flow with its own distinct stop reason instead.
optionExplanations.B: Compaction does not trigger a tool_use stop reason; it has its own dedicated stop reason for signaling the pause.
optionExplanations.C: Pause-after-compaction returns a 'compaction' stop_reason, signaling that the harness should inject the updated, current market data into context before continuing with the next request.
optionExplanations.D: A 'max_tokens' stop reason would indicate the output was truncated due to budget limits, which is not what's happening when pause-after-compaction is configured and triggers.

text: An architect suspects the root and a subdirectory CLAUDE.md have contradictory testing instructions. What's the most efficient way to diagnose this?

options.text: Use /memory to see all loaded memory files and check for conflicts
Read each CLAUDE.md manually and compare
Run /init to regenerate and resolve conflicts
Add a debug: true flag to the root CLAUDE.md

optionExplanations.A: The /memory command shows exactly which CLAUDE.md files are loaded at each level (root and subdirectory), making it the most efficient way to spot contradictory testing instructions between them.
optionExplanations.B: Manually reading and comparing each CLAUDE.md file is slower and more error-prone than using a built-in command designed to surface the loaded memory hierarchy.
optionExplanations.C: Running /init would regenerate CLAUDE.md content and could overwrite existing configuration rather than simply diagnosing the conflict.
optionExplanations.D: There is no debug: true flag for CLAUDE.md files; this isn't a real diagnostic mechanism.

text: On which model family is interleaved thinking automatically available with adaptive thinking mode?

options.text: Claude 4 models (e.g. Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6) with adaptive thinking enabled
Only Sonnet 4.6 with an explicit interleaved_thinking: true flag
All Claude models when thinking is set to 'enabled'
Only Mythos Preview, as an experimental feature

optionExplanations.A: On Claude 4 models such as Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6, enabling adaptive thinking automatically makes interleaved thinking (thinking between tool calls) available with no special flag required.
optionExplanations.B: Interleaved thinking isn't gated behind an explicit interleaved_thinking: true flag restricted to a single model; it's automatically available via adaptive thinking on Claude 4 models.
optionExplanations.C: Interleaved thinking isn't available on all Claude models simply by setting thinking to 'enabled' — it's specifically tied to Claude 4 models with adaptive thinking.
optionExplanations.D: Interleaved thinking is not restricted to Mythos Preview as an experimental feature; it's available across Claude 4 models like Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6.

text: A Claude-based reviewer flags intentional design patterns as issues; 'be less aggressive' didn't help. Most effective technique?

options.text: Add a whitelist of approved patterns never to flag
Lower Claude's confidence threshold for flagging
Use a simpler model that flags less
Provide 2–4 targeted few-shot examples of the false-positive patterns and the correct (non-flagging) behavior

optionExplanations.A: A whitelist of approved patterns is rigid and incomplete — it can't anticipate every legitimate design pattern variation that might trigger a false positive.
optionExplanations.B: Confidence threshold is not actually a configurable parameter for this kind of behavioral adjustment, so lowering it isn't a viable technique.
optionExplanations.C: Switching to a simpler model risks missing true positives along with reducing false positives, trading away detection quality rather than fixing the underlying issue.
optionExplanations.D: Providing 2-4 targeted few-shot examples of the false-positive patterns alongside the correct, non-flagging behavior teaches the model to recognize legitimate design patterns and generalizes far better than a vague instruction like 'be less aggressive.'

text: An agent with extended thinking strips thinking blocks from previous turns; after a tool call, removing the thinking block causes an API error. Which rule was violated?

options.text: Thinking blocks must never be removed from history
Thinking blocks tied to a tool_use must be returned alongside the tool results; they can be dropped only after those results have been processed
Only the model can decide when to remove thinking blocks
Thinking blocks must be moved to the system prompt after tool use

optionExplanations.A: Thinking blocks can be dropped on later turns once their associated tool results have been processed, so saying they must never be removed is too strict.
optionExplanations.B: When thinking and tool use co-occur, the thinking block(s) for that turn must be returned alongside the tool results; removing the thinking block before those results have been processed violates this requirement and causes the API error described.
optionExplanations.C: It is the client/application, not the model, that controls when thinking blocks are removed from history, so this option misattributes the decision.
optionExplanations.D: Thinking blocks are not relocated to the system prompt after tool use; that isn't how the API manages thinking-block lifecycle.

text: Can subagent definitions be used as templates for teammates in agent teams?

options.text: No — agent teams and subagents are completely separate with no interoperability
Yes — subagent definitions can be referenced when spawning teammates, reusing agent configurations
No — teammates must be fully defined at spawn time
Yes — but only for the Explore subagent

optionExplanations.A: Agent teams and subagents are not completely separate with no interoperability — subagent definitions can in fact be reused when spawning teammates.
optionExplanations.B: Subagent definitions can be referenced when spawning teammates in agent teams, allowing well-defined agent configurations to be reused across standalone subagent use and team-based use.
optionExplanations.C: Teammates do not need to be fully defined at spawn time from scratch; existing subagent definitions can be referenced and reused.
optionExplanations.D: Reuse of subagent definitions as templates isn't limited to the Explore subagent; any well-defined subagent configuration can be referenced.

text: You must classify 80,000 documents for a weekly report generated every Friday; each needs category, confidence, and key entities. Most cost-effective approach?

options.text: Real-time API calls processed sequentially through the week
Real-time API with prompt caching
Multiple small batch jobs submitted daily
Message Batches API submitted Thursday, results within 24 hours for Friday

optionExplanations.A: Processing all 80,000 documents sequentially through real-time API calls during the week is the most expensive approach, since it pays full real-time pricing for every request.
optionExplanations.B: Prompt caching helps with repeated prompt prefixes but each request still incurs full per-request real-time pricing, so it doesn't capture the savings available for this latency-tolerant workload.
optionExplanations.C: Submitting multiple small batch jobs daily adds unnecessary operational complexity without any added benefit over a single well-timed batch submission.
optionExplanations.D: Since the report is only needed by Friday, submitting all 80,000 documents via the Message Batches API on Thursday delivers results within its 24-hour window while saving roughly 50% compared to real-time processing — the most cost-effective fit for this weekly, latency-tolerant workload.

text: Cache writes are charged but cache reads are 0 on subsequent requests with identical content, and the TTL hasn't expired. Most likely configuration issue?

options.text: Content after the breakpoint changes between requests
More than 4 breakpoints are configured, so all are ignored
The cacheable content is below the model's minimum token threshold
The API key lacks caching permissions

optionExplanations.A: The scenario states the content is identical between requests, so changing content after the breakpoint is ruled out as the cause.
optionExplanations.B: There isn't a behavior where having more than 4 breakpoints causes all of them to be ignored; this isn't the documented failure mode here.
optionExplanations.C: Each model has a minimum cacheable prefix length (roughly 1024-4096 tokens depending on the model); if the cacheable content falls below that threshold, a write may register but no readable cache entry actually forms, explaining why reads show 0 even with identical content and unexpired TTL.
optionExplanations.D: Caching isn't gated behind a separate API key permission, so a lack of 'caching permissions' isn't a real configuration issue.

text: For a complex multi-session agentic workflow (data collection, analysis, report) across context windows, what state-management approach does the agentic guidance recommend?

options.text: Structured JSON for data state, unstructured text for progress notes, and git for version tracking
Store all state in environment variables that persist across sessions
Store everything in a single JSON file read at each session start
Use a database for all state management

optionExplanations.A: A multi-format approach — structured JSON for queryable data state, unstructured text for human-readable progress notes, and git for version tracking across iterations — balances structure and flexibility, which is what the agentic guidance recommends for complex multi-session workflows spanning context windows.
optionExplanations.B: Storing all state in environment variables is fragile and not designed to persist reliably across separate sessions in the way this workflow needs.
optionExplanations.C: A single JSON file read at each session start lacks the granularity needed to separately track structured data, progress notes, and version history.
optionExplanations.D: Using a full database for all state management is unnecessary infrastructure overhead for what the recommended lightweight, multi-format approach already handles well.

text: A team wants Claude Code to automatically run the test suite after any file edit, without adding test commands to every prompt. What mechanism achieves this?

options.text: A PostToolUse hook on the Edit and Write tools that runs the test suite
Add 'always run tests after editing' to CLAUDE.md
A custom slash command combining edit + test
An --auto-test flag when starting Claude Code

optionExplanations.A: A PostToolUse hook bound to the Edit and Write tools automatically runs the test suite after every file modification, requiring no manual prompting and reliably enforcing the desired behavior.
optionExplanations.B: Adding an instruction like 'always run tests after editing' to CLAUDE.md is just a suggestion the model may or may not follow consistently, not a guaranteed enforcement mechanism.
optionExplanations.C: A custom slash command combining edit and test still requires the developer to manually invoke it rather than running automatically after every edit.
optionExplanations.D: There is no real --auto-test flag in Claude Code, so this isn't an actual mechanism for achieving automatic test runs.

text: A billing agent must NEVER refund over $500 without approval. The rule was in the system prompt, but long conversations sometimes lose it during compaction. Correct architectural approach?

options.text: Repeat the refund-limit instruction every 10 messages
Lower max_tokens to prevent conversations from getting long enough to compact
Add the rule to custom compaction instructions so it persists through summarization
Implement the $500 limit as a PreToolUse hook that blocks the refund tool when the amount exceeds $500

optionExplanations.A: Repeating the refund-limit instruction every 10 messages is a fragile workaround that still relies on the model consistently following a repeated prompt instruction rather than deterministic enforcement.
optionExplanations.B: Lowering max_tokens to prevent long conversations would cripple the agent's functionality and isn't a real solution to preserving the rule through compaction.
optionExplanations.C: Adding the rule to custom compaction instructions helps it survive summarization, but it still relies on the model honoring a rule embedded in a summarized prompt rather than enforcing it deterministically.
optionExplanations.D: Implementing the $500 limit as a PreToolUse hook that blocks the refund tool when the amount exceeds $500 enforces the rule deterministically in code, so it can never be lost to compaction or prompt drift, unlike a rule that lives only in the system prompt or summarized instructions.

text: A product-catalog MCP tool has optional filters (category, priceRange, brand, inStock) as nullable types. For 'show me laptops under $1000', the agent passes null for brand and inStock. What helps the model understand when to omit parameters?

options.text: Change nullable types to required with defaults
Remove optional parameters and require all fields
Add a 'required' field listing mandatory parameters
Add input examples showing correct usage that includes only the relevant filters and omits the others

optionExplanations.A: Changing nullable types to required with defaults would force values onto every call, including simple queries like 'laptops under $1000' that shouldn't need to specify brand or inStock at all.
optionExplanations.B: Removing optional parameters and requiring all fields would force the agent to always supply brand and inStock even when they're irrelevant to the user's request, which is the opposite of what's needed.
optionExplanations.C: A 'required' field listing mandatory parameters is standard schema practice but doesn't demonstrate to the model when and how to omit irrelevant optional parameters.
optionExplanations.D: Adding input examples that show correct usage — including only the relevant filters (like priceRange) and omitting the irrelevant ones (brand, inStock) — teaches the model the right behavior far better than schema types alone, directly addressing when to pass null versus omit a parameter.

text: A monorepo has frontend (React), backend (Python/FastAPI), and infra (Terraform), each with different linting/testing/coding conventions. How should the CLAUDE.md hierarchy be structured?

options.text: A single root CLAUDE.md with sections per service
Root CLAUDE.md plus .claude/rules/ files (glob-scoped) for each service
Only subdirectory CLAUDE.md files, no root
Root CLAUDE.md plus per-service subdirectory CLAUDE.md files

optionExplanations.A: A single root CLAUDE.md with sections per service would become unwieldy as the monorepo grows, since it doesn't scope conventions automatically to the relevant files being edited.
optionExplanations.B: A root CLAUDE.md for shared conventions plus glob-scoped .claude/rules/ files for each service (frontend, backend, infra) auto-loads the right service-specific rules based on the file being edited, providing context-aware guidance without requiring you to be in a particular directory.
optionExplanations.C: Using only subdirectory CLAUDE.md files with no root file would lose any conventions shared across all three services (frontend, backend, infra).
optionExplanations.D: Root plus per-service subdirectory CLAUDE.md files is a reasonable alternative, but it only applies when editing within those specific directories, making it less flexible than the glob-scoped rules approach.

text: A 6-step onboarding flow runs sequentially in one conversation; the credit-check step occasionally times out, forcing the whole flow to restart. How should it be redesigned for reliability?

options.text: Add retry logic for the credit-check step within the existing flow
Run all six steps in parallel
Move the credit check to a separate service outside the agent flow
Implement each step as an independent stage with checkpointing, so a failure resumes from that stage rather than restarting

optionExplanations.A: Adding retry logic for just the credit-check step within the existing flow doesn't address the broader reliability pattern needed for restarting from a failure point rather than retrying in place.
optionExplanations.B: Running all six steps in parallel ignores the dependencies between steps, such as the credit check needing to happen before account setup can proceed.
optionExplanations.C: Moving the credit check to a separate service outside the agent flow doesn't by itself add resumability to the overall onboarding flow when a step fails.
optionExplanations.D: Implementing each step as an independent stage with checkpointing lets a failure resume from that specific stage rather than restarting the entire six-step flow from the beginning — the correct reliability pattern for multi-step flows with dependencies.

text: A tool expects ISO 8601 dates but receives natural-language dates like 'next Tuesday'. The parameter is type: 'string'. What schema improvement fixes this?

options.text: Add a regex pattern enforcing ISO 8601
Add format: 'date' and a clear description ('Date in YYYY-MM-DD format')
Change the type to 'date', which auto-formats to ISO 8601
Server-side validation that rejects non-ISO dates with an error

optionExplanations.A: A regex pattern enforcing ISO 8601 may not be reliably honored by all models as a way to shape natural-language input into the correct format.
optionExplanations.B: Adding format: 'date' along with a clear description like 'Date in YYYY-MM-DD format' guides Claude to emit properly formatted ISO 8601 dates, since descriptions are the primary signal models use to understand expected input formats.
optionExplanations.C: There is no JSON-Schema 'date' type that automatically reformats natural-language input into ISO 8601; type alone doesn't perform that transformation.
optionExplanations.D: Server-side validation that rejects non-ISO dates with an error only catches the problem after the fact, adding round-trips instead of preventing the malformed input from being generated in the first place.

text: Using the API with a 6,000-token system prompt and 3,000-token examples, you want to cache both on Sonnet 4.5+ / Claude 4 models. How should breakpoints be structured?

options.text: A single breakpoint at the end of the system prompt only
Breakpoints after the system prompt AND after the examples
No explicit breakpoints needed — automatic caching handles it on these models
A breakpoint only at the end of the examples, caching everything as one segment

optionExplanations.A: A single manual breakpoint only at the end of the system prompt would manage the placement yourself, when on these current models that placement is already handled automatically.
optionExplanations.B: Manually placing breakpoints after both the system prompt and the examples micromanages something that current models already handle automatically, making explicit breakpoints unnecessary here.
optionExplanations.C: On Sonnet 4.5+ and Claude 4 models, automatic caching identifies and caches the stable prefix (system prompt and examples) without requiring you to place explicit breakpoints; manual breakpoints are mainly useful for fine-grained control or on older models, so relying on automatic caching is the correct, simplest approach.
optionExplanations.D: A breakpoint only at the end of the examples, treating everything as one segment, is another form of manual micromanagement that isn't needed given the automatic caching behavior on these models.

text: A tool_use was issued but execution was interrupted (server crash) with no corresponding tool_result. What must happen before continuing the conversation?

options.text: Provide a tool_result for the pending tool_use — either by retrying the tool or returning an error result indicating the failure
Send a new message without the tool_use block, pretending it never happened
Retry the entire conversation from the beginning
Send an empty assistant turn to reset state

optionExplanations.A: Every tool_use must be answered by a matching tool_result; since execution was interrupted, the application must supply one — either by retrying the tool or returning an error tool_result (is_error: true) — before the conversation can continue.
optionExplanations.B: Sending a new message without the tool_use block, pretending it never happened, violates the API's requirement that every tool_use have a corresponding tool_result.
optionExplanations.C: Retrying the entire conversation from the beginning is unnecessary and would discard all progress made before the interruption.
optionExplanations.D: Sending an empty assistant turn to reset state isn't a supported mechanism for resolving an unanswered tool_use.

text: A team wants Claude Code to enforce a specific import ordering at the tool level. Which approach reliably enforces it?

options.text: A PreToolUse hook that inspects the proposed edit content before it's applied and blocks non-compliant edits
A PostToolUse hook that runs an import-sorting linter and rejects non-compliant edits after applying
A custom slash command that sorts imports after each edit
CLAUDE.md instructions describing the import order

optionExplanations.A: A PreToolUse hook that inspects the proposed edit content before it's applied and blocks non-compliant import ordering gives deterministic enforcement, preventing violations from ever landing in the codebase.
optionExplanations.B: A PostToolUse hook running an import-sorting linter only catches the problem after the edit has already been applied, meaning the non-compliant change briefly exists before being caught.
optionExplanations.C: A custom slash command that sorts imports after each edit requires manual invocation, so it doesn't reliably enforce the ordering on every edit automatically.
optionExplanations.D: CLAUDE.md instructions describing the import order are guidance the model may or may not follow consistently, not a hard enforcement mechanism.

text: An NL-to-SQL agent with tool_choice 'auto' sometimes answers from training knowledge instead of querying; forcing the run_query tool makes it generate SQL even for follow-up clarifying questions. Best solution?

options.text: tool_choice 'auto' with a stronger system prompt to prefer queries
Force the run_query tool on the first turn only, then switch to 'auto' for subsequent turns
tool_choice 'any' so it always uses some tool
Add a 'clarify' tool and keep forced tool choice

optionExplanations.A: Using tool_choice 'auto' with a stronger system prompt is unreliable because the model can still choose to answer from training knowledge instead of querying, even with stronger wording.
optionExplanations.B: Forcing the run_query tool on the first turn guarantees the agent queries the database for the initial request, then switching to 'auto' for subsequent turns lets follow-up clarifying questions be handled naturally without being forced into generating unnecessary SQL.
optionExplanations.C: Setting tool_choice to 'any' forces some tool to be used on every turn, including turns that are really just clarifying questions, which is the same problem as the original forced-tool approach.
optionExplanations.D: Adding a 'clarify' tool while keeping forced tool choice is unnecessary added complexity when simply switching to 'auto' after the first turn already solves the problem.

text: An IDE extension must show Claude's reasoning while also getting a structured code suggestion. Can extended thinking and structured outputs be combined?

options.text: Yes — but only with json_object mode, not json_schema
No — they're mutually exclusive
Yes — enable thinking and set the response schema; thinking blocks show reasoning while the final output follows the schema
Yes — but only on Opus 4.6

optionExplanations.A: Extended thinking combined with structured outputs is not limited to json_object mode only; it also works with json_schema.
optionExplanations.B: Extended thinking and structured outputs are not mutually exclusive — they can be used together.
optionExplanations.C: Enabling thinking and setting the response schema together lets the model reason in thinking blocks while still emitting a final response that conforms to the schema, giving the IDE extension both visible reasoning and a reliable structured code suggestion.
optionExplanations.D: This combination is not restricted to Opus 4.6 only; it works across applicable models, not just one.

text: An agent extracting from a 500-page manual reliably gets details from the beginning and end but misses the middle. What document-processing strategy helps?

options.text: Increase max_tokens to process the full document
Use extended thinking to improve attention
Place important queries at the beginning, document after
Structure the prompt with the document/context at the top and the questions at the bottom (recommended long-context layout)

optionExplanations.A: Increasing max_tokens affects the output length budget, not how well the model attends to and extracts information from the middle of a long input document.
optionExplanations.B: Using extended thinking doesn't specifically fix attention placement issues caused by document structuring; it's a different feature aimed at reasoning depth.
optionExplanations.C: Placing important queries at the beginning with the document afterward reverses the recommended long-context layout and would likely worsen rather than fix the attention issue.
optionExplanations.D: Structuring the prompt with the document/context at the top and the questions at the bottom is the recommended long-context layout — it lets the model process the entire document before addressing the task, improving extraction accuracy even from the middle of long documents like a 500-page manual.

text: A security team wants to restrict which domains an HTTP-making MCP tool can contact. Where should this be enforced?

options.text: In the system prompt by listing allowed domains
In the MCP server with an allowlist, plus a PreToolUse hook validating the URL against allowed domains before execution
In the tool's description documenting allowed domains
In the API config via an mcp_domain_allowlist parameter

optionExplanations.A: Listing allowed domains in the system prompt is just guidance text that a model could be prompted around or fail to honor; it isn't actual enforcement.
optionExplanations.B: Domain restrictions must be enforced in code: a server-side allowlist in the MCP server that rejects unauthorized requests, combined with a PreToolUse hook that validates the URL against allowed domains before execution, provides real enforcement plus defense in depth.
optionExplanations.C: Documenting allowed domains in the tool's description is bypassable guidance, not actual access control.
optionExplanations.D: There is no mcp_domain_allowlist parameter in the API config; this isn't a real mechanism for enforcing domain restrictions.

text: After hours and hundreds of tool calls, Claude Code repeats earlier actions and re-makes previously fixed mistakes, suggesting lost context. What is happening?

options.text: The session exceeded the context window, triggering automatic compaction that lost some earlier detail
The model's weights degraded from prolonged use
Claude Code has a session timeout that resets context
The terminal history buffer overflowed

optionExplanations.A: Exceeding the context window triggers automatic compaction, and the resulting summary can drop specific earlier details, causing Claude to repeat earlier actions and re-make previously fixed mistakes after hours of work — the fix is persisting key state to scratchpad files.
optionExplanations.B: Model weights do not degrade from prolonged use within a session; this isn't a real phenomenon explaining the behavior.
optionExplanations.C: There is no session timeout in Claude Code that resets context independently of the compaction mechanism.
optionExplanations.D: A terminal history buffer overflow is unrelated to how Claude Code manages its own conversational context and isn't the cause described.

text: An architect wants a 'skills marketplace' to share reusable agent behaviors across projects. What is the architectural foundation?

options.text: Shared CLAUDE.md files in a central repository
SKILL.md files packaging prompt instructions, tool access, and variable interfaces into shareable units
MCP servers exposing skill endpoints
Custom slash commands encapsulating workflows

optionExplanations.A: Shared CLAUDE.md files in a central repository lack the structure for bundling tool access and variable interfaces, which a true shareable skill needs.
