optionExplanations.B: SKILL.md files package prompt instructions, tool access permissions, and variable/argument interfaces into self-contained, shareable units, making them the natural architectural foundation for a skills marketplace across projects.
optionExplanations.C: MCP servers exposing endpoints are designed for external service integration, not for packaging shareable prompt-and-tool-access behaviors as portable skills.
optionExplanations.D: Custom slash commands encapsulate workflows but don't capture the full behavior definition (tool access, variable interfaces, context injection) the way SKILL.md files do.

text: A hook meant to block dangerous file deletions never triggers; the developer's hook config matched a 'Delete' tool. What tool does Claude Code actually use for file deletion?

options.text: A dedicated Delete tool
A RemoveFile tool with confirmation prompts
A FileSystem.delete method
The Bash tool with an rm command — there is no dedicated delete tool

optionExplanations.A: There is no dedicated Delete tool in Claude Code, so a hook configured to match one would never trigger.
optionExplanations.B: There is no RemoveFile tool with confirmation prompts in Claude Code; this tool doesn't exist.
optionExplanations.C: There is no FileSystem.delete method used by Claude Code for file deletion.
optionExplanations.D: Claude Code deletes files via the Bash tool using commands like rm — there is no dedicated delete tool, so to gate deletions the hook should target PreToolUse on Bash and inspect the command for rm/delete patterns rather than matching a nonexistent 'Delete' tool.

text: A HIPAA-regulated agent's error messages sometimes include patient identifiers from the failed record. What design principle should apply?

options.text: Disable error messages entirely
Route all errors to a human to sanitize manually
Use structured error responses with errorCategory, isRetryable, and sanitized messages that never include raw input data
Encrypt error messages with an admin-only key

optionExplanations.A: Disabling error messages entirely would remove diagnostic information needed to recover from or understand failures, which is too drastic a fix.
optionExplanations.B: Routing all errors to a human to sanitize manually doesn't scale to a production HIPAA-regulated agent's error volume.
optionExplanations.C: Using structured error responses with an errorCategory, isRetryable flag, and sanitized messages that never include raw input data gives the agent the recovery information it needs (referencing records by a non-sensitive ID) without leaking patient identifiers into the conversation.
optionExplanations.D: Encrypting error messages with an admin-only key adds complexity but doesn't stop the underlying problem of raw patient identifiers being embedded in the message content that reaches the agent's context.

text: A CI pipeline needs strictly-JSON output for downstream tooling and must prevent Claude from reading any local config files. Which flags accomplish both?

options.text: --json and --no-config
--output-format json and --skip-config
--format=json and --headless
--json-schema with a schema, plus --bare (which disables CLAUDE.md/config loading)

optionExplanations.A: --json and --no-config are not the documented flag names for enforcing strict JSON output and disabling local config loading in Claude Code.
optionExplanations.B: --output-format json and --skip-config are not the actual documented flags for this purpose.
optionExplanations.C: --format=json and --headless are not the correct documented flags for enforcing schema-conforming JSON output and disabling local config discovery.
optionExplanations.D: --json-schema with a provided schema forces well-formed, schema-conforming JSON output for downstream tooling, and --bare disables auto-discovery of CLAUDE.md and other local configuration, together preventing Claude from reading local config files while guaranteeing strict JSON for the CI pipeline.

text: A security-analysis system uses effort='low' universally but wants deeper analysis on critical vulnerabilities. Recommended approach for varying effort by criticality?

options.text: Use effort='high' for everything and accept the cost
A routing layer that sets effort by severity: low for informational, medium for medium severity, high for critical
Use extended thinking for critical vulnerabilities instead of changing effort
Train a classifier to choose the effort level

optionExplanations.A: Using effort='high' for everything and accepting the cost wastes budget on informational and low-severity findings that don't need deep analysis.
optionExplanations.B: A routing layer that sets effort by severity — low for informational findings, medium for medium severity, and high for critical vulnerabilities — optimizes the cost/quality tradeoff by allocating cheap, fast processing where it's sufficient and deep analysis where it matters most.
optionExplanations.C: Using extended thinking for critical vulnerabilities instead of changing effort doesn't address the cost allocation problem across the full range of severities the way a severity-based effort routing layer does.
optionExplanations.D: Training a classifier to choose the effort level adds unnecessary complexity when the severity classification is already known and can be mapped directly to an effort level.

text: A researcher subagent gets 'stuck' in recursive web searches, making dozens of tool calls without converging. What architectural pattern prevents this?

options.text: Set an iteration limit for the researcher and require it to report findings at the limit even if incomplete
Increase the researcher's context window
Remove the researcher and have the main agent do research directly
A PostToolUse hook that terminates after exactly 10 tool calls

optionExplanations.A: Setting an iteration limit for the researcher and requiring it to report findings at the limit even if incomplete forces convergence and bounds runaway exploration, preventing the dozens of unconverging recursive search calls described.
optionExplanations.B: Increasing the researcher's context window doesn't make it converge faster or stop the recursive searching; it just allows more room for the same unbounded behavior.
optionExplanations.C: Removing the researcher subagent and having the main agent do research directly just centralizes the same unbounded recursive-search problem rather than fixing it.
optionExplanations.D: A hard cutoff after exactly 10 tool calls via a PostToolUse hook is too rigid, since varied research tasks may legitimately need more or fewer calls than a fixed number.

text: An app sends 10,000 messages/hour with an identical 5,000-token system prompt. With automatic caching, roughly how much is saved on the system-prompt portion vs. uncached?

options.text: ~90% — cache reads cost about 10% of base input price
~50% — cached reads are half price
~10% — caching is a marginal reduction
100% — cached tokens are free

optionExplanations.A: Cache reads cost roughly 10% of the base input price, meaning the cached system-prompt portion is roughly 90% cheaper than uncached processing, with the small write premium amortized across the 10,000 messages per hour that reuse it.
optionExplanations.B: Cached reads are not simply half price; the actual savings are much larger, around 90%, not 50%.
optionExplanations.C: Caching is not a marginal 10% reduction in cost — the actual reduction on the cached portion is around 90%, not 10%.
optionExplanations.D: Cached tokens are not completely free; they cost roughly 10% of the base input price, not 0%.

text: A 'turn' in the Agent SDK makes multiple Claude API calls with tool use. Which metric counts the agent turns within an API call?

options.text: Number of API calls times input tokens
Number of tool_use blocks across all responses
The usage.iterations field, which counts agent turns within an API call
Total output tokens divided by average tokens per turn

optionExplanations.A: Multiplying the number of API calls by input tokens doesn't correspond to a metric for counting agent turns within an API call.
optionExplanations.B: Counting tool_use blocks across all responses measures the number of tool calls made, not the number of agent turns (iterations) within a single API call loop.
optionExplanations.C: The usage.iterations field is the metric that counts the agent turns (model invocations) within an API call's internal loop, making it the correct metric for this purpose.
optionExplanations.D: Dividing total output tokens by average tokens per turn is only an approximation and not the actual metric the SDK reports for counting turns.

text: A structured-output pipeline extracting from papers needs Claude to indicate uncertainty. Required fields are title, authors, abstract. How should uncertain fields be handled?

options.text: Make all fields optional so Claude can omit uncertain ones
Add a confidence score alongside each field
Use a nullable structured pattern per field: {value: string|null, confidence: 'high'|'medium'|'low', reasoning: string}
Let Claude add uncertainty notes in an explanation field

optionExplanations.A: Making all fields optional so Claude can omit uncertain ones would lose the guarantee that required fields like title, authors, and abstract are always present in the output.
optionExplanations.B: Adding a bare confidence score alongside each field doesn't specify what the actual extracted value would be when uncertain, missing useful information.
optionExplanations.C: Using a nullable structured pattern per field — {value: string|null, confidence: 'high'|'medium'|'low', reasoning: string} — preserves the required-field schema while letting the model express uncertainty clearly and explain its reasoning for each field individually.
optionExplanations.D: Letting Claude add uncertainty notes in a free-text explanation field lacks the structure needed to programmatically associate confidence with each specific required field.

text: A team wants to test that their agent handles a rare MCP tool timeout (occurs ~1 in 10,000 in production). How can they reliably test it?

options.text: Send 10,000 test requests and wait for it to occur naturally
Test only the happy path and monitor for the timeout in production
Set the MCP server timeout to 1ms to force frequent timeouts
Mock the MCP tool to return a timeout error and verify the agent's retry/fallback behavior

optionExplanations.A: Sending 10,000 test requests and waiting for the rare timeout to occur naturally is wasteful and unreliable, since the failure might not occur even across that many attempts.
optionExplanations.B: Testing only the happy path and monitoring for the timeout in production skips testing the actual retry/fallback behavior altogether, leaving it unverified until it happens for real.
optionExplanations.C: Setting the MCP server timeout to 1ms to force frequent timeouts can cause unrelated cascading failures elsewhere in the system, making it an unreliable way to isolate just the timeout-handling behavior.
optionExplanations.D: Mocking the MCP tool to deterministically return a timeout error lets the team reliably and repeatedly test the agent's retry, fallback, and error-messaging behavior on demand, without waiting for the rare real-world occurrence.

text: An agent manages a 12-state order-processing state machine, and standard compaction loses the current-state tracking. What configuration preserves the state-machine context through compaction?

options.text: Increase the context window to avoid triggering compaction
Store the current state in a scratchpad file referenced after compaction
Use custom compaction instructions that explicitly preserve current state, valid transitions, and pending actions
Disable compaction and manually truncate old messages

optionExplanations.A: Increasing the context window only delays when compaction is triggered; it doesn't actually solve the problem of state being lost once compaction does occur.
optionExplanations.B: Storing the current state in a scratchpad file referenced after compaction is a reasonable alternative that works, but it adds file I/O overhead compared to handling preservation within the compaction mechanism itself.
optionExplanations.C: Using custom compaction instructions that explicitly require the summarizer to preserve the current state, valid transitions, and pending actions keeps the 12-state order-processing state machine's critical context intact through compaction, directly solving the problem within the compaction mechanism.
optionExplanations.D: Disabling compaction and manually truncating old messages is fragile and risks losing old context entirely rather than preserving the specific state-machine information needed.

text: A legal-review agent loads an 80,000-token regulatory document plus legal documents of ~20,000 tokens each, in a 200,000-token context window, and wants to batch documents efficiently with room for the system prompt and Claude's output. How many documents per batch is the safe choice?

options.text: One document at a time to maximize quality
Three per batch: 80K + 3×20K = 140K, leaving comfortable room for system prompt and output
Five per batch: 80K + 5×20K = 180K (plus overhead ≈190K)
Ten per batch by loading only relevant regulation sections per document

optionExplanations.A: One document at a time underuses the 200K context window, leaving most of the room idle and making batching far less efficient than necessary.
optionExplanations.B: Three documents per batch uses 80K (fixed regulatory text) plus 3x20K = 140K, leaving comfortable headroom within the 200K window for the system prompt and Claude's output — the safe, correct choice.
optionExplanations.C: Five documents per batch uses 80K + 5x20K = 180K, and with overhead this reaches roughly 190K, leaving too little room once the system prompt and output space are reserved.
optionExplanations.D: Loading only relevant regulation sections per document to fit ten documents risks missing cross-references in the full regulatory text, which is unsafe for thorough legal review.

text: A CRM MCP server's agent often calls search_contacts before every update, even when the contact ID is already known from a previous result. What design change reduces these calls?

options.text: Add a cache layer in the server that returns the last search result automatically
Return the contact ID prominently in all tool results, and update descriptions to note that search_contacts is unnecessary when the ID is already available
Remove search_contacts and require callers to provide IDs
Rate-limit search_contacts to once per conversation

optionExplanations.A: Adding a hidden cache layer in the server adds complexity and doesn't change the model's behavior of calling search_contacts redundantly, since Claude still doesn't know the ID is already available.
optionExplanations.B: Returning the contact_id prominently in all tool results and updating tool descriptions to note that search_contacts is unnecessary when the ID is already known teaches Claude to reuse identifiers, directly eliminating the redundant lookups.
optionExplanations.C: Removing search_contacts entirely breaks a capability the agent legitimately needs when it doesn't already have a contact ID.
optionExplanations.D: Rate-limiting search_contacts to once per conversation is too blunt an instrument and would block legitimate searches later in a long conversation.

text: A developer wants a custom /generate-tests slash command that accepts a file path and a coverage target. How is it defined?

options.text: A shell script in .claude/commands/ named after the command
Registered in CLAUDE.md under a commands: section
A .claude/commands/generate-tests.md file whose prompt template uses $ARGUMENTS for the parameters
A TypeScript function in .claude/plugins/

optionExplanations.A: Slash commands are not shell scripts; they are markdown files, so a shell script in .claude/commands/ is not the correct mechanism.
optionExplanations.B: Slash commands are not registered via a commands: section in CLAUDE.md; that file is for general project instructions, not command definitions.
optionExplanations.C: A custom slash command is defined as a .claude/commands/generate-tests.md file, where the filename becomes the command name and the file body is a prompt template that uses $ARGUMENTS to receive the file path and coverage target parameters.
optionExplanations.D: Slash commands don't require a TypeScript function in .claude/plugins/; that is not how custom commands are defined.

text: A multi-model complaint-handling system can route to Opus (thorough, expensive) or Haiku (fast, cheap). Which routing strategy is recommended?

options.text: Always use Opus for customer-facing interactions
Randomly distribute requests to balance load
Use the cheap model for everything and escalate to Opus only on low confidence
Haiku for classification/routing, Opus for the customer-facing response, Haiku for logging and follow-up actions

optionExplanations.A: Always using Opus for customer-facing interactions wastes budget on trivial steps like classification and routing that don't need the expensive model.
optionExplanations.B: Randomly distributing requests ignores actual quality requirements and would route complex tasks to the cheap model and trivial tasks to the expensive one arbitrarily.
optionExplanations.C: Using the cheap model for everything and escalating only on low self-reported confidence is risky because model self-confidence is poorly calibrated, so real quality issues can be missed.
optionExplanations.D: Using Haiku for classification and routing, Opus for the customer-facing response generation, and Haiku again for logging and follow-up actions matches model cost/capability to the quality requirement of each step — cheap models for low-stakes steps, the expensive model only where it matters most.

text: A schema uses anyOf to allow a measurement to be either {value, unit} or {range:{min,max}, unit}. Validation sometimes fails. What is the likely issue / fix?

options.text: The union exceeds the maximum number of types allowed in strict mode
anyOf isn't supported in Claude's structured output schema
Both members share a 'unit' field, causing ambiguity, so the members must be made fully distinct
anyOf members should include a discriminator field to help the model pick the right variant unambiguously

optionExplanations.A: Two anyOf variants do not exceed any maximum-type cap in strict mode; that isn't a real constraint being hit here.
optionExplanations.B: anyOf is supported in Claude's structured output schema, so this isn't the source of the validation failures.
optionExplanations.C: Requiring the members to share no fields at all (full mutual exclusivity) is impractical here since both legitimately need a 'unit' field, and it isn't the recommended fix compared to using a discriminator — though it's a defensible runner-up.
optionExplanations.D: The anyOf variants overlap in shape, making it ambiguous which one applies; adding a discriminator field lets both the model and the validator unambiguously pick the correct variant, which is the standard fix.

text: A financial agent asked to 'compare Apple and Microsoft's last quarter' immediately starts fetching Apple data only. What orchestration improvement ensures balanced analysis?

options.text: Ask the user which company to analyze first
Queue sequentially: complete Apple, then Microsoft, then compare
Use parallel subagents — one per company — that fetch and analyze simultaneously, then combine
Fetch all data for both companies in a single tool call

optionExplanations.A: Asking the user which company to analyze first is unnecessary friction when the task already specifies both companies should be compared.
optionExplanations.B: Completing Apple's analysis fully before starting Microsoft's introduces order bias, where the second company's analysis is colored by having just analyzed the first.
optionExplanations.C: Using parallel subagents — one per company — to fetch and analyze simultaneously avoids order bias entirely since neither analysis influences the other, and the results are combined only at the end for a balanced comparison.
optionExplanations.D: Fetching all data for both companies in a single tool call may not be supported by the available tools and doesn't by itself ensure the analysis treats both companies evenhandedly.

text: A compliance agent records violations with a severity field, currently any string. With strict-mode json_schema, what improves reliability?

options.text: Use a regex pattern on the severity string
Use an enum: ['critical','high','medium','low'] to constrain the value
Normalize severity values in the application layer
Add a description explaining the expected values

optionExplanations.A: A regex pattern on the severity string may not be reliably enforced by the model the way a strict-mode constraint is.
optionExplanations.B: Using an enum constrains the severity value to exactly ['critical','high','medium','low'] under strict mode, eliminating inconsistent variants like 'High', 'HIGH', or 'critical-level' by construction.
optionExplanations.C: Normalizing severity values in the application layer is extra code that reacts after the fact rather than preventing inconsistent values from being generated in the first place.
optionExplanations.D: Adding a description explaining expected values is only guidance to the model, not an enforced constraint, so it doesn't guarantee reliability the way an enum does.

text: An Agent SDK tool database_query sometimes returns 100K+ tokens, and the agent's reasoning quality drops as the result dominates context. What pattern addresses this?

options.text: Implement pagination — return the first page plus a total count and a next_page token
Increase max_tokens so Claude has room after the large result
gzip-compress the result before returning it
Filter the result set in the application layer before the agent sees it

optionExplanations.A: Implementing pagination — returning the first page plus a total count and a next_page token — keeps the tool result small and manageable, preserving context space for the agent's reasoning instead of letting a 100K+ token result dominate it.
optionExplanations.B: Increasing max_tokens affects how much output Claude can generate, not the size of the oversized tool result already bloating the input context.
optionExplanations.C: gzip-compressing the result isn't usable in a tool result, since Claude needs to read the text content directly, not a binary-compressed blob.
optionExplanations.D: Filtering the result set in the application layer before the agent sees it may drop data the agent actually needs and removes the agent's control over what to fetch.

text: 15 developers want different code-style-feedback verbosity without per-developer CLAUDE.md files in the repo. How?

options.text: Each developer passes --style flags when starting Claude Code
A CLAUDE.md branch per developer in git
Environment variables controlling verbosity per developer
Each developer configures personal preferences in their user-level CLAUDE.md (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md)

optionExplanations.A: There is no standard --style flag mechanism for configuring per-developer code-style-feedback verbosity in Claude Code.
optionExplanations.B: Maintaining a separate CLAUDE.md branch per developer in git pollutes the repository with personal configuration that doesn't belong in shared version history.
optionExplanations.C: There isn't a real mechanism of environment variables controlling feedback verbosity per developer in Claude Code.
optionExplanations.D: Each developer configuring personal preferences in their user-level CLAUDE.md (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md) keeps individual verbosity/style preferences out of the repo entirely, while the project CLAUDE.md continues to hold the shared team standards.

text: A developer sees a 'redacted_thinking' block instead of actual thinking content, using the API with a valid key. What causes this?

options.text: Safety classifiers determined the thinking content was potentially sensitive and redacted it; thinking still occurred and influenced the output
Extended thinking is in beta and needs a special feature flag
The API key lacks permission to view thinking content
The model version doesn't support transparent thinking

optionExplanations.A: Redacted thinking appears when internal safety classifiers determine the model's reasoning content was potentially sensitive and redact it from view; the thinking still occurred and still influenced the final output, it's just not shown in raw form.
optionExplanations.B: This isn't caused by extended thinking being in beta or requiring a special feature flag; redaction is a content-safety behavior, not a feature-availability issue.
optionExplanations.C: This isn't an API key permission issue — there's no key-level permission that controls visibility of thinking content.
optionExplanations.D: This isn't a model-version limitation; transparent thinking is supported, but specific content can still be redacted by safety classifiers regardless of model version.

text: A task needs code written to a specific file and an explanation shown in the chat. In Claude Code, how should this dual-output workflow be structured?

options.text: Generate both in a single prompt and let Claude decide formatting
Two separate conversations: one for code, one for explanation
Use the Write tool for the code file and include the explanation in Claude's text response — Claude naturally combines tool use with text
Use a PostToolUse hook to capture the generated code and redirect it to a file

optionExplanations.A: Generating both in a single freeform prompt and letting Claude decide formatting lacks a reliable mechanism for guaranteeing the code actually lands in a specific file.
optionExplanations.B: Using two separate conversations for the code and the explanation would lose the shared context connecting the two, making the explanation less grounded in the actual generated code.
optionExplanations.C: Using the Write tool to create the code file while including the explanation in Claude's accompanying text response works naturally, since Claude Code turns naturally combine tool use with text output in the same turn.
optionExplanations.D: Using a PostToolUse hook to capture generated code and redirect it to a file is needlessly complex when the Write tool can directly write the file as part of the normal turn.

text: An agent scores its own outputs 8–9 across accuracy, helpfulness, and tone every time. What bias is this and how should it be addressed?

options.text: Self-review bias — use a separate evaluation agent with a rubric containing calibration examples across the full score range
Anchoring bias — use randomized starting scores
Recency bias — evaluate in random order
Confirmation bias — remove the scoring labels

optionExplanations.A: This is self-review bias — an agent grading its own output tends to inflate scores; the fix is to use a separate evaluation agent with a rubric containing calibration examples spanning the full score range (e.g., what 2/10, 5/10, and 10/10 look like) so the scale is properly anchored.
optionExplanations.B: This isn't anchoring bias, and randomizing starting scores wouldn't address the real problem, which is the agent evaluating its own work rather than an external anchor point.
optionExplanations.C: This isn't recency bias, and evaluating in random order doesn't address an agent's tendency to inflate scores when reviewing its own output.
optionExplanations.D: This isn't confirmation bias, and removing scoring labels wouldn't fix the underlying self-evaluation inflation problem.

text: A response has stop_reason 'tool_use', but the developer only handles text blocks. What must change to support tool use?

options.text: Parse the text block for a special embedded tool-call syntax
Make a separate API call to a tool-execution endpoint with the response ID
Check content blocks for type 'tool_use', extract name/id/input, execute the tool, then send a tool_result back in the next request
Set auto_execute_tools: true in the initial request

optionExplanations.A: Tool calls are not embedded as special syntax inside text blocks; they arrive as distinct structured content blocks, so parsing text for embedded syntax is the wrong approach.
optionExplanations.B: There is no separate tool-execution API endpoint that takes a response ID; tool execution happens entirely on the developer's side using the returned tool_use information.
optionExplanations.C: The correct change is to check content blocks for type 'tool_use', extract the name, id, and input, execute the corresponding tool locally, and then send a tool_result block (matched to that id) back in the next request — this is required to properly support the 'tool_use' stop_reason.
optionExplanations.D: There is no auto_execute_tools flag; Claude's API does not automatically execute tools on the developer's behalf.

text: A multi-tenant SaaS has a 3,000-token shared system prompt plus 1,000–5,000 tokens of tenant-specific config. How should prompts be structured for cost efficiency?

options.text: Cache the shared portion as the first breakpoint (shared across all tenants), then the tenant-specific portion as a second breakpoint (shared across that tenant's requests)
Concatenate shared and tenant prompts into one system message per request
Store tenant config in an MCP tool Claude calls at the start of each conversation
Use a different API key per tenant

optionExplanations.A: Caching the shared 3,000-token portion as the first breakpoint (reused across all tenants) and the tenant-specific 1,000-5,000 token portion as a second breakpoint (reused across that tenant's requests) maximizes cache reuse at both the global and per-tenant level, minimizing recomputation cost.
optionExplanations.B: Concatenating shared and tenant prompts into a single system message without separate cache breakpoints misses the opportunity for cross-tenant caching of the shared portion.
optionExplanations.C: Storing tenant config in an MCP tool that Claude calls at the start of each conversation adds latency from the extra tool round trip rather than improving cost efficiency.
optionExplanations.D: Using a different API key per tenant doesn't reduce token cost at all; caching is what reduces cost, and key choice is unrelated to prompt caching behavior.

text: An agentic loop executes tool_use blocks one at a time and is slow when Claude requests multiple tools in a single response. What optimization should be implemented?

options.text: Execute independent tool calls in parallel and return all tool_results together
Limit Claude to one tool call per response via tool_choice
Queue tool calls and batch them on a fixed schedule
Increase the API timeout to accommodate sequential execution

optionExplanations.A: Executing independent tool calls in parallel and returning all their tool_results together in the next message cuts end-to-end latency directly, addressing the slowness from sequential one-at-a-time execution.
optionExplanations.B: Limiting Claude to one tool call per response via tool_choice handicaps the model's ability to request multiple tools when it legitimately needs them, and doesn't solve the underlying latency issue.
optionExplanations.C: Queuing tool calls and batching them on a fixed schedule adds artificial latency waiting for the schedule rather than executing calls as soon as they're available.
optionExplanations.D: Increasing the API timeout doesn't address the root cause of slowness — it just tolerates a slow sequential execution pattern for longer.

text: A report generator must always include exactly 5 sections: Executive Summary, Methodology, Findings, Recommendations, Appendix. Which approach guarantees this structure?

options.text: json_schema strict mode with section titles as an enum and the array constrained to minItems: 5, maxItems: 5
Few-shot examples showing all 5 sections
System-prompt instructions listing the 5 required sections
Post-processing that checks for 5 sections and retries if any are missing

optionExplanations.A: Using json_schema strict mode with the five section titles defined as an enum, combined with array constraints minItems: 5 and maxItems: 5, deterministically guarantees the output always contains exactly the five required sections.
optionExplanations.B: Few-shot examples showing all five sections only probabilistically encourage the right structure; they don't guarantee it the way schema-level constraints do.
optionExplanations.C: System-prompt instructions listing the five required sections are also probabilistic guidance and can still be missed by the model.
optionExplanations.D: Post-processing that checks for the five sections and retries if any are missing can catch problems, but it wastes API calls compared to a schema that guarantees correctness up front.

text: A new MCP server is added to .claude/settings.json but its tools don't appear. Most likely issue?

options.text: The server must be registered in CLAUDE.md to be discoverable
New MCP tools need explicit permission (an allowedTools pattern); by default they require approval
MCP config should be in settings.local.json for local servers
Claude Code must be restarted to pick up new MCP configs

optionExplanations.A: MCP servers are not registered in CLAUDE.md for discoverability; that file is unrelated to MCP tool registration.
optionExplanations.B: Claude Code gates new MCP tools behind an allowlist as a safeguard against untrusted tools running automatically — until the tools are permitted via an allowedTools pattern or explicitly approved, they won't appear or be usable, which is the most likely cause here.
optionExplanations.C: Whether MCP config lives in settings.local.json versus settings.json is about configuration scope and sensitivity, not about whether the tools are visible or gated by permission.
optionExplanations.D: A restart might incidentally help in some cases, but it isn't the core cause of new MCP tools not appearing — the permission gate is.

text: A supervisor agent aggregates results from worker agents that sometimes conflict. How should it handle conflicting worker outputs?

options.text: Always trust the worker that responded first
Average the conflicting values for consensus
Discard all conflicting results and re-run the whole workflow
Structured conflict handling: compare results, request clarification from specific workers, and apply domain-specific resolution rules

optionExplanations.A: Always trusting whichever worker responded first is arbitrary and ignores which result is actually more accurate or authoritative.
optionExplanations.B: Averaging conflicting values only works for numeric data and can fabricate a value that no worker actually produced, rather than resolving the conflict meaningfully.
optionExplanations.C: Discarding all conflicting results and re-running the entire workflow wastes resources when only the specific conflicting parts need re-examination.
optionExplanations.D: Structured conflict handling — comparing the results, requesting clarification from the specific workers involved, and applying domain-specific resolution rules (such as preferring the more authoritative or recent source) — resolves conflicts deliberately rather than arbitrarily.

text: A structured output sometimes returns 0 items when the input genuinely has no matching data, but downstream treats an empty array as an error. How should the schema distinguish 'no data' from 'extraction error'?

options.text: Use minItems: 1 with a placeholder 'no data' item
Add a boolean found and only populate items when found is true
Return null instead of an empty array for 'no data'
Add a status field: {status: 'success'|'no_data'|'error', items: [...], errorMessage?: string}

optionExplanations.A: Using minItems: 1 with a placeholder 'no data' item fabricates data that doesn't actually exist, polluting downstream consumers with a fake entry.
optionExplanations.B: A boolean found field is less expressive than a full status enum, since it can't cleanly represent a genuine error state separately from 'no data found'.
optionExplanations.C: Returning null instead of an empty array doesn't separate a genuine 'error' condition from a legitimate 'absence of data' condition — both would look the same to downstream code.
optionExplanations.D: Adding an explicit status field with values 'success', 'no_data', or 'error', alongside items and an optional errorMessage, cleanly distinguishes successful extraction with results, genuine absence of data, and actual extraction errors, giving downstream consumers clear semantics.

text: A CI code review in headless mode should focus only on security issues, but 'Only report security vulnerabilities' in the prompt isn't always honored. What additional headless configuration improves consistency?

options.text: Filter out non-security findings in post-processing
Use a --security-only flag
Set effort='low' to reduce analysis breadth
Add --append-system-prompt 'Focus exclusively on security vulnerabilities; do not report style, performance, or other issues.'

optionExplanations.A: Filtering out non-security findings in post-processing is reactive — it still required the model to generate and reason about irrelevant findings in the first place.
optionExplanations.B: There is no --security-only flag in Claude Code's headless mode.
optionExplanations.C: Setting effort='low' to reduce analysis breadth could cause the review to miss real security issues, which directly undermines the review's purpose.
optionExplanations.D: Adding --append-system-prompt with an instruction like 'Focus exclusively on security vulnerabilities; do not report style, performance, or other issues' reinforces the focus with an authoritative system-level instruction, improving consistency for this narrow-scope headless task.

text: A batch of 10,000 requests is submitted via the Message Batches API. How do you retrieve results for a specific request?

options.text: Poll the batch status until it ends, then download a results file where each line is a result indexed by custom_id
Each request returns its result immediately while the batch processes
Query a GET endpoint with batch_id and custom_id for an individual result
Results are sent to a webhook configured at submission

optionExplanations.A: Batches are processed asynchronously: you poll the batch status until processing_status is ended, then download the results file where each line is a result identified by its custom_id, which is how you retrieve the result for a specific request.
optionExplanations.B: Individual results are not returned immediately as the batch processes; results only become available once the batch completes.
optionExplanations.C: There is no per-item GET endpoint that retrieves an individual result by batch_id and custom_id; results come from the completed results file.
optionExplanations.D: Batch results aren't delivered via a webhook configured at submission; you retrieve them by downloading the results file after polling shows the batch has ended.

text: Extended thinking with a fixed 10,000-token budget is sometimes insufficient for complex proofs; raising the budget increases cost on every request. What is the recommended approach to optimize thinking depth vs. cost?

options.text: Set the budget to the maximum for all requests
Use adaptive thinking, which dynamically allocates thinking depth based on task complexity
Start with a low budget and retry with higher budgets only when output quality is insufficient
Disable thinking and rely on few-shot examples for complex reasoning

optionExplanations.A: Setting the budget to the maximum for all requests wastes tokens and money on simple tasks that don't need deep reasoning.
optionExplanations.B: Adaptive thinking dynamically allocates thinking depth based on task complexity, letting the model spend little on simple tasks and more on genuinely hard ones — this is the recommended approach to balance thinking depth against cost instead of a fixed budget.
optionExplanations.C: Starting with a low budget and retrying with higher budgets only when output quality is insufficient adds latency from repeated retries compared to adaptive allocation.
optionExplanations.D: Disabling thinking and relying on few-shot examples loses the reasoning quality benefits needed for complex proofs.

text: A travel chatbot's system prompt says to recommend partner airlines/hotels 'without being obvious'. An audit flags this as potentially deceptive. Per usage policies, what should they do?

options.text: Continue, since system prompts aren't visible to users
Disclose to users that the chatbot may prioritize partner services, and frame recommendations transparently
Remove the preference entirely and recommend on merit
Move the preference into tool descriptions so it's less visible

optionExplanations.A: Continuing because system prompts aren't visible to users ignores the actual ethical issue — hidden commercial bias is deceptive regardless of whether the user can see the prompt that causes it.
optionExplanations.B: Disclosing to users that the chatbot may prioritize partner services and framing recommendations transparently directly addresses the deceptive-practice concern, since transparency about behavior that affects user decisions is required when there's a hidden commercial preference.
optionExplanations.C: Removing the preference entirely and recommending purely on merit may be more than what's required if disclosed partner promotion is otherwise acceptable; transparency is the targeted fix.
optionExplanations.D: Moving the preference into tool descriptions so it's less visible makes the deceptive behavior harder to audit rather than addressing the transparency problem.

text: An agent uses tool_choice {type:'tool', name:'analyze'} with extended thinking enabled and gets an error. What constraint is being hit?

options.text: Extended thinking is incompatible with any tool_choice setting
'analyze' is a reserved keyword conflicting with thinking mode
Extended thinking with forced (specific) tool_choice isn't allowed — use 'auto' or 'any'... actually only 'auto' or 'none'
Extended thinking requires tool_choice 'none'

optionExplanations.A: Extended thinking is not incompatible with all tool_choice settings — it works fine with non-forcing options like 'auto'.
optionExplanations.B: 'analyze' is not a reserved keyword; the issue is the forced tool_choice mode itself, not the specific tool name chosen.
optionExplanations.C: Extended thinking with a forced, specific tool_choice (naming a particular tool) isn't allowed; only non-forcing settings such as 'auto' (or 'none' to disable tools) are supported alongside extended thinking, so forcing a named tool like 'analyze' triggers this error.
optionExplanations.D: Extended thinking does not require tool_choice 'none' — that setting would disable tool use entirely, which is not the actual requirement; 'auto' also works.

text: A developer suspects Claude Code made an unnecessary file read before editing and wants to review the last interaction's token usage and tool-call sequence. Which feature helps?

options.text: Check terminal history for command output logs
Review a .claude/logs/ directory for interaction traces
Enable a --debug verbose mode
Use /cost to review token usage from the last interaction

optionExplanations.A: Terminal history only shows command output logs, not a structured breakdown of token usage or the tool-call sequence for the interaction.
optionExplanations.B: There is no standard .claude/logs/ directory that stores interaction traces for this purpose.
optionExplanations.C: A --debug verbose mode is not the standard feature for reviewing token usage and tool-call sequences after the fact.
optionExplanations.D: Using /cost surfaces token usage from the last interaction, which is the standard feature that helps spot wasteful operations like an unnecessary file read and optimize cost.

text: A payment agent retries a timed-out payment call and causes a duplicate charge. What pattern prevents duplicate transactions?

options.text: Increase the timeout to avoid timeouts
Check the account balance before retrying
Use idempotency keys so a retry produces the same result as the original call
Add a 30-second delay between retries

optionExplanations.A: Increasing the timeout to avoid timeouts doesn't prevent duplicate charges caused by network-level retries after a timeout — it just changes how often timeouts happen.
optionExplanations.B: Checking the account balance before retrying has a race condition and doesn't prevent the underlying duplicate operation from being submitted to the payment gateway.
optionExplanations.C: Using idempotency keys lets the payment gateway recognize a retried request as the same operation and return the same result instead of processing it twice, which directly prevents duplicate charges on timeout-triggered retries.
optionExplanations.D: Adding a delay between retries doesn't guarantee safety against duplicate charges — it only changes timing, not whether the gateway treats the retry as a new transaction.

text: A database-query MCP tool can return 500 rows (~200K tokens). Following MCP design best practices, what should the tool return?

options.text: A summary with row count, column names, the first ~10 rows as examples, and a pagination token to fetch more
The full 200K-token result for completeness
Only the first row plus the total count
A compressed binary representation

optionExplanations.A: Returning a summary with row count, column names, the first ~10 rows as examples, and a pagination token to fetch more gives a 'just right' response — enough metadata and sample data to reason about the result without flooding context, following MCP design best practices.
optionExplanations.B: Returning the full 200K-token result wastes context space and degrades the agent's reasoning quality, which is exactly the problem this guidance aims to avoid.
optionExplanations.C: Returning only the first row plus the total count is too sparse to give Claude meaningful insight into the shape of the data.
optionExplanations.D: A compressed binary representation isn't processable by the model, which needs readable text content to reason about the result.

text: A team wants Claude Code to generate commit messages in their conventional-commits format (type(scope): description). Which configuration approach is foundational?

options.text: A Git prepare-commit-msg hook that invokes Claude Code
A custom /commit slash command that generates and commits
Instructions in CLAUDE.md specifying the conventional-commits format with examples
A PostToolUse hook on Bash intercepting git commit

optionExplanations.A: A Git prepare-commit-msg hook that invokes Claude Code can supplement the workflow but isn't the foundational mechanism for teaching Claude the desired commit format.
optionExplanations.B: A custom /commit slash command that generates and commits can build on the convention but isn't itself the foundational place where the format convention is defined.
optionExplanations.C: Instructions in CLAUDE.md specifying the conventional-commits format (type(scope): description) with examples is the foundational mechanism, since CLAUDE.md is where behavioral conventions like commit-message format are defined and Claude follows them whenever it writes commit messages.
optionExplanations.D: A PostToolUse hook on Bash intercepting git commit can enforce or adjust commits after the fact, but it isn't the base mechanism for establishing the format convention itself.

text: An architect sets budget_tokens: 50000 but Claude usually uses only ~2,000 thinking tokens with high-quality results, and worries about wasted budget. Is there an issue?

options.text: Yes — reduce budget_tokens to 5,000 to prevent waste
No — budget_tokens is a maximum, not an allocation; Claude uses only what it needs and you're billed only for tokens actually generated
Yes — unused budget_tokens are still billed at the thinking rate
No — but the high budget prevents optimal request batching

optionExplanations.A: Reducing budget_tokens to 5,000 risks starving genuinely complex tasks that might occasionally need more thinking room, when there's no actual waste to prevent.
optionExplanations.B: There is no issue: budget_tokens is a maximum cap, not a pre-allocation — Claude uses only the thinking tokens it actually needs, and billing is based only on tokens actually generated, so a high cap on simple tasks costs nothing extra.
optionExplanations.C: Unused budget_tokens are not billed at the thinking rate; you're only billed for tokens actually generated, so there's no hidden cost from an unused high cap.
optionExplanations.D: A high budget_tokens cap does not prevent optimal request batching; the two are unrelated, since the budget is just an upper bound, not a scheduling constraint.

text: A refund agent must require manager approval for refunds over $100. The current architecture uses direct tool calls. Recommended implementation?

options.text: A system-prompt instruction: 'For refunds over $100, ask the customer to contact a manager'
A PreToolUse hook on refund_payment that checks the amount and blocks when it exceeds $100, returning a message that approval is required
A separate manager_approve tool that must be called before refund_payment
A $100 limit in the refund_payment parameter validation schema

optionExplanations.A: A system-prompt instruction asking the customer to contact a manager is probabilistic and doesn't reliably prevent the refund_payment tool from being called for amounts over $100.
optionExplanations.B: A PreToolUse hook on refund_payment that checks the amount and deterministically blocks calls exceeding $100, returning an explanatory message that approval is required, provides enforcement that prompt instructions can't guarantee.
optionExplanations.C: A separate manager_approve tool that must be called first complicates the agent's flow and still relies on the agent reliably calling it before refund_payment, rather than enforcing the rule structurally.
optionExplanations.D: A $100 limit in the refund_payment parameter validation schema would reject malformed input but doesn't implement an actual approval workflow for legitimate refund requests over $100.

text: Generating TypeScript interfaces, a schema has both a free-text 'code' string and a structured 'interfaces' array describing the same thing. What issue might arise?

options.text: The schema is too complex for strict mode
JSON schema can't contain both string and array fields
TypeScript code in a JSON string always has escaping issues that make it invalid
The code string and the interfaces array may become inconsistent — representing the same information twice risks divergence

optionExplanations.A: Schema complexity for strict mode isn't the core issue here; the schema can validly contain both a string and an array field.
optionExplanations.B: JSON schema can absolutely contain both string and array fields together; that isn't a real limitation.
optionExplanations.C: TypeScript code embedded in a JSON string doesn't inherently always have escaping issues that make it invalid; escaping is manageable with proper JSON encoding.
optionExplanations.D: Representing the same information twice — once as free-text 'code' and once as a structured 'interfaces' array — risks the two becoming inconsistent over time, since there's no guarantee they stay in sync; a single structured representation that generates the code is the better design.

text: An architect wants a circuit-breaker for an agent's external tool calls: after 3 consecutive failures, stop calling for 5 minutes. Where is this best implemented?

options.text: In the MCP server, tracking failure counts and timestamps and short-circuiting when the breaker is open
In the system prompt: 'If a tool fails 3 times, stop for 5 minutes'
In a PreToolUse hook that tracks failure history and blocks when open
In orchestrator code with tool-call interception logic

optionExplanations.A: Implementing the circuit breaker in the MCP server, tracking failure counts and timestamps and short-circuiting calls when the breaker is open, places the reliability logic in the tool layer where it belongs, encapsulating it close to the calls it protects.
optionExplanations.B: A system-prompt rule like 'if a tool fails 3 times, stop for 5 minutes' is probabilistic and doesn't reliably enforce the circuit-breaker behavior.
optionExplanations.C: A PreToolUse hook that tracks failure history and blocks when open can work, but it's less encapsulated than putting the logic directly in the MCP server that owns the failing tool calls.
optionExplanations.D: Implementing this in orchestrator code with tool-call interception logic mixes concerns that are better handled at the tool layer itself.

text: An Agent SDK agent has 3 tools and a system prompt. When agent.run() is called, what happens internally?

options.text: One API call with all tools, returning the complete result
Tools are sent to a separate execution service and polled
Parallel API calls, one per tool, combined
An agentic loop: send messages → receive response → if tool_use, execute tools and append results → repeat until end_turn

optionExplanations.A: agent.run() does not make just one API call with all tools and return a complete result in one shot; it runs an iterative loop when tools are involved.
optionExplanations.B: Tools are not sent to a separate execution service and polled; tool execution happens through the SDK's own agentic loop, not an external polling service.
optionExplanations.C: Tools aren't called via parallel API calls one per tool that get combined; the loop is driven by a single conversation thread that may include multiple tool_use blocks per response.
optionExplanations.D: Internally, agent.run() executes the standard agentic loop: send messages, receive a response, and if the response includes tool_use, execute the requested tools and append their results to the conversation, repeating until the model reaches end_turn.

text: An app on serverless functions needs MCP tool access. Which deployment model is appropriate?

options.text: A local MCP server as a sidecar container alongside the function
Embed the MCP server logic directly in the function code
An MCP server on a dedicated instance the function connects to
The API-level MCP connector — Anthropic manages the MCP connection, no persistent local process needed

optionExplanations.A: A local MCP server as a sidecar container doesn't fit the ephemeral, scale-to-zero nature of serverless functions, which don't have a persistent environment for a sidecar to run alongside.
optionExplanations.B: Embedding the MCP server logic directly in the function code bloats the function and conflates application logic with MCP server implementation.
optionExplanations.C: An MCP server on a dedicated instance the function connects to adds infrastructure to maintain, which is unnecessary overhead for this use case.
optionExplanations.D: The API-level MCP connector is appropriate here because Anthropic manages the MCP connection directly, meaning no persistent local process needs to be maintained — ideal for ephemeral serverless functions.

text: Using plan mode for a multi-step task, Claude proposes a risky database migration the developer wants to modify before execution. Correct workflow?

options.text: Review the plan, give feedback in plan-mode iterations until satisfactory, then switch to execution mode to implement the refined plan
Edit Claude's plan text directly in chat and re-submit
Accept the plan and use hooks to block the risky migration during execution
Cancel and start a new conversation with more specific instructions

optionExplanations.A: Reviewing the plan, giving feedback in plan-mode iterations until it's satisfactory, and only then switching to execution mode to implement the refined plan is exactly what plan mode is for — iterating on the approach before any execution happens, ensuring the risky migration step is fixed beforehand.
optionExplanations.B: Hand-editing Claude's plan text directly in chat and re-submitting doesn't guarantee Claude actually follows the edited text as its new plan.
optionExplanations.C: Accepting the plan as-is and then using hooks to block the risky migration during execution works around a plan that should have been fixed in the first place, rather than properly revising it.
optionExplanations.D: Canceling and starting a new conversation with more specific instructions loses the context and progress already built up in the current plan-mode session.

text: Summaries of papers over 100K tokens drop in quality and miss later-section findings. Which technique specifically addresses this?

options.text: Generate section-by-section summaries first, then combine them into a final summary
Use max_tokens to force more concise summaries
Send only the abstract and conclusion
Use extended thinking to improve attention

optionExplanations.A: Generating section-by-section summaries first and then combining them into a final summary is a form of chaining that gives each section focused attention, directly addressing the attention degradation that causes later-section findings to be missed in very long inputs.
optionExplanations.B: Using max_tokens to force more concise summaries only limits output length; it doesn't address the problem of the model failing to attend properly to later sections of a long input.
optionExplanations.C: Sending only the abstract and conclusion loses the substantive content in the middle sections, discarding exactly the findings that are being missed.
optionExplanations.D: Using extended thinking may help somewhat, but it may not fully solve attention degradation on inputs over 100K tokens the way explicit section-by-section chunking does.

text: A Claude Code init.sh installs dependencies, sets env vars, and starts background services. What is the critical design consideration?

options.text: It must be written in Python for cross-platform compatibility
It should be idempotent — running it multiple times yields the same result, handling already-installed deps and already-running services gracefully
It must complete in under 10 seconds or be killed
It runs with root permissions automatically

optionExplanations.A: init.sh doesn't need to be written in Python for cross-platform compatibility; it's typically a shell script, and language choice isn't the critical design consideration here.
optionExplanations.B: It should be idempotent — running it multiple times yields the same result, gracefully handling already-installed dependencies and already-running services — since init.sh may run at each session start and must behave safely on repeated runs.
optionExplanations.C: There's no strict requirement that it complete in under 10 seconds or be killed; that isn't the critical design consideration.
optionExplanations.D: It does not run with root permissions automatically; it runs with the user's own permissions, so root access isn't assumed.

text: An agentic system has an 8K system prompt, up to 5 tool definitions (2K each), ~500-token user messages, and up to 4K responses, on a 200K model. Roughly how many turns before hitting the context limit?

options.text: ~40 — the system prompt and tools count only once
~20 — each turn consumes roughly 9K tokens (user message + response + overhead) atop the ~18K fixed cost
~180 — the 200K limit applies per message, not per conversation
Unlimited — old messages auto-truncate

optionExplanations.A: The system prompt and tool definitions are not sent only once; they are resent with every request in the conversation, so they don't count only once toward context usage.
optionExplanations.B: The fixed cost is roughly 18K tokens (8K system prompt + 5 tools x 2K each), leaving about 182K; each turn adds roughly 500 tokens (user message) plus up to 4K (response) plus overhead, totaling about 9K per turn, so dividing 182K by 9K gives roughly 20 turns before the context limit is reached and compaction is needed.
optionExplanations.C: The 200K limit applies to the full conversation context, not per individual message, so this option misstates how the limit works.
optionExplanations.D: Old messages do not automatically truncate; without compaction being applied, the conversation will hit the context limit rather than continuing unlimited.

text: An appointment agent must check availability across three calendars, find common free slots, and propose options. What tool architecture minimizes round trips?

options.text: A single find_available_slots tool that accepts an array of calendar IDs and a date range, returning merged availability in one call
Three separate tools requiring three sequential calls
One calendar_query tool called once per calendar, with caching
A get_all_calendars tool returning all raw calendar data for Claude to analyze

optionExplanations.A: A single find_available_slots tool that accepts an array of calendar IDs and a date range, performing the merge of availability across calendars server-side and returning the merged result in one call, minimizes round trips and matches the agent's actual workflow need.
optionExplanations.B: Three separate tools requiring three sequential calls — one per calendar — multiplies round trips compared to a single combined tool.
optionExplanations.C: A single calendar_query tool called once per calendar, even with caching, still requires multiple calls to gather all the data needed before the intersection can be computed.
optionExplanations.D: A get_all_calendars tool that returns all raw calendar data for Claude to analyze bloats context with unprocessed data and pushes the availability-intersection work onto the model instead of the tool.

text: A .claude/rules/python-testing.md uses glob pattern *.py, but rules don't apply to test files in the tests/ subdirectory. What's wrong with the pattern?

options.text: The rules file must live in tests/ to apply there
Rules files only support file-extension patterns, not directories
*.py matches only files in the root directory; use **/*.py to match recursively in all subdirectories
Rules files don't support glob patterns

optionExplanations.A: The rules file does not need to live inside the tests/ directory to apply there; rule scope is controlled by the glob pattern, not the rule file's own location.
optionExplanations.B: Rules files do support directory-aware glob patterns, not just file-extension patterns, so this isn't the actual limitation.
optionExplanations.C: The pattern *.py only matches files in the root directory; using **/*.py instead matches Python files recursively at any depth, including those inside tests/, which fixes the issue.
optionExplanations.D: Rules files do support glob patterns in general; the problem is specifically that *.py isn't recursive, not that globs aren't supported at all.

text: A system has Claude generate SQL plus a {query, explanation, estimated_cost} structured output, but estimated_cost is wildly inaccurate. What is the fundamental issue?

options.text: estimated_cost should be a number, not a string
Claude can't reliably estimate SQL query cost — this should come from the database engine's EXPLAIN
Claude needs the database schema to estimate cost accurately
Estimated cost needs real-time metrics Claude lacks

optionExplanations.A: Whether estimated_cost is typed as a number or a string is a minor formatting detail and not the fundamental reason the values are wildly inaccurate.
optionExplanations.B: Claude can't reliably estimate SQL query cost because that depends on runtime factors like table sizes, indexes, and data distribution that the model has no way to know; the estimate should instead come from the database engine's own EXPLAIN output.
optionExplanations.C: Providing the database schema gives some context but still wouldn't let Claude accurately predict cost, since cost depends on runtime statistics the schema alone doesn't capture.
optionExplanations.D: Needing real-time metrics is a symptom of the underlying issue — that query cost estimation fundamentally belongs to the database engine, not the model — rather than being the root cause itself.

text: An architect wants graceful degradation: when the primary MCP server is unavailable, fall back to cached/static implementations of common tools. Where should this failover logic live?

options.text: In the system prompt: 'If tools are unavailable, use your training knowledge'
In a separate failover MCP server that activates when the primary is down
In the orchestrator that manages tool execution — it detects server failures and swaps in cached implementations
In the MCP server itself via internal caching

optionExplanations.A: A system-prompt rule like 'if tools are unavailable, use your training knowledge' loses the structure and reliability of actual tool-based data and isn't a substitute for proper failover.
optionExplanations.B: A separate failover MCP server that activates when the primary is down adds coordination overhead and may itself be unreachable during the same outage conditions.
optionExplanations.C: The orchestrator that manages tool execution is the right place for this logic because it can detect server failures directly and transparently swap in cached or static implementations, keeping the agent functional without the agent needing to know a failure occurred.
optionExplanations.D: Internal caching inside the MCP server itself only helps with data the server already has cached, but doesn't address failover when that server itself becomes unavailable.

text: Streaming a response with both text and thinking blocks, you want to route thinking content to a separate UI panel. How do streaming events distinguish them?

options.text: Thinking arrives first, then text — they're time-separated
Thinking uses a different streaming endpoint
content_block_start events identify each block's type (thinking vs text), letting the client route accordingly
All content arrives with inline markdown tags distinguishing thinking

optionExplanations.A: Thinking is not guaranteed to be strictly time-separated before text; the two block types can interleave, so timing alone isn't a reliable way to distinguish them.
optionExplanations.B: There is no separate streaming endpoint for thinking content; both thinking and text blocks stream over the same connection.
optionExplanations.C: Each content block is introduced by a content_block_start event that identifies the block's type (such as thinking or text), letting the client route content to the appropriate UI panel based on that type.
optionExplanations.D: Content is not distinguished using inline markdown tags; block type information comes from the structured streaming events themselves, not text formatting.

text: An automated 8-step deployment has an irreversible step 5 (database migration). The team wants Claude Code to pause for human approval before step 5. What mechanism enables this?

options.text: A PreToolUse hook that detects the migration command and requires confirmation before proceeding
CLAUDE.md: 'Always ask for confirmation before database migrations'
Plan mode requiring approval for every step
A custom slash command that pauses with a confirmation prompt

optionExplanations.A: A PreToolUse hook that detects the migration command and requires confirmation before proceeding provides a deterministic human-in-the-loop gate exactly at the irreversible step, rather than relying on the model to remember to ask.
optionExplanations.B: A CLAUDE.md instruction like 'always ask for confirmation before database migrations' is probabilistic guidance and doesn't guarantee the pause will happen at the right moment.
optionExplanations.C: Requiring approval for every step in plan mode adds unnecessary friction to all seven non-critical steps, not just the one irreversible step that actually needs a human gate.
optionExplanations.D: A custom slash command that pauses with a confirmation prompt requires manual invocation, so it doesn't automatically trigger right before the irreversible step in an automated deployment.

text: An agent makes a tool call, gets a result, then immediately repeats the same call with identical parameters 3–4 times before proceeding — and it isn't in an error state. Most likely cause and fix?

options.text: Network latency makes it miss the first result — increase timeout
Temperature is too high, causing non-determinism
The agent loop fails to append tool results to the conversation
The tool result lacks clear success/failure indicators, so the agent is unsure the operation completed — add explicit status fields

optionExplanations.A: Network latency causing a missed first result would typically surface as an actual error or timeout, not a clean silent repeat with no error state.
optionExplanations.B: Temperature isn't a current knob being described here, and non-determinism from temperature wouldn't specifically cause exact repeated tool calls with identical parameters.
optionExplanations.C: If the agent loop failed to append tool results to the conversation, it would likely break tool use broadly across the board, not produce a clean pattern of repeating just one specific call 3-4 times before proceeding normally.
optionExplanations.D: The tool result likely lacks clear success or failure indicators, so the agent can't tell the operation actually completed and re-calls to confirm; adding explicit status fields (e.g., success: true) resolves this uncertainty and is the most likely cause and fix.

text: A customer-email generator should respond in the customer's language (English, Spanish, French) without explicit language instructions. Most effective approach?

options.text: Detect language with a classification call first, then include a language instruction
Include a few-shot example in each language
Use a separate translation API in and out
Place the customer's original message prominently in the prompt — Claude naturally mirrors the input language

optionExplanations.A: Detecting language with a separate classification call first and then including a language instruction adds an unnecessary extra step and latency when Claude can mirror the language naturally.
optionExplanations.B: Including a few-shot example in each language adds maintenance overhead for every supported language when it isn't actually needed.
optionExplanations.C: Using a separate translation API in and out adds unnecessary complexity and an extra integration point.
optionExplanations.D: Placing the customer's original message prominently in the prompt is most effective because Claude naturally mirrors the input language, producing a same-language reply without any extra detection or translation machinery.

text: An Agent SDK agent with max_turns: 10 stops before completing a workflow that needs 15–20 tool calls. What are the two options for handling this?

options.text: Increase max_turns to 25 and hope it's enough
Split the workflow into 10 steps or fewer
Either increase max_turns, or implement an outer loop that checks completion after max_turns and re-invokes the agent with current state
Remove the max_turns limit entirely

optionExplanations.A: Simply increasing max_turns to 25 and hoping it's enough is a fragile guess that may still fall short for a workflow whose actual length isn't precisely known in advance.
optionExplanations.B: Forcing the workflow into 10 steps or fewer may not be feasible when the task genuinely requires 15-20 distinct tool calls to complete.
optionExplanations.C: The two real options are to either increase max_turns to accommodate the workflow's length, or implement an outer loop that checks for completion after max_turns is reached and re-invokes the agent with its current state — the latter gives more control and avoids runaway execution.
optionExplanations.D: Removing the max_turns limit entirely risks unbounded execution with no safeguard against the agent looping indefinitely.

text: A RAG system injects 5 retrieved chunks (~15K tokens) and wants to maximize cache hits across users asking similar questions. What prompt structure optimizes caching?

options.text: System prompt → document chunks → user question (chunks change per query, breaking the cache)
System prompt → user question → document chunks
Everything in a single user message
System prompt (cached) → common document corpus (cached) → query-specific chunks → user question

optionExplanations.A: Placing the system prompt, then document chunks, then the user question breaks caching because the chunks change per query, invalidating the cache prefix on every new question.
optionExplanations.B: Placing the user question before the document chunks loses the ability to cache the document portion, since the cacheable content would come after the variable question.
optionExplanations.C: Putting everything in a single user message doesn't leverage prefix caching at all, since there's no stable, separately-cacheable prefix established.
optionExplanations.D: Ordering as system prompt (cached) followed by a common document corpus (cached), then query-specific chunks, then the user question last, maximizes the stable, cacheable prefix shared across users asking similar questions while still allowing query-specific content at the end.

text: Claude Code has project (.claude/settings.json), local (.claude/settings.local.json), and user (~/.claude/settings.json) settings. A developer adds an MCP server to project settings; a teammate adds the same server with different config to their local settings. Which takes precedence?

options.text: Project settings always win
Local settings override project settings for the same configuration
User settings have the highest precedence
The settings are merged and conflicts cause an error

optionExplanations.A: Project settings do not always win; local settings can override project settings for the same configuration keys.
optionExplanations.B: Local settings (.claude/settings.local.json) override project settings for the same configuration, which is exactly why the teammate's locally-configured MCP server settings take precedence over the project-level one for that teammate.
optionExplanations.C: User settings do not have the highest precedence; they serve as defaults that both project and local settings can override.
optionExplanations.D: The settings aren't merged in a way that causes an error on conflict; instead, conflicts are resolved by precedence, with local overriding project.

text: An agent generates a financial model with nested calculations; you need intermediate values in a structured, verifiable format plus reasoning visibility. What approach combines both?

options.text: Enable thinking and ask Claude to put intermediate values in the thinking blocks
A structured output schema with nested objects mirroring the calculation hierarchy ({inputs, intermediate:{step1,step2}, final}), with thinking enabled for reasoning visibility
Separate API calls for each calculation step
Tool calls for each intermediate calculation

optionExplanations.A: Putting intermediate values inside thinking blocks isn't reliably parseable as structured data, since thinking content isn't guaranteed to follow a consistent, extractable format.
optionExplanations.B: A structured output schema with nested objects mirroring the calculation hierarchy ({inputs, intermediate:{step1,step2}, final}), combined with thinking enabled, captures verifiable intermediate values in a structured form while also providing reasoning visibility through the thinking blocks — satisfying both requirements at once.
optionExplanations.C: Separate API calls for each calculation step would lose shared context between steps, making it harder to maintain consistency across the nested calculation.
optionExplanations.D: Using a tool call for each intermediate calculation adds unnecessary complexity and overhead compared to a single structured schema that already captures the calculation hierarchy.

text: An agent handles both simple lookups ('status of order #1234?') and complex investigations ('why was #1234 delayed and what's the impact?'). Both currently use the same pipeline with extended thinking, making simple lookups slow. What architecture balances speed and depth?

options.text: Disable extended thinking for all requests
Let users select 'quick' or 'detailed' mode
Add a timeout that kills thinking after 3 seconds for simple queries
Use a classifier to route simple lookups (low effort, no thinking) to a fast path and complex investigations (high effort, thinking) to a deep path

optionExplanations.A: Disabling extended thinking for all requests would speed up simple lookups but loses the depth needed for complex investigations, so it doesn't balance both needs.
optionExplanations.B: Letting users manually pick 'quick' or 'detailed' mode adds friction and shifts a classification decision the system itself should make onto the user.
optionExplanations.C: A hard timeout that kills thinking after a fixed duration produces inconsistent results since some complex queries may need more or less time than an arbitrary cutoff allows.
optionExplanations.D: A classifier that routes simple lookups to a fast, low-effort path without thinking and complex investigations to a thorough, thinking-enabled path optimizes both latency and quality, making it the correct architecture.

text: An image-processing MCP tool only supports HTTP URLs, but Claude sometimes passes a base64 data URL. How should the tool handle this gracefully?

options.text: Return 'Invalid URL format' with no further guidance
Accept data URLs by decoding the base64 inside the tool
Return a structured error stating only HTTP/HTTPS URLs are supported, that data URLs can't be processed, and asking for the image's HTTP URL (isRetryable: true)
Silently ignore the data URL and return empty results

optionExplanations.A: Returning 'Invalid URL format' with no further guidance gives Claude nothing to act on, so it can't self-correct.
optionExplanations.B: Accepting and decoding base64 data URLs inside the tool adds scope beyond the tool's intended HTTP-only design, which may be unwanted.
optionExplanations.C: A structured error stating the constraint (HTTP/HTTPS only), the reason (data URLs can't be processed), and that it's retryable gives Claude exactly what it needs to self-correct by supplying a valid URL, which is why this is correct.
optionExplanations.D: Silently ignoring the data URL and returning empty results hides the failure entirely, preventing any correction.

text: After /init generates a CLAUDE.md from repository analysis, how should the team customize it?

options.text: Delete the generated file and write one from scratch
Keep the generated file as a base and iteratively refine it — add project conventions, remove irrelevant sections, and commit the result
Generate multiple CLAUDE.md files and merge the best parts
Never modify /init output — it's optimized for the repo

optionExplanations.A: Deleting the generated file and starting from scratch discards the useful detected context that /init already produced.
optionExplanations.B: Keeping the generated file as a base and iteratively refining it — adding conventions, trimming irrelevant sections, and committing — is the recommended approach because it preserves useful auto-detected content while customizing it for the team.
