optionExplanations.C: Generating multiple CLAUDE.md files and merging the best parts is redundant compared to simply iterating on one generated file.
optionExplanations.D: Never modifying /init output misses the opportunity to customize it with project-specific conventions.

text: A content-moderation system classifies content as safe/questionable/unsafe and needs a legal audit trail of why each decision was made. Which API features support this?

options.text: Enable extended thinking to capture reasoning, and use structured output for the classification — thinking blocks form the audit trail, structured output gives the consistent decision
Use the Messages API with logging of all request/response pairs
Separate API calls for classification and explanation
Store the raw API response JSON as the audit trail

optionExplanations.A: Enabling extended thinking captures the reasoning behind each classification as an audit trail, while structured output provides a consistent, parseable decision — together forming a complete legal record, which makes this correct.
optionExplanations.B: Logging request/response pairs via the Messages API captures what was asked and answered but lacks the underlying decision reasoning needed for a legal audit trail.
optionExplanations.C: Making separate API calls for classification and explanation doubles cost without the integrated reasoning-plus-decision benefit that thinking blocks provide.
optionExplanations.D: Storing the raw API response JSON captures the decision but not the deliberation behind it.

text: An agent injects all 50 prior customer interactions (~100K tokens) into each new interaction for personalization, leaving little room for the current conversation. Better approach?

options.text: Summarize the 50 interactions into a concise (~5K token) customer profile and inject that instead
Retrieve only the 3–5 most relevant prior interactions via embeddings and inject those
Switch to a 1M-token model to fit all history
Store interactions in a tool Claude queries on demand

optionExplanations.A: Summarizing the 50 interactions into a concise ~5K-token customer profile preserves durable signals like preferences, recurring issues, and style at a fraction of the tokens, making it the best approach.
optionExplanations.B: Retrieving only the 3-5 most relevant prior interactions via embeddings is a reasonable, defensible alternative, but it may miss broader patterns that span many interactions.
optionExplanations.C: Switching to a 1M-token model to fit all history is expensive and still wasteful, since most of the raw interaction detail isn't needed.
optionExplanations.D: Storing interactions in a tool Claude queries on demand adds extra round trips compared to a precomputed profile.

text: In a testing multi-agent system, Agent B discovers a test-environment misconfiguration affecting all remaining tests. How should it communicate this blocking issue to the orchestrator?

options.text: Continue executing tests and note failures for Agent C
Stop and return a generic error message
Return a structured status with escalation metadata: {status:'blocked', reason:'environment_misconfiguration', details, blockedTests, suggestedAction}
Call Agent A to regenerate tests that work with the misconfigured environment

optionExplanations.A: Continuing to execute tests despite a known environment misconfiguration wastes resources on tests that are likely to fail for the wrong reason.
optionExplanations.B: Stopping and returning only a generic error message lacks the actionable detail the orchestrator needs to respond intelligently.
optionExplanations.C: Returning a structured status with escalation metadata (status, reason, affected tests, suggested remediation) gives the orchestrator the actionable detail needed to handle the blocking issue intelligently, which is why this is correct.
optionExplanations.D: Calling Agent A to regenerate tests that work around a misconfigured environment papers over the real infrastructure problem instead of surfacing it.

text: An MCP server in Claude Code needs sensitive credentials kept out of version control. Where should they be configured?

options.text: Directly in .claude/settings.json in the MCP server config
In environment variables referenced via ${VAR_NAME} expansion in the config
In CLAUDE.md with instructions on where to find credentials
Hardcoded in the MCP server source code

optionExplanations.A: Putting credentials directly in .claude/settings.json risks committing secrets into version control along with the config file.
optionExplanations.B: Referencing credentials via ${VAR_NAME} environment-variable expansion keeps the committed config secret-free while letting each environment supply its own value, which is the correct approach.
optionExplanations.C: Putting credentials in CLAUDE.md with instructions on where to find them exposes them in a documentation file that's likely also checked into version control.
optionExplanations.D: Hardcoding credentials in the MCP server source code leaks secrets directly into the codebase.

text: An architect wants a 'dry run' mode where all tool calls are simulated without executing, for testing behavior and estimating cost. Most effective implementation?

options.text: Add 'this is a dry run, don't execute anything' to the system prompt
Replace all tool implementations with mocks that return realistic responses
Use PreToolUse hooks that intercept all tool calls, log them, and return simulated results without executing the real tools
Set effort='low' to minimize tool-call impact

optionExplanations.A: Adding a 'don't execute anything' instruction to the system prompt is unreliable since it depends on the model consistently following a natural-language directive rather than a deterministic mechanism.
optionExplanations.B: Replacing all tool implementations with mocks that return realistic responses is more invasive, requiring every tool to be rewritten just for dry-run testing.
optionExplanations.C: Using PreToolUse hooks to intercept all tool calls, log them for analysis/cost estimation, and return simulated results without executing the real tools achieves a true dry-run mode without changing any tool code, making this the most effective implementation.
optionExplanations.D: Setting effort='low' minimizes the depth of reasoning but does not prevent tool calls from actually executing.

text: A developer wants to count tokens before sending requests to avoid exceeding context limits. What does the documentation recommend?

options.text: Use the token-counting API endpoint to get exact counts before sending
Use a third-party tokenizer library to estimate locally
Estimate ~4 characters per token for English
Send the request and read the usage field afterward

optionExplanations.A: Using the token-counting API endpoint returns exact, model-specific counts for pre-flight checks before sending a request, which is exactly what the documentation recommends.
optionExplanations.B: Using a third-party tokenizer library to estimate locally is inaccurate for Claude's specific tokenization.
optionExplanations.C: Estimating ~4 characters per token is a rough heuristic that is inaccurate for Claude's actual tokenizer.
optionExplanations.D: Reading the usage field after sending the request is post-hoc information, not a pre-flight check that could prevent exceeding context limits.

text: A legal agent must cross-reference clauses across a 200-page contract; loading the full document and asking for all cross-references gives inconsistent results (some found, some missed). What strategy improves reliability?

options.text: Use a larger model for better attention
Split the document into overlapping sections, find cross-references per section, then merge and dedupe
Index by clause number, then query specific clause pairs
Use extended thinking with a large budget so Claude methodically works through the whole document tracking cross-references

optionExplanations.A: Using a larger model for better attention can help but isn't sufficient alone to guarantee reliable cross-reference tracking.
optionExplanations.B: Splitting the document into overlapping sections and merging results is a reasonable approach, but per-section chunking risks missing cross-references that span across section boundaries.
optionExplanations.C: Indexing by clause number and querying specific clause pairs requires already knowing which pairs to check, which isn't known in advance for an open-ended cross-reference task.
optionExplanations.D: Using extended thinking with a large budget lets Claude methodically work through the entire 200-page document in one pass, tracking cross-references without losing cross-section context, which makes it the more reliable strategy.

text: An agent's rapid tool calls can exceed a third-party API's 100 requests/minute rate limit. Where should rate limiting be implemented?

options.text: In the system prompt: 'wait at least 600ms between calls'
In a PreToolUse hook that adds a delay between invocations
In the MCP server, using a token-bucket algorithm that queues requests exceeding the limit
Via a Claude API request_delay parameter

optionExplanations.A: A system-prompt instruction like 'wait at least 600ms between calls' is imprecise and depends on the model consistently following a timing instruction rather than a deterministic mechanism.
optionExplanations.B: A PreToolUse hook that adds a delay between invocations penalizes all calls indiscriminately rather than only those that would actually exceed the rate limit.
optionExplanations.C: Implementing rate limiting in the MCP server using a token-bucket algorithm that queues requests exceeding the limit is transparent to the agent and correctly handles bursts, making it the right place to enforce the third-party API's rate limit.
optionExplanations.D: There is no request_delay parameter in the Claude API, so this option doesn't exist as a real mechanism.

text: To generate high-quality unit tests for a complex function, given the implementation and existing tests for similar functions, what prompting strategy is best?

options.text: Send only the function and ask for tests
Send the function, its type definitions, and 2–3 example test files for similar functions as reference, to calibrate testing style, coverage, and assertion conventions
Ask Claude to list all edge cases first, then write one test per case
Send the function plus a testing-framework docs page

optionExplanations.A: Sending only the function and asking for tests lacks any calibration to the team's testing conventions.
optionExplanations.B: Sending the function, its type definitions, and 2-3 example test files for similar functions calibrates Claude to the team's assertion style, mocking approach, naming, and coverage patterns via few-shot examples, making this the best strategy.
optionExplanations.C: Asking Claude to list edge cases first and write one test per case may not align the resulting tests with the team's existing conventions.
optionExplanations.D: Sending the function plus a testing-framework docs page conveys general framework usage but not the team's specific testing patterns.

text: A team uses citations to display inline source links in a RAG app. What does the citations feature provide?

options.text: URLs to source documents used during training
A list of the document IDs most relevant to the response
Character-level pointers back to specific locations in the provided input documents, showing exactly which parts Claude referenced for each claim
A confidence score for how well-supported each claim is

optionExplanations.A: Citations are not URLs to documents used during training; they reference the documents actually provided in the request.
optionExplanations.B: Citations provide more than just a list of relevant document IDs; they pinpoint exact locations within those documents.
optionExplanations.C: Citations provide character-level pointers back to specific locations in the provided input documents, showing exactly which parts Claude referenced for each claim, enabling features like inline source links — this is the correct description of the feature.
optionExplanations.D: Citations identify source location, not a confidence score for how well-supported a claim is.

text: A CI pipeline using headless mode with --json-schema sometimes gets extra explanation text alongside the JSON. What ensures only the schema output is returned?

options.text: Using --json-schema in headless mode already yields only schema-compliant JSON output
Add --quiet to suppress all logging
Add --json-only to suppress non-JSON output
Pipe output through jq to extract the JSON

optionExplanations.A: Using --json-schema in headless mode is designed to emit only schema-conforming JSON output suitable for machine consumption, which already solves the problem without needing an additional flag.
optionExplanations.B: Adding --quiet suppresses logging output but isn't the mechanism responsible for ensuring schema-only output.
optionExplanations.C: There is no --json-only flag; this option does not correspond to a real mechanism.
optionExplanations.D: Piping output through jq to extract the JSON is a workaround for the symptom rather than the proper mechanism, since --json-schema should already produce only schema output.

text: A hospital chatbot must NEVER give specific medical diagnoses while remaining helpful on general health info. A system-prompt-only attempt failed when a patient described symptoms and Claude offered a tentative diagnosis. Correct implementation?

options.text: Add ABSOLUTELY-NEVER language to the system prompt
Two-layer approach: system prompt for guidance plus a separate classifier that screens every response and blocks diagnostic statements before they reach the patient
A PostToolUse classification step that checks responses and regenerates if diagnostic
Limit conversation length to prevent detailed symptom discussion

optionExplanations.A: Adding more emphatic 'ABSOLUTELY-NEVER' language to the system prompt is still probabilistic and isn't reliable enough for a safety-critical requirement, as shown by the original failure.
optionExplanations.B: A two-layer approach — system prompt guidance plus a separate classifier that screens every response and blocks diagnostic statements before they reach the patient — provides defense in depth with a deterministic safety check, making it the correct implementation for a safety-critical requirement.
optionExplanations.C: A PostToolUse classification step that regenerates if diagnostic is essentially a single-layer check and, in this context, less robust than a dedicated screening layer that blocks before output reaches the patient.
optionExplanations.D: Limiting conversation length to prevent detailed symptom discussion degrades the user experience without actually ensuring diagnostic statements are blocked.

text: An MCP tool wraps a SOAP API returning deeply-nested, namespaced XML. What transformation should the tool apply before returning results to Claude?

options.text: Return the raw XML — Claude parses XML natively
Base64-encode the XML and return it as a string
Convert to a flat key-value format
Convert to simplified JSON that strips namespaces and flattens unnecessary nesting, keeping only business-relevant fields

optionExplanations.A: Returning raw XML wastes tokens on namespace overhead and assumes Claude parses XML natively in an efficient way, which isn't the recommended approach.
optionExplanations.B: Base64-encoding the XML and returning it as a string makes the data unusable to Claude without further processing.
optionExplanations.C: Converting to a flat key-value format can lose meaningful hierarchy present in the original nested data.
optionExplanations.D: Converting to simplified JSON that strips namespaces and flattens unnecessary nesting, keeping only business-relevant fields, is the best transformation for context efficiency and usability, making it the correct choice.

text: An app has a 3K system prompt, a 10K knowledge base, a 2K conversation history, and ~200-token user messages, on Sonnet 4.5 (1024-token cache minimum). Which portions should be cached?

options.text: System prompt (3K) + knowledge base (10K) as a single cached prefix — both are stable and well above the minimum
Only the system prompt (3K)
Everything, including the conversation history
Only the knowledge base, as the largest component

optionExplanations.A: Caching the system prompt (3K) and knowledge base (10K) together as a single stable, cached prefix captures the full 13K of stable shared content, both of which are well above the 1024-token cache minimum, making this the most effective caching strategy.
optionExplanations.B: Caching only the system prompt misses the larger savings available from also caching the knowledge base.
optionExplanations.C: Caching everything, including the conversation history, wastes cache writes on content that changes per turn and isn't reliably cacheable.
optionExplanations.D: Caching only the knowledge base skips the system-prompt portion of the stable prefix, missing additional savings.

text: A quiz generator must produce exactly 4 options with one correct answer (strict mode), but occasionally two options are semantically identical (e.g., 'Use a load balancer' vs 'Implement load balancing'). What additional quality measure is needed?

options.text: Add a uniqueItems constraint in the JSON schema
An application-layer semantic-similarity check comparing option pairs and flagging near-duplicates for regeneration
Use extended thinking to verify distinctness before generating
A system-prompt instruction that options must be semantically distinct

optionExplanations.A: A uniqueItems constraint in the JSON schema only catches exact duplicate strings, not semantically identical options phrased differently.
optionExplanations.B: An application-layer semantic-similarity check (e.g., using embeddings) comparing option pairs and flagging near-duplicates for regeneration catches cases where options are phrased differently but mean the same thing, which is the additional quality measure needed.
optionExplanations.C: Using extended thinking to verify distinctness before generating can help but doesn't reliably catch semantic overlap the way a dedicated similarity check does.
optionExplanations.D: A system-prompt instruction that options must be semantically distinct helps but doesn't reliably catch semantic overlap that slips through model generation.

text: A support agent should detect frustration (repeated questions, escalating language) and switch handling. Where should frustration-detection logic live?

options.text: In the system prompt: 'detect frustration and adjust'
In a PostToolUse hook monitoring the conversation
As a separate sentiment tool the agent calls periodically
In the orchestrator, as a classifier analyzing each customer message and adjusting the system prompt/routing based on sentiment

optionExplanations.A: A system-prompt instruction telling the agent to 'detect frustration and adjust' relies on the model reliably self-detecting sentiment, which is unreliable.
optionExplanations.B: A PostToolUse hook monitoring the conversation only sees tool interactions, not the customer's actual message sentiment.
optionExplanations.C: A separate sentiment tool the agent calls periodically adds call overhead and isn't as immediate as analyzing every message.
optionExplanations.D: Implementing frustration detection in the orchestrator, as a classifier analyzing each customer message and adjusting the system prompt/routing based on sentiment, correctly treats this as a cross-cutting concern handled centrally, making it the right place for this logic.

text: In a long code-review session, after compaction Claude re-reviews files it already covered. What technique preserves review progress through compaction?

options.text: Custom compaction instructions that explicitly preserve the list of reviewed files and their status
Maintain a REVIEW_PROGRESS.md scratchpad of completed reviews and read it after compaction
Increase the compaction summary length to capture all reviewed files
Disable compaction and manage context manually

optionExplanations.A: Custom compaction instructions that explicitly require the summarizer to retain the list of reviewed files and their status let progress survive compaction without needing external files, making this the correct technique.
optionExplanations.B: Maintaining a REVIEW_PROGRESS.md scratchpad and reading it after compaction also works as a solid alternative, but it adds extra file I/O compared to instructing compaction itself to preserve the state.
optionExplanations.C: Increasing the compaction summary length to capture all reviewed files isn't a precise control over what specifically gets retained.
optionExplanations.D: Disabling compaction and managing context manually is fragile for long sessions since context will eventually need to be reduced.

text: Claude generates regex from natural-language descriptions, but the regex often fails edge cases. What approach improves quality?

options.text: Use a larger model
Ask Claude to generate multiple variants and pick the best
Use extended thinking to reason through edge cases
Provide test strings (matching and non-matching), then use a tool that tests the generated regex against all examples and returns failures for iteration

optionExplanations.A: Using a larger model may improve regex generation somewhat but doesn't verify correctness against concrete edge cases.
optionExplanations.B: Asking Claude to generate multiple variants and pick the best one, without testing those variants, doesn't guarantee the chosen regex actually handles edge cases correctly.
optionExplanations.C: Using extended thinking to reason through edge cases helps reasoning but doesn't verify the result against real test strings.
optionExplanations.D: Providing test strings (matching and non-matching), then using a tool that tests the generated regex against all examples and returns failures for iteration creates a test-driven loop that verifies correctness and drives improvement, making this the best approach.

text: An enterprise agent platform serves IT, HR, and Finance, each needing different tools and access controls. How should tool access be scoped?

options.text: Give all departments all tools and rely on prompt instructions for scope
Separate Claude API keys per department with different tool permissions
Per-agent tool configurations exposing only department-appropriate tools, with MCP server permissions enforcing access at the execution layer
A single MCP server that checks the caller's department before returning results

optionExplanations.A: Giving all departments all tools and relying on prompt instructions for scope violates least privilege, since prompt-level restrictions aren't enforced.
optionExplanations.B: Separate Claude API keys per department don't scope tool access in the way the question requires.
optionExplanations.C: Per-agent tool configurations exposing only department-appropriate tools, combined with MCP server permissions enforcing access at the execution layer, ensures least privilege with enforcement at both the configuration and execution layers, making this the correct architecture.
optionExplanations.D: A single MCP server that checks the caller's department before returning results centralizes the check but doesn't restrict which tools each department's agent can even see or attempt to call.

text: A 4-step pipeline (extract → normalize → analyze → report) has 40K-token normalized data after step 2, and you worry step 3 loses accuracy over long context. What prompting pattern optimizes the pipeline?

options.text: Run all four steps in one conversation to maintain context
Combine steps 1–2 and 3–4 into two larger steps
Run all steps in parallel in separate conversations
Use prompt chaining — each step is a separate API call passing only the necessary output to the next

optionExplanations.A: Running all four steps in one conversation accumulates all intermediate context, including the full 40K-token normalized data plus everything from earlier steps, working against the goal of preserving accuracy in step 3.
optionExplanations.B: Combining steps 1-2 and 3-4 into two larger steps doesn't isolate context the way separating each step does.
optionExplanations.C: Running all steps in parallel in separate conversations breaks the sequential data-flow dependency between extract, normalize, analyze, and report.
optionExplanations.D: Using prompt chaining, where each step is a separate API call passing only the necessary output to the next, keeps each step focused so step 3 receives only the 40K normalized data without unrelated context, leaving maximum room for accurate analysis — making this the optimal pattern.

text: An agent must comply with a 15-page brand-voice guide (placed in the system prompt). Output generally matches, but occasionally goes off-brand for edge-case topics not covered. How to improve consistency?

options.text: Reduce the brand guide to key bullet points
Add few-shot examples applying the brand voice across scenarios, including edge cases similar to the problematic ones
A PostToolUse classifier that checks every response and regenerates if off-brand
Fine-tune a custom model on brand-compliant responses

optionExplanations.A: Reducing the brand guide to key bullet points risks losing the nuance needed to handle edge-case topics.
optionExplanations.B: Adding few-shot examples applying the brand voice across scenarios, including edge cases similar to the problematic ones, shows the model HOW to apply the voice in varied situations, helping it generalize beyond what the written guide covers — making this the best fix.
optionExplanations.C: A PostToolUse classifier that checks every response and regenerates if off-brand adds latency and cost compared to fixing the underlying prompting approach.
optionExplanations.D: Fine-tuning a custom model on brand-compliant responses is overkill for what is fundamentally a prompt-engineering problem.

text: A Claude Code session becomes sluggish with degraded quality after extended use, suggesting context saturation. Recommended maintenance approach?

options.text: Restart Claude Code for a fresh window
Temporarily delete all CLAUDE.md files to reduce context
Use /compact to trigger compaction, then verify Claude's understanding by asking it to summarize the current task
Switch to a larger-context model

optionExplanations.A: Restarting Claude Code for a fresh window loses all existing context rather than preserving useful state.
optionExplanations.B: Temporarily deleting all CLAUDE.md files removes important configuration along with the context, which isn't a targeted fix for saturation.
optionExplanations.C: Using /compact to trigger compaction, then verifying Claude's understanding by asking it to summarize the current task, reduces context while confirming what survived and surfacing anything that needs to be re-provided, making this the recommended maintenance approach.
optionExplanations.D: Switching to a larger-context model may not be available and doesn't address the underlying issue of context accumulation degrading quality.

text: A fraud-detection agent flags 30% of transactions, 90% of which are false positives. Blocking legit transactions frustrates customers; missing fraud is costly. What analysis framework should be applied?

options.text: Lower the sensitivity threshold to reduce false positives
A tiered confidence system: high-confidence → block; medium → add friction (2FA); low → allow with monitoring, each with its own precision/recall tradeoff
Remove automated blocking and rely on post-transaction review
Train a separate classifier to pre-filter results

optionExplanations.A: Lowering the sensitivity threshold to reduce false positives bluntly trades one type of error (false positives) for another (missed fraud) without a more nuanced framework.
optionExplanations.B: A tiered confidence system — high-confidence transactions blocked, medium-confidence given added friction like 2FA, and low-confidence allowed with monitoring — lets each tier balance precision and recall appropriately for its risk level, making this the right analysis framework.
optionExplanations.C: Removing automated blocking and relying on post-transaction review addresses the symptom of false positives but doesn't provide a decision framework for balancing the tradeoffs in real time.
optionExplanations.D: Training a separate classifier to pre-filter results addresses a different problem and doesn't itself provide the tiered decision framework needed.

text: A search MCP tool returns URLs, but Claude sometimes fabricates similar-looking URLs that don't exist. What server-side/attribution measure helps prevent this?

options.text: A system-prompt instruction to only use URLs from tool results
Use citations to track which URLs come from tool results vs. model generation, so the app can verify attribution
Client-side validation checking all URLs in the response
Return URLs as structured data and have Claude reference results by ID rather than regenerating URLs

optionExplanations.A: A system-prompt instruction to only use URLs from tool results is unreliable since it depends on the model consistently following the directive rather than a verifiable mechanism.
optionExplanations.B: Using citations to track which URLs come from tool results versus model generation lets the app verify attribution and catch fabricated URLs at the source, making this the correct server-side/attribution measure.
optionExplanations.C: Client-side validation checking all URLs in the response can confirm a URL exists but doesn't verify its provenance — whether it actually came from a tool result.
optionExplanations.D: Returning URLs as structured data and having Claude reference results by ID rather than regenerating URLs helps prevent fabrication but doesn't itself verify attribution the way citations do; it's complementary to citations rather than a substitute.

text: A team adds an MCP tool that can execute shell commands; the agent gets a permission error using it. Where are MCP tool permissions managed in Claude Code?

options.text: In settings (e.g. .claude/settings.json) via allowedTools/blockedTools arrays, with glob patterns for MCP tool names
In CLAUDE.md under an allowed_tools section
In the MCP server configuration file
Per-conversation, reset each time

optionExplanations.A: Claude Code manages MCP tool permissions in settings files (e.g., .claude/settings.json) via allowedTools/blockedTools arrays, supporting glob patterns for MCP tool names, which is exactly where this permission error needs to be resolved.
optionExplanations.B: CLAUDE.md is meant for instructions and context, not for managing tool permissions.
optionExplanations.C: The MCP server's own configuration file doesn't control Claude Code's permission settings for that tool.
optionExplanations.D: MCP tool permissions persist in settings rather than resetting per conversation, so this option misrepresents how permissions are managed.

text: A RAG app uses citations (character-level indices) and wants to render inline citations. How should the client render them?

options.text: Insert reference markers using the indices, linking each to the specific source passage
Append all citations as footnotes at the end
All of these are valid rendering approaches depending on UX requirements
A sidebar with source highlights shown on hover over cited text

optionExplanations.A: Inserting reference markers using the indices and linking each to the source passage is one valid way to render citations, but it isn't the only valid approach.
optionExplanations.B: Appending all citations as footnotes at the end is also a valid rendering approach, just one of several options.
optionExplanations.C: Citations provide the raw character-level data, and the client is free to choose the presentation — inline markers, footnotes, hover sidebars, or highlights — based on UX needs, so all of the listed approaches are valid, making this the correct answer.
optionExplanations.D: A sidebar with source highlights shown on hover is also a valid rendering approach among several reasonable options.

text: An agent processes user-uploaded CSVs ranging from 100 rows to 1 million rows. Small files work in context; large files can't fit. What scalable architecture handles both?

options.text: Always process the full CSV, relying on compaction if needed
Always chunk into 100-row segments processed sequentially
For small files (<~5K rows) include directly in context; for large files load into a database and give the agent SQL query tools to explore on demand
Convert all CSVs to summary statistics before processing

optionExplanations.A: Always processing the full CSV and relying on compaction if needed can't actually fit a 1-million-row file into context regardless of compaction.
optionExplanations.B: Always chunking into 100-row segments processed sequentially is inefficient overhead for small files that would otherwise fit directly in context.
optionExplanations.C: Routing small files (under ~5K rows) directly into context for full visibility, while loading large files into a database and giving the agent SQL query tools to explore on demand, scales to handle both ends of the size range, making this the correct architecture.
optionExplanations.D: Converting all CSVs to summary statistics before processing loses row-level access that may be needed for detailed queries.

text: A team must test whether an agent handles tool failures gracefully across 50 error scenarios; each manual run takes 10 minutes. Most efficient approach?

options.text: One comprehensive prompt covering all 50 scenarios in one conversation
Test 10 representative scenarios and extrapolate
Hire a QA team to test each manually
Use the Message Batches API: each scenario is a separate request with a mocked tool failure, analyzed programmatically

optionExplanations.A: One comprehensive prompt covering all 50 scenarios in a single conversation can't isolate each scenario's mocked failure cleanly.
optionExplanations.B: Testing only 10 representative scenarios and extrapolating risks missing edge cases among the other 40 untested scenarios.
optionExplanations.C: Hiring a QA team to test each scenario manually is slow and expensive compared to an automated approach.
optionExplanations.D: Using the Message Batches API, where each of the 50 scenarios is a separate request with a mocked tool failure and results are analyzed programmatically, runs everything in parallel at roughly half cost and comprehensively covers all scenarios, making this the most efficient approach.

text: An MCP tool generates images and returns base64 data; Claude's descriptions don't match because it can't see the tool output as an image. How should this be architected?

options.text: Have Claude describe the image from the original prompt without seeing it
Have the tool return a text description alongside the image
Store the image and return a URL Claude mentions to the user
Include the base64 image in a subsequent message using the image content type so Claude can actually see and describe it

optionExplanations.A: Having Claude describe the image purely from the original prompt without seeing it is unreliable, since the description may not match what the tool actually generated.
optionExplanations.B: Having the tool return a text description alongside the image is less accurate than Claude directly seeing the image via vision.
optionExplanations.C: Storing the image and returning a URL Claude mentions to the user still doesn't feed the actual pixel data to the model for analysis.
optionExplanations.D: Including the base64 image in a subsequent message using the image content type lets Claude's vision capability actually see and accurately describe the generated image, making this the correct architecture.

text: A project's CLAUDE.md has grown to 800 lines and rules in the latter half seem ignored. Recommended approach for managing large CLAUDE.md files?

options.text: Delete rules that seem ignored
Increase the context window to process the full file
Convert CLAUDE.md to a compact YAML format
Split into a concise root CLAUDE.md with critical rules, use @import for detailed sections, and use .claude/rules/ for context-specific rules with glob patterns

optionExplanations.A: Deleting rules that seem ignored loses information that may still be relevant, rather than fixing the organizational problem causing them to be ignored.
optionExplanations.B: Increasing the context window to process the full file doesn't fix the underlying attention/organization problem with a large unstructured file.
optionExplanations.C: Converting CLAUDE.md to a compact YAML format doesn't address the organizational issue of having too much content competing for attention in one file.
optionExplanations.D: Splitting into a concise root CLAUDE.md with critical rules, using @import for detailed sections, and using .claude/rules/ for context-specific rules with glob patterns decomposes the file so only relevant rules load when needed, making this the recommended approach for managing large CLAUDE.md files.

text: An Agent SDK agent should use the 'search' tool before answering, but often answers from training knowledge despite a system-prompt instruction. Most effective fix?

options.text: Force tool_choice to 'search' on the first turn, then switch to 'auto' for subsequent turns
Rewrite the system prompt to be more emphatic ('ALWAYS use search')
Set tool_choice 'any' to force tool use every turn
Add a PreToolUse hook that blocks non-search responses

optionExplanations.A: Forcing tool_choice to 'search' on the first turn guarantees a search happens before any answer, then switching to 'auto' for subsequent turns allows natural follow-up behavior, making this the most effective and targeted fix.
optionExplanations.B: Rewriting the system prompt to be more emphatic is unreliable since it still depends on the model consistently following a natural-language instruction.
optionExplanations.C: Setting tool_choice to 'any' forces a tool call on every turn, including unwanted follow-up turns, which is broader than needed.
optionExplanations.D: A PreToolUse hook that blocks non-search responses operates at the wrong level, since PreToolUse hooks intercept tool calls rather than controlling whether the model chooses to call a tool at all.

text: A form has 20 fields but typically only 5–8 are relevant per request; remaining should be null. With all 20 required, Claude hallucinates values for irrelevant fields. What schema design fixes this?

options.text: Make all 20 fields nullable with clear descriptions of when each applies
Generate a per-request dynamic schema containing only the fields relevant to that request's context
Keep all fields required but add a confidence field per field
Create 4 schemas for different form types, each with only relevant required fields

optionExplanations.A: Making all 20 fields nullable with clear descriptions of when each applies is also a strong, simpler pattern — letting Claude return null instead of guessing — and is a defensible alternative to a dynamic schema.
optionExplanations.B: Generating a per-request dynamic schema containing only the fields relevant to that request's context eliminates any opportunity to hallucinate values for irrelevant fields, since those fields simply aren't present in the schema, making this the best fix.
optionExplanations.C: Keeping all fields required but adding a confidence field per field doesn't prevent fabrication of values for irrelevant fields; it only annotates the fabricated values.
optionExplanations.D: Creating 4 fixed schemas for different form types is less flexible than dynamically generating a schema tailored to each specific request.

text: Migrating an agent from a 200K-context model (Sonnet 4.5) to a 1M-context model (Sonnet 4.6), besides handling longer documents, what else should you account for?

options.text: Pricing per token is identical, so cost only scales with usage
Prompt-caching minimum thresholds can differ between model versions (e.g., Sonnet 4.5 ~1024 vs Sonnet 4.6 ~2048 tokens), affecting your caching strategy
No architecture changes are needed — just update the model parameter
Response quality is identical regardless of context length

optionExplanations.A: Pricing per token is not necessarily identical across model versions, so cost doesn't only scale with usage when migrating models.
optionExplanations.B: Prompt-caching minimum thresholds can differ between model versions (e.g., roughly 1024 tokens for Sonnet 4.5 versus roughly 2048 for Sonnet 4.6), which affects caching strategy and breakpoints, making this the key consideration to account for.
optionExplanations.C: Architecture changes may be needed beyond just updating the model parameter, since caching thresholds and long-context attention behavior can shift between versions.
optionExplanations.D: Response quality is not guaranteed to be identical regardless of context length when migrating between model versions.

text: A Salesforce MCP server has 8 tools and Claude occasionally picks the wrong one (e.g., search_contacts vs get_contact_by_id). What improves selection accuracy?

options.text: Detailed descriptions differentiating each tool with usage guidance, e.g. 'search_contacts: use to FIND contacts by name/email/company; NOT for retrieving a known contact (use get_contact_by_id)'
Reduce to 4 tools by combining operations
Add a tool_router tool Claude calls first to decide
Rename tools with numbered prefixes (tool1_search, tool2_get)

optionExplanations.A: Detailed descriptions differentiating each tool with usage guidance — including positive ('use to FIND...') and negative ('NOT for...use get_contact_by_id instead') guidance — is the primary signal Claude uses for tool selection, making this the most effective way to disambiguate similar tools.
optionExplanations.B: Reducing to 4 tools by combining operations loses the granularity needed to support the distinct operations the 8 tools provide.
optionExplanations.C: Adding a tool_router tool Claude calls first adds an extra layer of indirection rather than directly fixing the underlying selection ambiguity.
optionExplanations.D: Renaming tools with numbered prefixes like tool1_search doesn't convey any semantic meaning that would help Claude choose correctly.

text: What is the default keyboard shortcut to toggle plan mode in Claude Code?

options.text: Shift+Tab
Tab to cycle modes
Ctrl+P
Escape

optionExplanations.A: Shift+Tab is the default keyboard shortcut that toggles plan mode in Claude Code, where Claude outlines its approach before executing.
optionExplanations.B: Tab to cycle modes is not the shortcut used to toggle plan mode specifically.
optionExplanations.C: Ctrl+P is not the shortcut that toggles plan mode.
optionExplanations.D: Escape does not toggle plan mode.

text: An eval framework should measure whether the agent asks for clarification on ambiguous requests rather than assuming. What metric captures this?

options.text: Response accuracy — whether final answers are correct
Clarification rate — the percentage of ambiguous inputs where the agent asks for clarification, measured against a labeled set of inputs that require it
Response time — faster responses imply assumptions
Token efficiency — fewer tokens imply decisiveness

optionExplanations.A: Response accuracy measures whether final answers are correct, not whether the agent appropriately seeks clarification on ambiguous input.
optionExplanations.B: A clarification-rate metric — the percentage of ambiguous inputs where the agent asks for clarification, measured against a labeled set of inputs that require it — directly captures whether the agent asks for clarification rather than assuming, making this the correct metric.
optionExplanations.C: Response time doesn't reliably indicate whether assumptions were made instead of asking for clarification; a fast response could still be appropriately a clarifying question.
optionExplanations.D: Token efficiency measures verbosity, not whether the agent sought clarification versus assumed.

text: Claude must generate JSON config files that include comments explaining each option, but JSON doesn't support comments. Best structured-output approach?

options.text: Use a json_object mode that allows comments
Use YAML mode for comment support
Generate JSON and comments in separate API calls
Use a schema with parallel fields: each option has a value and a comment, e.g. {port: {value: 8080, comment: 'Server listening port'}}

optionExplanations.A: There is no json_object mode that allows comments; standard JSON does not support comments regardless of mode.
optionExplanations.B: Using YAML mode for comment support isn't a structured-output mode Claude offers; the requirement is for valid JSON output.
optionExplanations.C: Generating JSON and comments in separate API calls loses the direct association between each value and its corresponding comment.
optionExplanations.D: Using a schema with parallel fields, where each option has both a value and a comment (e.g., port: {value: 8080, comment: 'Server listening port'}), captures both the configuration and its documentation in valid JSON, making this the best structured-output approach.

text: Multiple Claude agents share information via a central message bus (metadata, status, data payloads). What is the key consideration for the message format?

options.text: A consistent structured format each consuming agent can parse without extra tool calls, with clear metadata for routing and versioning
Plain text for maximum compatibility
Different formats per agent pair for efficiency
Let each agent define its own preferred input format

optionExplanations.A: A consistent structured format that each consuming agent can parse without extra tool calls, with clear metadata for routing and versioning, lets agents reliably consume messages without translation overhead, making this the key consideration.
optionExplanations.B: Plain text lacks the structure needed for agents to reliably parse metadata, status, and data payloads.
optionExplanations.C: Different formats per agent pair create incompatibilities and unnecessary complexity across the message bus.
optionExplanations.D: Letting each agent define its own preferred input format creates the same incompatibility problem as per-pair formats, undermining shared communication.

text: A project uses both JavaScript (Prettier) and Rust (rustfmt). How should CLAUDE.md handle the dual-language formatting setup?

options.text: Both conventions in the root CLAUDE.md with section headers
Separate CLAUDE.md files in js/ and rust/ directories
Glob-scoped rule files: one matching **/*.js,**/*.ts for Prettier rules, another matching **/*.rs for rustfmt rules
Rely on the formatters and don't include formatting rules in CLAUDE.md

optionExplanations.A: Putting both conventions in the root CLAUDE.md with section headers mixes unrelated language-specific rules together rather than scoping them to when they're actually relevant.
optionExplanations.B: Separate CLAUDE.md files in js/ and rust/ directories require being physically located in those directories to take effect, which is less flexible than glob-based scoping.
optionExplanations.C: Using glob-scoped rule files — one matching **/*.js,**/*.ts for Prettier rules and another matching **/*.rs for rustfmt rules — auto-loads the right formatting rules based on the file being edited, working correctly even outside dedicated language directories, making this the best approach.
optionExplanations.D: Relying solely on the formatters and omitting formatting rules from CLAUDE.md misses the chance to guide Claude's code generation toward the right conventions up front.

text: A developer finds 60% of API cost is input tokens (mostly repeated context), 15% output, 25% thinking. Which optimization gives the biggest cost reduction?

options.text: Reduce output length to save on the 15% output cost
Reduce thinking budget to save on the 25% thinking cost
Switch to a smaller model
Implement prompt caching to cut the 60% input cost — cache reads cost ~10% of base input price

optionExplanations.A: Reducing output length only targets the 15% output cost, a smaller component than the input cost.
optionExplanations.B: Reducing the thinking budget only targets the 25% thinking cost, a smaller component than the dominant input cost.
optionExplanations.C: Switching to a smaller model requires a quality tradeoff rather than directly addressing the repeated-context cost driver.
optionExplanations.D: Implementing prompt caching targets the largest cost component (60% input, mostly repeated context) and cuts the cached portion by roughly 90%, making it the highest-impact optimization available.

text: A Kubernetes MCP tool exposes high-impact operations (scale_deployment, delete_pod, drain_node) that should require confirmation. How should the tool design handle dangerous operations?

options.text: Prefix descriptions with 'DANGEROUS:' and rely on Claude to warn the user
Put dangerous tools on a separate MCP server requiring admin credentials
Require the user to type 'CONFIRM' before each dangerous operation
Two-phase execution: the first call returns a preview (dry run), and execution requires a second call with a confirmation token

optionExplanations.A: Prefixing descriptions with 'DANGEROUS:' and relying on Claude to warn the user is a prompt-level signal that doesn't reliably prevent execution of a high-impact operation.
optionExplanations.B: Putting dangerous tools on a separate MCP server requiring admin credentials adds infrastructure but doesn't itself add a preview/confirm gate before execution.
optionExplanations.C: Requiring the user to type 'CONFIRM' before each dangerous operation is a prompt-level confirmation step rather than a deterministic safety mechanism built into the tool design.
optionExplanations.D: Two-phase execution, where the first call returns a preview (dry run) and execution requires a second call with a confirmation token, is a deterministic safety layer that shows impact before anything happens and doesn't rely on the model for safety, making this the correct design for dangerous operations.

text: Streaming a response, you want a 'typing' indicator while thinking blocks generate, switching to text display when the response text starts. Which streaming events signal this transition?

options.text: A 'phase_change' event indicates the switch
content_block_stop for the thinking block, followed by content_block_start for the text block, signals the transition
message_delta includes a 'current_phase' field
Thinking and text generation use separate streaming connections

optionExplanations.A: There is no 'phase_change' event in the streaming API; this option does not correspond to a real streaming event.
optionExplanations.B: The content_block_stop event for the thinking block, followed by content_block_start for the text block, marks the actual transition between blocks, so the client should switch UI state on these real events, making this the correct answer.
optionExplanations.C: message_delta does not include a 'current_phase' field; this option doesn't correspond to an actual field in the streaming protocol.
optionExplanations.D: Thinking and text generation do not use separate streaming connections; they occur within the same single stream, delineated by content block boundaries.

text: An agent serves a multi-language base (English, Spanish, Japanese). The system prompt and tool results are in English; when a customer writes Japanese, the agent should respond in Japanese. How should translation be handled?

options.text: Keep the system prompt and tools in English — Claude understands the English config/results and naturally responds in the customer's detected language
Translate the system prompt into each language and use the matching one per request
Use a translation MCP tool to convert everything to the customer's language first
Create separate agent configurations per language

optionExplanations.A: Keeping the system prompt and tools in English works because Claude understands the English configuration and tool results and naturally responds in the customer's detected language (Japanese), so no translation infrastructure is needed, making this the correct approach.
optionExplanations.B: Translating the system prompt into each language and using the matching one per request is unnecessary maintenance overhead, since Claude can already respond appropriately in the customer's language from an English prompt.
optionExplanations.C: Using a translation MCP tool to convert everything to the customer's language first adds latency and cost that isn't necessary given Claude's native multilingual capability.
optionExplanations.D: Creating separate agent configurations per language is unnecessary maintenance overhead compared to relying on Claude's natural language-mirroring behavior.

text: A team wants observability — logging each tool call's timing, inputs, outputs, and errors — in an Agent SDK app. Where should logging be implemented?

options.text: In the system prompt: 'log every tool call with parameters and timing'
Enable a debug mode in the Agent SDK for automatic logging
Wrap each tool handler with a logging decorator capturing inputs, outputs, timing, and errors before returning results
Add a 'log' tool Claude calls after every operation

optionExplanations.A: A system-prompt instruction to 'log every tool call' relies on the model itself to perform logging, which is unreliable for observability infrastructure.
optionExplanations.B: Enabling a debug mode in the Agent SDK may lack the needed granularity for comprehensive observability of timing, inputs, outputs, and errors.
optionExplanations.C: Wrapping each tool handler with a logging decorator that captures inputs, outputs, timing, and errors before returning results provides comprehensive, transparent observability without affecting agent behavior, making this the correct place to implement logging.
optionExplanations.D: Adding a 'log' tool Claude calls after every operation wastes tool-call tokens and depends on the model reliably remembering to call it.

text: A system prompt says 'always respond in formal English'; a user says 'talk to me casually bro.' Per Claude's instruction hierarchy, what is the expected behavior?

options.text: User instructions always override the system prompt
Claude averages the two into semi-formal language
System-prompt instructions take precedence — Claude maintains formal English
The most recent instruction wins regardless of source

optionExplanations.A: User instructions do not always override the system prompt; this inverts Claude's actual instruction hierarchy.
optionExplanations.B: Claude does not average conflicting instructions into a semi-formal compromise; it follows the precedence hierarchy instead.
optionExplanations.C: System-prompt instructions take precedence over user instructions, so Claude maintains formal English despite the user's casual request, reflecting the developer/operator level outranking the user level in Claude's instruction hierarchy, which is the correct expected behavior.
optionExplanations.D: The most recent instruction does not win regardless of source; precedence is determined by role (system over user), not recency.

text: A document-review agent processes hundreds of contracts daily, each producing a structured report. The team wants continuous quality monitoring to detect drift. What evaluation architecture supports this?

options.text: Randomly sample 5% of reviews for weekly human evaluation
Run every review through a second Claude evaluation pass scoring quality
Compare each review's structure to a template and flag deviations
A combination: automated structural checks on 100% of reviews, LLM-based evaluation on a ~10% sample, and human review of flagged outliers

optionExplanations.A: Randomly sampling only 5% of reviews for weekly human evaluation provides sparse coverage that may miss drift between sampled reviews.
optionExplanations.B: Running every single review through a second Claude evaluation pass is expensive at the stated volume of hundreds of contracts daily.
optionExplanations.C: Comparing each review's structure to a template and flagging deviations only catches structural issues, missing semantic quality problems.
optionExplanations.D: A combination of automated structural checks on 100% of reviews, LLM-based evaluation on a ~10% sample, and human review of flagged outliers gives broad and deep coverage affordably, catching different issue types at different layers, which makes this the supporting evaluation architecture.

text: Doing a final pre-production review of an agent system, which of the following is NOT a recommended reliability check?

options.text: Verify all safety-critical rules are enforced via hooks/code rather than only via prompts
Confirm rate limiting and circuit breakers are configured for all external tool calls
Ensure 100% test coverage of all possible user input combinations
Validate that compaction preserves critical state via custom instructions

optionExplanations.A: Verifying that all safety-critical rules are enforced via hooks/code rather than only via prompts is a sound, recommended pre-deployment reliability check.
optionExplanations.B: Confirming rate limiting and circuit breakers are configured for all external tool calls is a sound, recommended pre-deployment reliability check.
optionExplanations.C: Ensuring 100% test coverage of all possible user input combinations is impossible for open-ended natural-language input, making this the check that is NOT a recommended practice and therefore the correct answer to this 'NOT recommended' question.
optionExplanations.D: Validating that compaction preserves critical state via custom instructions is a sound, recommended pre-deployment reliability check.
